| **Complete Case & Terminate Employee** (cobalt CTA, `(2FA)`) | ŌĆö | `offboarding.manage` | Only visible once the settlement is approved. Behaviour as above. |

> *Notes:* The "Earnings" card is rendered straight from the `FinalSettlementResource::earnings` array ŌĆö gratuity, severance, leave encashment, prorated 13th, ex-gratia, and a heavy "Gross Settlement" row. The "Deductions" card renders `deductions.outstanding_loans` (computed live from open `LoanAccount` rows at calculation time), garnishments, other, PAYE on settlement, and a highlighted "Net Payable" in the brand cobalt. The full computation breakdown (inputs, multipliers, narrative string) is stored in the `breakdown` JSON column on `final_settlements` so an auditor pulling the row in five years' time can reconstruct exactly how the figure was arrived at ŌĆö including which Act 651 multipliers were in force.

### Audit tab

| Item | What it shows | Who can see it |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical timeline** | Four nodes ŌĆö Case Initiated (`initiated_by`, notice date, exit type), Settlement Calculated (net payable, calculated_at), Settlement Approved (approved_at), Case Completed (terminated date). Nodes that haven't happened yet are omitted; the vertical line stops at the last completed step. | Anyone with view |
| **Reference card** | Case reference (`OFF-YYYY-NNNNN`), initiator name, exit type. | Same |
| **Effective dates card** | Rehire-eligible flag (yes/no), last working day, effective termination date. | Same |
| **Reason / Context** | Free text typed by the initiator. | Same |
| **Exit Interview Summary** | Free text ŌĆö populated through the HR Records clearance area's exit-interview item (today it lives in the case's `exit_interview_summary` field; no dedicated UI panel for typing it in the MVP). | Same |

## The data behind it

CIHRMS stores three tables and four enum types to run off-boarding:

- **`offboarding_cases`** ŌĆö one row per departing employee. Fields: `reference` (unique, `OFF-YYYY-NNNNN`, minted via `SequenceService::next("offboarding:{year}")`), `employee_id`, `initiated_by`, `exit_type` (cast to `ExitType` enum), `status` (cast to `OffboardingStatus` enum), `notice_received_on`, `last_working_day`, `effective_termination_date`, `rehire_eligible` (default `true`), `reason` (text), `exit_interview_summary` (text), `completed_at`, `completed_by`. Soft-deleted; uniqueness on reference is enforced at the DB.
- **`clearance_items`** ŌĆö many rows per case (the seeded default template creates 12). Fields: `offboarding_case_id`, `area` (cast to `ClearanceArea` enum ŌĆö nine values), `label` (free text ŌĆö the human-readable description), `status` (cast to `ClearanceItemStatus` ŌĆö `pending`, `cleared`, or `waived`), `responsible_department_id`, `responsible_user_id`, `is_required` (default `true`), `cleared_by`, `cleared_at`, `notes`, `evidence_paths` (JSON array of attachment paths). Indexed on `(offboarding_case_id, status)` and `(area, status)`.
- **`final_settlements`** ŌĆö one row per case (soft-deleted; the service replaces any unapproved prior snapshot when recalculating). Fields: `offboarding_case_id`, `status` (cast to `SettlementStatus` ŌĆö `calculated`, `approved`, `paid`, `cancelled`), snapshot inputs (`basic_salary`, `years_of_service`, `accrued_leave_days`, `working_days_per_month`), earnings (`gratuity`, `severance`, `leave_encashment`, `prorated_13th_month`, `ex_gratia`, `gross_settlement`), deductions (`outstanding_loans`, `garnishments`, `other_deductions`, `total_deductions`), `paye_on_settlement`, `net_payable`, workflow columns (`calculated_by`, `calculated_at`, `approved_by`, `approved_at`, `payroll_line_id`, `paid_at`), the `breakdown` JSON blob, and freeform `notes`.
- **Enums** ŌĆö `ExitType` (Resignation, Retirement, EndOfContract, Dismissal, Redundancy, MutualSeparation, Death, Abscondment) carries the methods `qualifiesForGratuity()` (Retirement / EndOfContract / Redundancy / MutualSeparation / Death) and `qualifiesForSeverance()` (Redundancy only ŌĆö Act 651 ┬¦31). `OffboardingStatus` distinguishes terminal states (`Completed`, `Cancelled`) via `isTerminal()`. `ClearanceArea` enumerates the nine sign-off lanes. `ClearanceItemStatus` is the simple `pending` / `cleared` / `waived` triple.

The case joins out to **one** Employee (cascade-delete from the employee), **one** initiating User, **one** completing User, **many** ClearanceItems, and **one** FinalSettlement (`HasOne::latestOfMany()` ŌĆö there can only be one active snapshot per case at a time). The settlement joins back to a `payroll_line_id` for the off-cycle disbursement once Finance pays it.

What every reader of the screen needs to keep in mind:

- **The case reference is not the same as a payroll voucher.** `OFF-2026-00012` is the audit identifier for the separation; the disbursement still needs a `PayrollLine` (Ch 19) with its own GIFMIS voucher (Ch 23) once Finance pays the net.
- **The settlement snapshot is immutable once approved.** Re-running `calculateSettlement` on an approved row throws `DomainException`; the only escape is to cancel the case and start over. This is intentional ŌĆö the figure that was approved is the figure that gets paid.
- **Dual control is enforced in code, not just policy.** `OffboardingService::approveSettlement()` raises a `DomainException` if `calculated_by === approved_by`. The Pest test `enforces dual control on settlement approval` covers this regression.
- **Years of service is computed at calculation time** as a `floatDiffInYears` between `hire_date` and the case's `effective_termination_date` (defaults to `last_working_day`). It is rounded to two decimal places and snapshotted ŌĆö so a partial year (4.50 = 4 years 6 months) becomes 4.50 months of basic times the gratuity multiplier.
- **Accrued leave days come from the current-year `leave_balances` annual row only.** Carry-over from prior years is not in scope of the MVP; if the institute lets unused leave carry forward, HR types the corrected day-count straight into the snapshot via an "other_deductions" line until Phase 2 generalises this.
- **The `effective_termination_date` defaults to `last_working_day` but is stored separately** so an institute that needs a paid notice period beyond the last day worked has a place to record the real termination date without overwriting the LWD.

## Lifecycle

The state machine is enforced by the service, not by the controller, so any future API entry-point follows the same rules:

```
draft ŌöĆŌöĆ[ initiate ]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢Č in_progress ŌöĆŌöĆ[ last required item cleared ]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢Č awaiting_settlement
                                                                              Ōöé
                                                                       [ calculateSettlement ]
                                                                              Ō¢╝
                                                              awaiting_settlement (FinalSettlement: calculated)
                                                                              Ōöé
                                                                       [ approveSettlement ŌĆö dual control + 2FA ]
                                                                              Ō¢╝
                                                              awaiting_settlement (FinalSettlement: approved)
                                                                              Ōöé
                                                                       [ complete ŌĆö 2FA ]
                                                                              Ō¢╝
                                                                          completed
                                                                          (Employee.status ŌåÉ terminated)
```

A case can be **cancelled** from any non-terminal state ŌĆö a withdrawn resignation, a re-instated employee ŌĆö and the cancellation reason is appended to the case `reason` with a `[CANCELLED]` prefix. Cancelling preserves the case row for the audit trail.

The default clearance template seeded at initiation (`OffboardingService::DEFAULT_CLEARANCE_TEMPLATE`) is:

| Area | Item | Required? |
|---|---|---|
| IT & Assets | Return laptop, mobile device & SIM | yes |
| IT & Assets | Disable accounts and revoke access | yes |
| IT & Assets | Return ID & access badge | yes |
| Finance | Reconcile outstanding imprest / advances | yes |
| Finance | Settle outstanding loans (or schedule netting) | yes |
| HR Records | Return staff file documents | yes |
| HR Records | Sign exit interview | yes |
| HR Records | Reaffirm NDA / confidentiality obligations | yes |
| Stores | Return uniforms / tools / vehicles | optional |
| Security & Access | Gate pass & parking access revoked | yes |
| Departmental Handover | Departmental handover note signed by supervisor | yes |
| Pension Discharge | SSNIT discharge form filed; Tier-2 trustee notified | yes |

The pension item explicitly cues the SSNIT (Tier-1) discharge paperwork and the Tier-2 trustee notification, so the handoff to the National Pensions Regulatory Authority's two-tier scheme is captured in the same checklist that IT uses to disable accounts. There is no automatic API call to SSNIT or to a Tier-2 trustee in the MVP ŌĆö the item is a clerical sign-off that the form has been filed.

## How it talks to other modules

- **`OffboardingInitiated` event** ŌåÆ fired at the end of every successful `initiate`. Nothing listens to it today; the event exists so Phase 1 can hang a "freeze access" listener off it without touching the service. (Roadmap, below.)
- **`OffboardingCompleted` event** ŌåÆ fired when a case completes. `FanOutWebhooks::handleOffboardingCompleted` picks it up and dispatches a `offboarding.completed` webhook payload to every active subscriber (Ch 24). The auto-discovery scanner finds the listener via its typed `handle()` parameter, so no explicit `Event::listen` registration is needed.
- **`SettlementApproved` event** ŌåÆ fired when the dual-control approval lands. The service uses this to mark the loan write-off (the loans are paid out of the settlement and any remaining scheduled instalments are voided). Webhook subscribers can also listen to it.
- **Employees (Ch 3)** ŌĆö `complete()` sets `employees.status = terminated`. The status field on the directory and detail screens immediately reflects this. The Employee soft-delete is **not** triggered ŌĆö the row stays in the table and the audit trail intact.
- **Leave (Ch 4)** ŌĆö `OffboardingService::accruedLeaveDays()` queries the current-year `leave_balances` annual row(s) and returns `max(0, total_days ŌłÆ used_days)`. Future-dated approved leave is not subtracted in the MVP ŌĆö that nuance is in the Phase 1 backlog.
- **Loans (Ch 21)** ŌĆö `outstandingLoanBalance()` sums `LoanAccount.outstanding_balance` for accounts in `Disbursed` or `Repaying` status, and `closeOutstandingLoans()` (called from `approveSettlement`) row-locks the loan accounts, marks every scheduled `LoanRepayment` as `Waived`, sets the loan to `PaidOff` with a zero balance and `actual_end_date = today`. The loan is considered netted against the settlement at this point.
- **Payroll (Ch 19)** ŌĆö `FinalSettlement::payroll_line_id` is a nullable FK to `payroll_lines`. The off-cycle disbursement is not auto-created in the MVP; Finance creates a one-line payroll run for the leaver and stitches it back to the settlement row when paying out. Phase 2 will auto-mint that line on approval.
- **Identity (Ch 25)** ŌĆö the high-value gates (`approveSettlement` and `complete`) require a **fresh** 2FA stamp via the `2fa:fresh` route middleware. A stale session that hasn't stepped up in the configured window cannot terminate an employee or release a settlement.
- **Assets (the Assets module exists at `/assets` but is not yet in this dossier's chapter list ŌĆö flag for inclusion).** Assets are touched only by the clearance checklist today ŌĆö the IT & Assets area carries the laptop / SIM / badge items, and HR ticks them off after the IT team confirms physical return. The Assets module has its own asset-assignment ledger which today is **not** automatically scanned for unreturned items on case initiation ŌĆö Phase 1 will add a pre-flight step that pulls every open `AssetAssignment` for the employee and seeds a clearance item per asset.
- **DPA (Ch 26)** ŌĆö the case row carries the legal anchor for processing personal data after departure (the Data Protection Act, 2012 ┬¦40 retention window). Soft delete on the case preserves the audit trail; the dedicated redaction job (Phase 2) is what handles statutory erasure of the employee's underlying personal fields after the retention window.

## Standards touchpoints

- **Ghana Labour Act, 2003 (Act 651) ┬¦17 ŌĆö termination of contract of employment.** The `notice_received_on` and `last_working_day` fields are the statutory dates ┬¦17 expects to see on the record; the `reason` text captures the ┬¦18 written reason for termination. The Initiate panel's subtitle calls out the Act explicitly so HR is reminded what the legal anchor is. See Chapter 44.
- **Ghana Labour Act ┬¦18 ŌĆö notice required for termination.** The FormRequest enforces `last_working_day >= notice_received_on` so a backdated last-day is impossible. The actual notice-period rules (one month for monthly-paid, two weeks for weekly-paid) are NOT computed in the MVP ŌĆö HR types the agreed last day; Phase 1 will validate it against the contract type.
- **Ghana Labour Act ┬¦31 ŌĆö redundancy (severance).** `ExitType::Redundancy.qualifiesForSeverance()` returns true; every other exit type returns false. The `FinalSettlementCalculator` then pays `basic_salary ├Ś severance_months ├Ś years_of_service` only on Redundancy; the default multiplier is 1.5├Ś.
- **National Pensions Act, 2008 (Act 766) ŌĆö Tier-1 (SSNIT) and Tier-2 (occupational scheme).** The default clearance checklist seeds a "SSNIT discharge form filed; Tier-2 trustee notified" item under the Pension Discharge area. The item is a clerical sign-off; a live SSNIT / Tier-2 API call is roadmapped in Phase 1.
- **National Pensions (Amendment) Act, 2014 (Act 883) ŌĆö Tier-3 portability.** Out of scope of the MVP; the case carries no Tier-3 fields today.
- **Public Services Commission Code of Conduct & Conditions of Service for the Public Services ŌĆö clearance & gratuity rules.** The default checklist mirrors the PSC's standard exit pack (IT, finance, library, stores, security, departmental handover). The exact gratuity multipliers in the PSC scheme can be expressed via the per-case overrides on the calculator.
- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦40 ŌĆö data retention after departure.** Cases and settlements are soft-deleted, never hard-deleted, so the statutory retention window is observed by default. Right-to-erasure on the underlying employee data is handled by a dedicated redaction job (out of scope of this chapter ŌĆö see Chapter 26).

## Honest gaps

The chapter would be dishonest if it didn't name these:

- **Account deprovisioning is a clerical tick, not an automated kill switch.** "Disable accounts and revoke access" is a checklist item in the IT & Assets area; the system **does not** automatically force-logout, revoke API tokens, disable SSO, or mark the User row inactive when the case completes. The `Employee.status` flips to *terminated* but the underlying `User.is_active` flag is not touched. **Phase 1 must add** a listener on `OffboardingCompleted` that disables the user account, revokes Sanctum tokens, and forces an SSO logout.
- **No document generation.** The MVP does not produce an exit letter, a clearance certificate PDF, or an end-of-service receipt. The `evidence_paths` JSON on each clearance item is the placeholder for attached PDFs / scans, but the generation side (templating, signing, sealing with the Documents module's stamps from Ch 8) is not wired. Phase 1 deliverable.
- **No automatic SSNIT / NPRA handoff.** The pension clearance item is a checklist tick; there is no API call to SSNIT, no Tier-2 trustee notification email, no SSNIT discharge form rendered. Filing the form is a manual HR task.
- **No exit-interview UI.** The case has an `exit_interview_summary` column and the clearance template has a "Sign exit interview" item, but the actual interview is captured offline; HR types the summary into the case row by other means (today: through the database or a future panel).
- **Assets are not pre-flighted.** The clearance checklist names "Return laptop, mobile device & SIM" generically ŌĆö it does **not** enumerate the specific assets the employee currently has assigned in the Assets module. A Phase 1 deliverable is to seed one clearance item per open `AssetAssignment` so the IT clearer can tick each piece of hardware individually.
- **No automatic off-cycle payroll line.** When the settlement is approved, no `PayrollLine` is created. Finance has to manually open a one-line payroll run for the leaver and link it back to the settlement via `payroll_line_id`. Phase 2 will auto-mint the line on approval.
- **Re-employment eligibility is captured as a boolean, not enforced.** `rehire_eligible` defaults to true and is editable; nothing in the recruitment module checks this flag when a former employee re-applies. Phase 1 will gate recruitment-shortlist visibility on it.
- **Notice-period validation is dumb.** The FormRequest only enforces `last_working_day >= notice_received_on`; it does not verify the gap matches the statutory minimum for the employee's pay frequency (1 month for monthly-paid; 2 weeks for weekly-paid). Phase 1 backlog.

## What's planned next

Phase 1 of the government-grade roadmap (8ŌĆō10 weeks) lands seven things on this module: (1) an `OffboardingCompleted` listener that disables the User account, revokes tokens, and pushes an SSO logout; (2) auto-seeded clearance items for every open Asset assignment, plus an "Asset returned" action on each item that flips the Asset's status back to *Available* in one click; (3) a real exit-letter PDF and a clearance-certificate PDF generated from the Documents module's templated stamps; (4) a Phase 1 SSNIT and Tier-2 trustee notification flow ŌĆö an email + a PDF discharge form pre-filled with the leaver's SSNIT number and the snapshot's pension-relevant fields; (5) auto-minting of the off-cycle payroll line on settlement approval so Finance no longer has to stitch it manually; (6) notice-period validation that knows the employee's pay frequency; (7) a "rehire-eligible" gate in the recruitment shortlist UI. Phase 2 picks up the harder bits ŌĆö full Tier-2 API integration, automatic SSNIT discharge submission, and the dedicated DPA-grade redaction job that handles right-to-erasure requests after the statutory retention window closes (Chapter 26).

<!-- ===== 11_tickets.md ===== -->

# Chapter 11 ŌĆö Tickets (Service Desk)

> *In one paragraph.* Tickets is the institute's internal service desk ŌĆö the single front door for any request, fault, or incident that someone in the workforce needs another team to act on. An employee opens a ticket against IT, HR, facilities, or any other function; an IT-support agent (the primary handler in the MVP) triages it, assigns an owner, moves it through the open ŌåÆ in-progress ŌåÆ resolved ŌåÆ closed lifecycle, and the requester gets a notification the moment a terminal state is reached. Every record is pinned to an Employee (Chapter 3), every change writes a notification (Chapter 16), and every create-event feeds the institute analytics warehouse (Chapter 31) ŌĆö so the same dashboard that shows leave throughput also shows desk throughput.

## Where to find it

- **Sidebar location:** **Workplace** group ŌåÆ **Service Desk** (top-level item, `support_agent` icon). The same destination is pinned in the app switcher (the 3├Ś3 grid in the top bar) as **Tickets**, and on the Quick Actions sheet as **Open ticket** ŌĆö the Quick Actions entry routes to `/tickets?new=1` so the create panel pops on arrival, then strips the flag from the URL so a browser refresh doesn't re-trigger it.
- **Roles that see it:**
    - **super_admin** and **ceo** ŌĆö every ticket, every action. The `TicketPolicy::before` short-circuit grants super-admin a blanket pass; CEO permissions are seeded with both `tickets.create` and `tickets.manage`.
    - **it_support** ŌĆö the primary handler. Holds `tickets.create` + `tickets.manage`; sees every ticket on the desk and is the default population on the "Assigned To" dropdown.
    - **hr_admin** ŌĆö also seeded with `tickets.create` + `tickets.manage` so HR can run an HR-side desk for personnel queries that aren't IT-flavoured.
    - **manager** ŌĆö also seeded with `tickets.create` + `tickets.manage`, intended for line managers who triage their own team's queue. They see every ticket the policy allows them to see; the assignee dropdown is restricted to the support-staff pool.
    - **dept_head** ŌĆö seeded with the same `tickets.create` + `tickets.manage` pair so department heads can run a divisional desk.
    - **employee** ŌĆö `tickets.create` only. Can open a ticket and see the ones they raised or that are assigned to them; they cannot reassign, change status, change priority, or delete.
    - **finance_officer**, **auditor**, **complaints_officer**, **recruiter**, **trainer** ŌĆö all seeded with `tickets.create` only, so any role can raise a request against the desk.
- **Related modules:** Employees (Ch 3) ŌĆö every ticket belongs to an `employee_id` and renders the requester's name and employee number; Notifications (Ch 16) ŌĆö the channel for assignment and resolution pings; Reports & Analytics (Ch 31) ŌĆö receives the `ticket.created` analytics event for every successful create; Audit Logs (Ch 24) ŌĆö Phase 2 will write the create/update/delete trail here; Standards (Ch 44) ŌĆö ITIL-aligned status taxonomy plus ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.16 coverage notes; Roadmap (Ch 46) ŌĆö SLA breach automation and a category taxonomy land in Phase 1.

## The screens

![Service Desk ŌĆö Kanban board with swimlanes, quick filters, and a labeled-row card layout](../assets/screenshots/11_tickets/board.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Editorial-Sovereign masthead ŌĆö Service Desk title, requests-in-flight subhead, and the Board / List view toggle plus the cobalt "New Ticket" CTA. The view choice persists in `localStorage` under `cihrms.tickets.view` (default: board) so the user lands wherever they left. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Jira-style toolbar ŌĆö assignee chip (default narrows to "me"), quick-filter pills (All ┬Ę Mine ┬Ę Unassigned ┬Ę Overdue ┬Ę Critical) with live counts, a "/" hotkey-focused search box, group-by switcher (None ┬Ę Priority ┬Ę Assignee ŌĆö the swimlane signature), and a Comfortable / Compact density toggle. All toolbar prefs except the search itself persist under `cihrms.tickets.boardPrefs`. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Filters strip ŌĆö server-side filters (Status ┬Ę Priority ┬Ę Assigned-to ┬Ę Overdue-only) that re-fire the Inertia request through `TicketService::list()`. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Four-column Kanban ŌĆö Open ┬Ę In Progress ┬Ę Resolved ┬Ę Closed. Each card carries a status pill, a priority pill, an issue key (`SD-NNN`), an assignee avatar stack with an "+ add" affordance, a due-date row with red overdue marker, and an age "freshness" pip (fresh ŌåÆ active ŌåÆ aging ŌåÆ stale ŌåÆ cold). Cards are drag-droppable only for users who can move them ŌĆö the backend policy is the source of truth.*

![Ticket detail ŌĆö description, update panel, activity timeline, and meta sidebar](../assets/screenshots/11_tickets/detail.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Breadcrumb and headline ŌĆö back-link to Tickets, the ticket id, the title as the page heading, and "opened X days ago" in muted type. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Description card with a 5% gold hairline accent on the top edge ŌĆö the body of the ticket as the requester wrote it. ┬Ę ŌØĖ "Update Ticket" panel (manager-only) ŌĆö two selects (Status ┬Ę Assigned To) and a single cobalt "Save Changes" button. PATCHes `/tickets/{id}` with whatever has changed. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Activity timeline ŌĆö vertical rail with three entries today: Ticket opened, Assigned to X, and Resolved at HH:MM (the third only shows when `resolved_at` is set). ┬Ę ŌØ║ Right sidebar ŌĆö Status ┬Ę Priority ┬Ę Requester ┬Ę Assigned To ┬Ę Due Date (overdue marker if past due) ┬Ę Created ŌĆö six labelled rows that mirror the database columns one-to-one.*

![New Ticket ŌĆö slide panel with title, description, priority, and due date](../assets/screenshots/11_tickets/create.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Title (required, max 255 chars). ┬Ę ŌØĘ Description (required, max 5000 chars, multi-line textarea). ┬Ę ŌØĖ Priority select ŌĆö Low ┬Ę Medium ┬Ę High ┬Ę Critical, defaulting to Medium. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Due Date ŌĆö optional `datetime-local` input; the FormRequest enforces `after:now`, so today-or-past dates are rejected client- and server-side. ┬Ę ŌØ║ "Submit Ticket" CTA (cobalt gradient with shimmer); cancel returns to the board without firing anything. On success the panel closes, the requester sees a green "Ticket created successfully" toast, and the new card lands in the Open column of the board.*

> The three screenshot files referenced above will be captured in Wave 1 (task W1.27). Until then the build will substitute a "missing image" placeholder ŌĆö that is expected and does not break the build.

## Every button, every action

### Service Desk (Board view)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **New Ticket** (cobalt CTA, top right) | Opens the right-hand slide panel for creating a ticket. | `tickets.create` ŌĆö every authenticated role holds this in the seed. | One-tap raise ŌĆö the primary verb on the page. |
| **Board / List** toggle | Switches between the Kanban board (default) and a table view. Choice persists per browser in `localStorage` under `cihrms.tickets.view`. | Anyone who can see the page | Triage agents live on the board; auditors and reporters prefer the table. Both views read the same paginated list. |
| **Assignee chip** (left of the toolbar) | A circular profile chip; click to open a popover with a "Show only mine" shortcut and a search box that hunts `props.staff`. Selecting a person narrows the board to their tickets, client-side. The "x" inside the chip clears the filter. | Anyone who can see the page | The most-asked question on a busy board is "what's on my plate?" ŌĆö the chip answers it in one click. |
| **Quick-filter pills** (All ┬Ę Mine ┬Ę Unassigned ┬Ę Overdue ┬Ę Critical) | Each pill applies a client-side filter on top of the server's paged data, with a live count badge. Overdue and Critical take warning tones (red / amber). | Anyone who can see the page | The five filters cover ~95% of triage views without a round-trip. |
| **Search box** ("Search boardŌĆ”") with "/" hotkey | Free-text search across title and description; debounced 380 ms after you stop typing. The "/" key focuses it from anywhere on the page (Jira-like). | Anyone who can see the page | Cross-page search ŌĆö the server query is `LOWER(title) LIKE %q%` plus `LOWER(description) LIKE %q%`, cross-database safe (SQLite, MySQL, Postgres). |
| **Group by** (None ┬Ę Priority ┬Ę Assignee) | Splits the board into horizontal swimlanes by the chosen dimension. "Priority" orders critical ŌåÆ high ŌåÆ medium ŌåÆ low; "Assignee" buckets by user with "Unassigned" pinned at the bottom. | Anyone who can see the page | The swimlane is the signature board feature ŌĆö it makes "who's overloaded?" and "what's burning?" answerable at a glance. |
| **Density** (Comfortable ┬Ę Compact) | Comfortable shows full cards with description, due date, and age pip. Compact crops to one-line titles and tightens vertical spacing ŌĆö more cards per screen. | Anyone who can see the page | Compact is for the agent running a 30-ticket queue; comfortable for the team-lead doing a weekly review. |
| **Filters strip** (Status ┬Ę Priority ┬Ę Assigned-to ┬Ę Overdue-only) | Server-side filters that re-fire the Inertia GET with the chosen combination; "Clear" wipes everything and re-queries. The "Assigned-to" dropdown only renders for users with `tickets.manage`. | Anyone who can see the page; "Assigned-to" requires `tickets.manage` | Server filters trim what's paginated; the toolbar's client filters narrow the *visible* slice. The two layer together ŌĆö server first, client second. |
| **Card drag (open ŌåÆ in_progress ŌåÆ resolved ŌåÆ closed)** | Optimistic move on the Kanban ŌĆö fires a PATCH with the new `status` (keeping the current assignee) and reloads the column on success. The `draggable` flag is per-card: a user can only move a card when they hold `tickets.manage` or are the assignee. | `tickets.manage` or the card's current assignee | Drag is the fastest possible transition. The per-card flag keeps the UI honest ŌĆö non-managers see locked cards instead of getting a 403 mid-drag. |
| **Card click** | Opens the right-hand detail drawer (`drawerTicket`) without leaving the board. The drawer carries the same inline status / priority / assignee editors as the page, plus an "Open full view" link to the detail page. | Anyone who can view the ticket | Triage-on-the-board pattern ŌĆö keep the agent in the queue, surface enough context to act, escalate to the full page only when needed. |
| **Resolve** (drawer footer) | One-click shortcut that PATCHes `status: resolved` and closes the drawer. | `tickets.manage` | The single most-used transition; the drawer rewards it with a green button in the footer. |
| **Escape** anywhere on the board | Closes the drawer. | Anyone | Standard keyboard escape. |
| **"+" on a column header** | Opens the New Ticket panel. The column id is *not* passed ŌĆö new tickets always land in Open, by server default. | `tickets.create` | Single source of truth for "where does a new ticket start". |

> *Notes:* The board reads `props.tickets.data` directly ŌĆö there is no client-side dataset. Quick-filter and assignee-filter pass on top of the same paged array, so the displayed counts always match what's reachable. The keyboard handler is bound on mount and torn down on `onBeforeUnmount`, so navigating away does not leak a global keydown listener. The detail drawer uses the same priority-rail palette as the cards (`#dc2626` critical, `#ea580c` high, `#d97706` medium, `#12d9e3` low), so the eye stays calibrated when jumping between views.

### Service Desk (List view)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Row click** (anywhere on the body) | Navigates to the ticket detail page. | Anyone with view on that ticket | Whole-row hit target ŌĆö same pattern as the Employee directory. |
| **View** (eye icon) | Same destination as the row click ŌĆö explicit affordance for screen readers and keyboard users. | Same | Accessibility ŌĆö a `<tr>` isn't naturally focusable, this button is. |
| **Priority pill** (clickable) | Opens an inline menu of the four priorities. Choosing one fires a partial PATCH carrying only `priority` ŌĆö `status` and `assigned_to` are left untouched. | `tickets.manage` (the policy denies the PATCH otherwise; the UI shows the menu to everyone for consistency, the backend is the gate) | Priority changes faster than any other field on a desk; the pill makes it one click. |
| **Assigned-to select** (per row) | Dropdown of every support-staff user (`it_support`, `hr_admin`, `super_admin`, `ceo`, `manager`). Choosing a name fires a PATCH carrying `status` (unchanged) and `assigned_to`. Picking "Unassigned" sends `null`, which the FormRequest accepts explicitly. | `tickets.manage` only ŌĆö the cell renders a static name for everyone else | Re-assignment is the second-most-common edit; doing it from the row is faster than opening the ticket. |
| **Mark resolved** (green check, row action) | One-click PATCH that flips status to Resolved. Hidden when the ticket is already resolved or closed. | `tickets.manage` | The fast-path for "this is done" ŌĆö duplicates the drawer's Resolve button. |
| **Delete** (red trash, row action) | Opens a confirmation dialog. On confirm, soft-deletes the ticket (`SoftDeletes` trait ŌĆö the row stays in the DB and can be restored from the console). | `tickets.manage` | Same one-way safeguard as Employee delete. Soft-delete keeps the audit trail. |
| **Pagination** (bottom-right) | Standard Inertia pagination ŌĆö 20 per page (overridable via `per_page` query param), with "Showing XŌĆōY of Z" on the left. | Anyone who can see the table | Keeps long lists responsive. |

> *Notes:* The "support staff" dropdown is server-built in `TicketController::supportStaff()` as a `whereIn('role', ['it_support', 'hr_admin', 'super_admin', 'ceo', 'manager'])` query ŌĆö five role slugs. It is the same list that powers the assignee dropdown on the detail page and the drawer. The list view's inline priority menu closes on any outside click; the listener is bound on mount and removed on `onBeforeUnmount` so it never leaks across navigations.

### Ticket detail page

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Back** (top left) | Returns to `/tickets`. | Anyone viewing | Standard back affordance ŌĆö Inertia preserves the board's scroll position. |
| **Delete** (top right, red) | Confirmation dialog ŌåÆ DELETE `/tickets/{id}`. Redirects to the index after success. | `tickets.manage` | Same soft-delete pathway as the list view's row action. |
| **Description card** | Read-only display of the ticket body, preserving line breaks. | Anyone with view | The actual problem statement ŌĆö the most-read region on the page. |
| **Update Ticket panel** ŌĆö Status select | Open ┬Ę In Progress ┬Ę Resolved ┬Ę Closed. The choice is staged in local state; the request only fires on Save Changes. | `tickets.manage` | Two-field combined edit ŌĆö status and assignee are the two fields that change most often together (e.g., assigning to someone *and* moving to In Progress). |
| **Update Ticket panel** ŌĆö Assigned To select | Same support-staff list as the index. Includes an "Unassigned" option that sends explicit `null`. | `tickets.manage` | See above. |
| **Save Changes** (cobalt CTA) | PATCH to `/tickets/{id}` with `status` and `assigned_to`. The service then writes `resolved_at = now()` if the new status is Resolved, and dispatches a `TicketResolved` notification to the requester if either Resolved or Closed is reached for the first time. | `tickets.manage` | One write, two side-effects ŌĆö see the service notes below. |
| **Activity timeline** | Vertical rail with one entry for "Ticket opened" (always shown), one for "Assigned to X" (when an assignee is set), one for "Resolved" (when `resolved_at` is set). Each entry shows the timestamp where available. | Anyone with view | The minimum-viable audit story for the MVP. A full keystroke-by-keystroke timeline lands in Phase 1 when the audit log integration is wired in. |
| **Sidebar ŌĆö Status / Priority / Requester / Assigned To / Due Date / Created** | Six labelled rows that mirror the database columns. Overdue due-dates render in red with a "schedule" icon and an "Overdue" tag. | Anyone with view | Single-glance summary ŌĆö the same six facts the list view shows, surfaced as a pinned card. |

> *Notes:* The detail page hydrates from one Inertia render ŌĆö `TicketController::show` returns the ticket with `employee.user` and `assignedTo` eager-loaded, plus the same support-staff list used everywhere else. The "manager-only" Update panel hides cleanly for employees ŌĆö they still see the description, the timeline, and the sidebar, just without the edit affordances. The `is_overdue` flag is computed at the resource layer via `Ticket::isOverdue()` ŌĆö overdue means "due date in the past AND not Resolved AND not Closed", so a late-resolved ticket stops flashing red the moment it lands.

### Create form (Open New Ticket slide panel)

| Field | Validation | Who can set it | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Title** | required, string, max 255 chars | `tickets.create` | A short headline ŌĆö the line that shows on the card and in every list. |
| **Description** | required, string, max 5000 chars | Same | Free-form body. Markdown rendering is not parsed in the MVP ŌĆö line breaks are preserved with `whitespace-pre-line` and links remain plain text. |
| **Priority** | required, must be one of the `TicketPriority` enum (`low`, `medium`, `high`, `critical`) | Same | Defaults to Medium in the UI. The enum is the source of truth ŌĆö adding a new priority is a one-file change. |
| **Due Date** | optional, valid date, **must be after now** (`after:now` rule on the FormRequest) | Same | Past dates and "now" are rejected ŌĆö past-due-on-arrival doesn't make sense for a new ticket. Optional because not every request has a deadline. |
| **Employee** (`employee_id`) ŌĆö not shown in the UI | optional; if absent, the service auto-fills with the signed-in user's `employee_id` | Same | The MVP always opens tickets against the current user. The API accepts an explicit `employee_id` for future "raise on behalf of" flows that will surface in Phase 1's category work. |

> *Notes:* The Add panel does not collect `assigned_to` ŌĆö assignment happens after creation, on the board or detail page. New tickets always land in Open by server default (the migration sets `status` default to `open` and the model doesn't override it on create). On success the service fires `TicketCreated($ticket, $request->user())` before returning, and the panel closes with a "Ticket created successfully" toast.

### Update transitions (board drag, drawer, detail, inline pills)

| Field | Validation | Who can change it | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Status** | `sometimes`, must be one of the `TicketStatus` enum (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`, `closed`) | `tickets.manage` | Transitioning to Resolved sets `resolved_at = now()` on the same write. Transitioning *away* from a terminal state does not clear `resolved_at` ŌĆö the column is the historical timestamp, not a live flag. |
| **Priority** | `sometimes`, must be one of the `TicketPriority` enum | Same | Changeable at any point in the lifecycle ŌĆö including after Resolved/Closed, so an auditor can re-classify a ticket post-mortem. |
| **Assigned To** | `sometimes`, `nullable`, must exist on `users` (any active user ŌĆö not just support staff) | Same | The UI restricts the dropdown to the five support roles, but the API accepts any user id. Sending explicit `null` unassigns. |
| **(At least one of)** | `withValidator`'s `after()` rule adds an error if no status, priority, or assigned_to is supplied | Same | Empty PATCHes are rejected with a clear "At least one of status, priority, or assigned_to must be supplied." validation error ŌĆö prevents accidental no-ops from the inline editors. |

> *Notes:* `UpdateTicketStatusRequest::authorize()` checks `tickets.manage` *only*. The board card's draggable flag also honours "is the assignee" as a relaxation, which the policy backs up via `TicketPolicy::update`. That double-gate is intentional ŌĆö the assignee can move their own ticket through the lifecycle (`open ŌåÆ in_progress ŌåÆ resolved`) without needing the full management permission, but they cannot reassign it to someone else (the assignment field is `tickets.manage`-only). The service-layer `updateStatus()` carefully uses `has('assigned_to')` not `filled('assigned_to')` so that an explicit `null` unassign is honoured rather than ignored.

## The data behind it

CIHRMS stores a deliberately small set of fields per ticket ŌĆö the MVP optimises for a fast triage loop, not a full ITSM record:

- **Lifecycle columns** ŌĆö `id` (auto-increment), `title` (string, max 255), `description` (text, max 5000), `priority` (string-backed `TicketPriority` enum), `status` (string-backed `TicketStatus` enum, defaulting to `open`), `due_at` (nullable datetime), `resolved_at` (nullable datetime, set only when status transitions into Resolved), and standard `created_at` / `updated_at` timestamps plus a soft-delete `deleted_at`.
- **Identity columns** ŌĆö `employee_id` is the requester (foreign key to `employees`, `nullOnDelete` so deleting an employee soft-orphans rather than cascade-deletes their tickets); `assigned_to` is the owner (foreign key to `users`, nullable ŌĆö "Unassigned" is a valid state).

The Ticket row joins out to **one** `Employee` (the requester, with their `user` eager-loaded for name/email), and **one** `User` (the assignee). There is no separate `categories` table in the MVP ŌĆö Phase 1's category work will introduce one and migrate priority away from the single-pill "Critical" semantic into a fuller (category ├Ś impact ├Ś urgency) matrix.

What every reader of the screen needs to keep in mind:

- **Soft delete** means "removed from every list, recoverable in the console". `Ticket` uses Laravel's `SoftDeletes` trait, so deletes set `deleted_at` rather than removing the row. Right-to-erasure requests follow the same redaction-job pathway as Employees.
- **`resolved_at` is one-way in the UI**. The service sets it the first time the ticket reaches Resolved; subsequent transitions back to Open or In Progress leave the timestamp in place. That is intentional ŌĆö the column is the audit fact "when was this first resolved?", not "is it resolved right now?". The current state is in `status`.
- **`assigned_to` is a User, not an Employee.** Service desks routinely route to people who aren't on the payroll ŌĆö vendors with a system login, contractors, or platform admins who don't carry an `employees` row. Keeping the column on `users` means the desk handles all of them with the same dropdown.
- **Visibility scope** is enforced server-side in `TicketPolicy::view` ŌĆö anyone with `tickets.manage` sees every ticket; the assignee sees their own; the requester sees their own. There is no department-scoped visibility in the MVP. A department head with `tickets.manage` therefore sees the whole desk, not just their team's tickets. Phase 1 will introduce a department gate that mirrors the Employees scope.
- **Server filters and client filters layer.** The Inertia request narrows the paginated dataset (status, priority, assigned-to, search, overdue-only); the toolbar's quick-filters and assignee-chip narrow the *visible slice* of that page. The two are designed to compose ŌĆö you can server-filter "Critical only" and then client-filter "Mine" without a second round-trip.

## How it talks to other modules

- **`TicketCreated` event** ŌåÆ fired at the end of every successful create. One listener picks it up in the MVP: `RecordAnalyticsEvent` writes a `ticket.created` row to `analytics_events` with the ticket id and priority as payload, which the Reports & Analytics dashboard (Ch 31) groups, plots, and exports. The listener is queue-aware (`ShouldQueue`) so analytics writes never block the user-facing response.
- **`TicketResolved` notification** ŌåÆ fired on the transition into Resolved or Closed (and only on the transition itself ŌĆö re-firing is suppressed when the ticket was already in a terminal state). The requester's `User` is notified via the `database` channel; the Notifications module (Ch 16) renders it in the bell drawer with the message `"Ticket #{id} \"{title}\" has been {resolved|closed} by {Resolver Name}"` and a `kind: ticket.completed` discriminator so the dashboard can theme it. The resolver is suppressed if they happen to be the requester.
- **`TicketAssigned` notification** ŌåÆ defined and ready to use (database channel, queued, same shape as the resolved notification). The MVP does *not* yet dispatch it from the service ŌĆö the wiring lands with Phase 1's "notify on assignment" toggle. The class is in `app/Notifications` so tests against it pass and the contract is locked.
- **`Employee` foreign key** ŌåÆ every ticket points at an `employee_id` with `nullOnDelete`. Deleting an employee (soft-delete in practice) leaves their tickets in the system with a null requester rather than cascade-deleting them ŌĆö the audit trail wins over the foreign-key purity. The Employee detail page's Tickets tab (Ch 3) reads the inverse relation to render the latest 8 tickets per employee.
- **`SequenceService` (not used yet)** ŌåÆ ticket ids are still auto-increment. Phase 1's category work introduces a per-category prefix (`IT-NNNNN`, `HR-NNNNN`) which will move generation onto `SequenceService::next()` to follow the canonical pattern (see [Finance SequenceService convention](project_finance_sequences.md)).
- **`TicketSlaExport`** ŌåÆ an `Excel` export class that streams every Resolved ticket with title, priority, opened/due/resolved-at columns, and an "SLA Met" Yes/No flag. There is no UI button for it in the MVP ŌĆö it is invoked from the artisan console as a stop-gap until the Phase 1 SLA dashboard ships.

## Standards touchpoints

- **ITIL 4 ŌĆö Incident & Request Management** ŌĆö the four-state lifecycle (`open ŌåÆ in_progress ŌåÆ resolved ŌåÆ closed`) is the ITIL-aligned status taxonomy. We do **not** claim ITIL certification; we mirror the canonical states so an ITIL-trained team is at home, and so the analytics dashboard can be benchmarked against ITIL-flavoured industry reports. The "Resolved ŌåÆ Closed" two-step is the ITIL convention: resolved means "agent believes it's done", closed means "the requester has confirmed (or the auto-close timer elapsed)". The auto-close timer is roadmapped ŌĆö in the MVP a Resolved ticket only moves to Closed by hand. See Chapter 44.
- **ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.5.24ŌĆōA.5.26 (Information security incident management)** ŌĆö the ticket record provides the minimal evidence trail an A.5.24 information-security incident-management process requires: a structured record per incident, an assigned owner, a resolution timestamp, and a separable taxonomy (priority today, category in Phase 1). A.5.25 (assessment) maps to the priority field; A.5.26 (response) maps to the assignment + status updates. The audit trail itself lands in Phase 2 with the tamper-evident log integration. See Chapter 44.
- **ISO/IEC 20000-1:2018 ┬¦8.6 (Incident management)** ŌĆö the same four-state lifecycle satisfies the ISO 20000 incident-management clause's "definition, recording, prioritization, and resolution" expectations. Where 20000-1 expects a documented major-incident pathway, the MVP currently surfaces "Critical" priority as the proxy; Phase 1's category work introduces an explicit "major incident" flag with its own escalation rules.
- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦17 ŌĆö lawful basis** ŌĆö ticket descriptions are processed under the employment-contract basis (the same as the rest of the Employee record). The description field's 5000-character limit is a soft data-minimisation gate; the upcoming "category" picker will tag tickets that carry sensitive personal data so they can be redacted at the resource layer when surfaced to non-managers. See Chapter 26.

## What's planned next

Phase 1 of the government-grade roadmap (8ŌĆō10 weeks; see the gap analysis) lands four upgrades directly on this screen:

1. **SLA matrix + breach escalations** ŌĆö a per-priority response and resolution target (Critical = 1h / 4h, High = 4h / 1d, Medium = 1d / 3d, Low = 3d / 5d in the seed default; overridable per institute). A scheduled job sweeps the desk every five minutes and dispatches a `TicketSlaBreached` notification to the assignee, the requester, and the requester's department head when either timer pops; a second alert at 200% of target carries an "escalated" tag. The `TicketSlaExport` already in `app/Exports` is the data shape the dashboard will plot.
2. **Categories + assignment routing** ŌĆö a `ticket_categories` table with code, name, default-priority, and default-assignee-group. A category picker lands in the create panel and the API accepts the foreign key. Assignment becomes a routed default ŌĆö pick "IT ┬Ę Hardware" and the ticket auto-assigns to the IT-support pool with a round-robin; pick "HR ┬Ę Leave query" and it routes to HR ŌĆö without touching the per-user dropdown.
3. **Comments + attachments** ŌĆö a `ticket_comments` table (one row per turn, with author and body), and a `ticket_attachments` table (one row per file, with the same `storage/app/public` pattern as employee documents). The detail page's timeline grows from three system entries to a full back-and-forth thread, and the create panel grows a file dropzone. The MVP's "Activity" rail is the placeholder.
4. **Audit log integration** ŌĆö every create, update, and delete writes to the tamper-evident audit log (Ch 24) so the Auditor-General pack has full provenance on every desk transaction. The current Activity timeline becomes the user-facing surface of that same log.

Phase 2 picks up the long tail: customer-confirmation auto-close (Resolved ŌåÆ Closed after N business days of silence), a public-facing ticket portal for non-staff requesters (vendors, applicants), and a "merge duplicates" flow for the inevitable triplicate raises on a critical outage.

<!-- ===== 12_complaints.md ===== -->

# Chapter 12 ŌĆö Complaints

> *In one paragraph.* Complaints is the **named, lower-severity grievance channel** that sits inside the day-to-day HR estate: an employee or department head opens a written grievance about a workplace issue ŌĆö an unfair shift, an interpersonal dispute, a missed allowance, an environment concern ŌĆö and HR triages it, assigns an investigator, runs it through four documented states (Open ŌåÆ Under Review ŌåÆ Resolved ŌåÆ Closed), and closes the loop. Every complaint is stamped with an auto-generated `CMP-XXXXXXXX` reference at intake, and that reference is the submitter's lookup key on the public `/complaints/track` page ŌĆö they get back a status pill and two timestamps, no PII. The whole module shares the application's standard authenticated chrome (sidebar, masthead, RBAC tree) ŌĆö deliberately so, because Complaints is *the* HR-managed channel for ordinary workplace issues. It is **explicitly distinct from Whistleblower (Chapter 27)** at the schema, permission, policy, and routing layers ŌĆö the two modules do not share a table, do not share a permission tree, and do not share an investigator pool. Whistleblower is for the protected disclosures (corruption, fraud, illegality) that by law must bypass HR; Complaints is everything else. The MVP ships the bare bones of this channel: intake, status board, inline reassign, public track-by-reference, and a slide-panel detail view; the deeper comment thread, the resolution-summary surface, and the per-status notification fan-out are roadmapped.

## Where to find it

- **Sidebar location:** Complaints is **not a top-level sidebar item** in the MVP layout. The page lives at `/complaints` under the page title **"Governance ŌĆö Complaints"** with a masthead label of **"COMPLAINTS GAZETTE ┬Ę ACT 715"** in the cobalt-on-cream "Editorial-Sovereign" style. The route is reached through three paths: (1) the **Department Head dashboard** (`RoleDeptHead.vue`) carries a "Complaints" quick-action tile in its action grid; (2) any role with `complaints.create` can hit `/complaints` directly and is dropped onto the **Submit Complaint** tab; (3) any role with `complaints.manage` lands on the **Management Queue** tab by default. The intentional absence from the sidebar is a soft signal ŌĆö Complaints is supposed to feel like a form you fill in, not a system you operate. The Whistleblower lane, by contrast, *does* get a top-level sidebar slot under "System" because its surface is operated by a designated investigator team.
- **Public track endpoint:** `GET /complaints/track?reference=CMP-XXXXXXXX` is the one route in this module that sits **outside the `auth` middleware** ŌĆö it is mounted at the top of `routes/web.php` alongside the kiosk and whistleblower public routes. A submitter who left the organisation, or who never had an account in the first place (the form accepts anyone with `complaints.create`), can still come back with their reference and look up the status pill and the last-updated timestamp.
- **Roles that see it:**
    - **super_admin** and **ceo** ŌĆö wildcard `*` permission, see every complaint in the management queue.
    - **hr_admin** ŌĆö explicitly seeded `complaints.create` + `complaints.manage`. The default investigator profile.
    - **manager** and **dept_head** ŌĆö explicitly seeded `complaints.create` only. They can file, they cannot manage. The dept-head dashboard surfaces an "Open complaints" KPI as an *org-wide* indicator (see the note from `DashboardService.php` below) ŌĆö the dept-head sees the institute's count, not their team's, because complaints carry no `employee_id` FK by design.
    - **employee, finance_officer, it_support, auditor** ŌĆö all explicitly seeded `complaints.create`. Anyone in the organisation can file; very few can triage. This is the deliberate slope of the channel.
    - **No role-level shadow** ŌĆö there is no special "complaints office" role in the MVP. The triage pool is "anyone with `complaints.manage`", which today maps to hr_admin + super_admin + ceo. The Phase 1 work to widen the pool is roadmapped.
- **Related modules:** **Whistleblower (Ch 27)** ŌĆö the sibling escalation lane for the disclosures that must not flow through HR; the two channels live on different tables, different policies, different permission trees. **Service Desk / Tickets (Ch 11 in the legacy numbering ŌĆö Tasks)** ŌĆö *service* issues (a broken printer, a slow VPN, a forgotten password reset) are tickets; *HR* issues (an unfair allocation, a disputed shift, an interpersonal grievance) are complaints. **Notifications (Ch 16)** ŌĆö status-change fan-out and submitter acknowledgement are roadmapped; the MVP fires nothing. **Audit Logs (Ch 24)** ŌĆö every authenticated mutation on the complaint runs through the standard Laravel audit chain. **Standards (Ch 44)** ŌĆö ISO 10002, Labour Act 651 ┬¦65, and DPA Act 843 ┬¦39 touchpoints land here.

## The screens

![Complaints management queue ŌĆö three-tab governance gazette with stat tiles, status filter, register table](../assets/screenshots/12_complaints/queue.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Masthead ŌĆö gavel icon, the "COMPLAINTS GAZETTE ┬Ę ACT 715" eyebrow in 10.5px black uppercase, the page title "Governance ŌĆö Complaints", and a one-line subtitle ("Grievances filed under Whistleblower Act 715 ┬Ę referenced, confidentially routed, on the resolution register"). ┬Ę ŌØĘ Three-tab bar ŌĆö "Management Queue" (managers only, opens by default for them), "Submit Complaint" (everyone), "Track Status" (everyone). The active tab uses the cobalt-secondary fill with a `shadow-glow-sm`; the inactive tabs sit on the surface-container outline. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Four stat cards ŌĆö Total Complaints (gold, oversight signal), Open (red, action-needed), Under Review (amber, in-flight), Resolved (green). Each card reads its number from the current page slice ŌĆö Total is the meta-total of the paginator. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Status filter dropdown ŌĆö All / Open / Under Review / Resolved / Closed. Re-fires the listing immediately via `router.get` with `preserveState:true`. ┬Ę ŌØ║ Register table ŌĆö Reference (mono cobalt), Submitted By (italic "Anonymous" or the typed name), Details (line-clamped to 2 lines), Submitted date, Status pill, Assigned-to dropdown, Update dropdown.*

![Submit Complaint ŌĆö clean named/anonymous form with confirmation card](../assets/screenshots/12_complaints/submit.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Submit tab ŌĆö title "File a Complaint", subtitle "All complaints are reviewed confidentially. You may submit anonymously or include your name for follow-up." ┬Ę ŌØĘ "Your Name (optional)" field ŌĆö blank value lands as `submitted_by = 'anonymous'` at the service layer. The placeholder reads "Leave blank to remain anonymous". ┬Ę ŌØĖ "Complaint Details" textarea ŌĆö 8 rows, required, max 5,000 chars; the helper line "Maximum 5,000 characters" sits below; the border turns red and `form.errors.details` renders on validation failure. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Submit button ŌĆö cobalt gradient (`linear-gradient(135deg,#0d1452,#1a237e)`), shimmer-on-hover, disabled during in-flight. ┬Ę ŌØ║ Confirmation card (post-submit) ŌĆö green panel with check_circle icon, "Complaint Submitted Successfully", the parsed `CMP-XXXXXXXX` reference in a 14px mono tracking-wider span, the line "Save this reference to track the status of your complaint. Your submission will remain confidential.", and a "Submit Another" button that clears the panel and re-mounts the form.*

![Track Status ŌĆö public lookup form with reference input and result card](../assets/screenshots/12_complaints/track.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ "Track Your Complaint" title ŌĆö sits on the same authenticated page when the Track tab is selected, OR on the standalone `/complaints/track` public page (no auth required) when reached directly via a URL with a `?reference=` query string. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Reference input ŌĆö `CMP-XXXXXXXX` placeholder, monospace font, `text-uppercase` cast, Enter-key bound to submit. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Result card ŌĆö case reference in 16px mono cobalt, status badge in `type="complaint"` colour scheme (red Open / amber Under Review / green Resolved / gray Closed), Submitted date, Last Updated date. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ "Not found" amber panel ŌĆö shown when the reference returns null; tells the submitter "We couldn't find a complaint with reference `XXX`. Double-check the reference and try again." No oracle: a wrong reference does not reveal whether one once existed.*

![Complaint detail ŌĆö slide panel with reference, submitter, status, submitted date, details](../assets/screenshots/12_complaints/detail.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Slide panel ŌĆö opens from the right when a manager clicks any row in the queue; titled with the complaint reference. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Five labelled blocks (Reference, Submitted By, Status, Submitted, Details) ŌĆö all read-only in the MVP. The Details block uses `whitespace-pre-line` so paragraph breaks from the submitter are preserved. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Status is rendered as the same `<StatusBadge type="complaint">` used in the table ŌĆö the inline update happens via the row dropdown, not in this panel. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Anonymous submissions show the italic "Anonymous" label in place of a name; the row-level dropdown beside it for reassignment is not duplicated in the panel.*

> The four screenshot files referenced above will be captured in Wave 1 (task W1.12). Until then the build will substitute a "missing image" placeholder ŌĆö that is expected and does not break the build.

## Every button, every action

### Submit Complaint tab (`/complaints` ŌĆö Submit)

| Field / control | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Your Name** (optional) | Free text, max 255 chars. When blank, the service layer assigns `submitted_by = 'anonymous'` before the row is created. When filled, the typed string is stored verbatim. There is no FK back to the `users` table ŌĆö the value is a free string by design (see "How it talks to other modules"). | Anyone with `complaints.create` ŌĆö i.e. every seeded role in the system | The whole point of "named but optional" ŌĆö an employee can stand behind their grievance or stay anonymous, and the channel doesn't force the choice up-front. |
| **Complaint Details** | Required textarea, 1ŌĆō5,000 chars. Validated server-side by `StoreComplaintRequest::rules()`. The client renders the inline error message under the field on a 422. | Same | The substance of the grievance. The 5,000-char ceiling is generous for an HR write-up but stops a runaway paste; the no-floor (just `required`) accepts terse one-liners ("Shift swap was denied without reason"). |
| **Submit Complaint** (cobalt gradient CTA) | POSTs to `complaints.store`. On success, the controller redirects back with a flash message `"Complaint submitted. Reference: CMP-XXXXXXXX"`. The Vue page parses the reference out of the flash with a `/CMP-[A-Z0-9]+/` regex and shows the green confirmation card. | `complaints.create` | The whole flow's commit. The `back()` redirect keeps the submitter on the same page so they can immediately copy the reference. |
| **Submit Another** (confirmation card button) | Clears `submittedRef` and re-renders the empty form. Does not navigate. | Same | The most common follow-up is a second, related grievance ŌĆö keeping the user on the same page avoids a wasted full-page roundtrip. |

> *Notes:* There is no rate-limit middleware on `complaints.store` in the MVP (unlike `/whistleblower` which is gated at `throttle:6,1`). The complaint endpoint sits inside the standard `auth + verified` route group, so the gating is "you must be logged in", which is a different threat model ŌĆö an authenticated employee spamming complaints is an HR problem, not a security problem. A burst-protection throttle is on the roadmap nonetheless.

### Track Status tab (`/complaints` ŌĆö Track) and public `/complaints/track`

| Field / control | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference input** | Free text, monospace, uppercase-cast in CSS. Pressing Enter triggers `router.get` to `complaints.track` with the trimmed value as `?reference=`. | Anyone ŌĆö the GET endpoint is **outside the auth middleware** | A submitter who has left the organisation, or who never had an account, can still come back with their reference. |
| **Track** (cobalt CTA) | Same effect as Enter ŌĆö navigates to `/complaints/track?reference=...`. The controller calls `ComplaintService::track()` which does a single `where('reference', $ref)->first()` and renders the `Complaints/Track` page with the resource (or `null`). | Same | Exact-match lookup. Wrong references get the amber "Not found" panel, not a guess at the closest match ŌĆö the surface never reveals whether a reference was once valid. |
| **Result card** | Renders the reference, status pill, submitted date, and last-updated date. **No submitter name, no details body, no investigator name** ŌĆö the public-track surface is deliberately information-sparse so anyone who finds (or guesses) a reference learns only the state, not the content. | Anyone | Track is a status check, not a case-file viewer. The full grievance text stays inside the management queue. |
| **Back to governance** link (on the standalone page) | Returns the user to `/complaints` ŌĆö which itself sits behind auth, so an unauthenticated visitor would land at the login page. | Anyone | The link is there for an internal employee who tracked from the public surface. |

> *Notes:* The MVP's public surface is intentionally thin ŌĆö there is no thread, no submitter-reply lane, no submitter-visible resolution summary. Whistleblower (Ch 27) carries a richer two-way thread because the legal regime under Act 720 demands it; Complaints carries the minimum because ISO 10002 only requires acknowledgement, status visibility, and notification of outcome ŌĆö the three core elements all sit on this card. A submitter-reply surface and a closure-summary message are both on the roadmap.

### Management Queue tab (`/complaints` ŌĆö Queue)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stat tiles** (Total / Open / Under Review / Resolved) | Read-only ŌĆö Total is the meta-total of the paginator, the other three are counts of `data` (i.e. the current page slice). Gold for Total (governance oversight signal), red for Open, amber for Under Review, green for Resolved. | `complaints.manage` | The four numbers a triage lead checks first thing. Gold on the Total card deliberately differs from the standard cobalt module accent ŌĆö it reads as "institute-level governance metric" rather than "operational queue". |
| **Status filter dropdown** | Five options ŌĆö All / Open / Under Review / Resolved / Closed. Re-fires the listing immediately via `router.get` with `preserveState:true, replace:true` so the back-button doesn't fill up with filter changes. | `complaints.manage` | The single most-used register affordance ŌĆö hiding resolved/closed cases to focus on open work. |
| **Table row click** (anywhere on the body) | Opens the right-hand `<SlidePanel>` with the full complaint detail. Does not navigate. | `complaints.manage` | Whole-row hit target is faster than chasing a small "view" button. |
| **Assigned-to dropdown** (per row, `@click.stop`) | Lists "Unassigned" + every user in the investigator pool (see below). Changing the value PATCHes `assigned_to` on the row via `complaints.updateStatus`. Selecting "Unassigned" sends an explicit `null`. | `complaints.manage` | One-click reassign without opening the detail panel ŌĆö the triage lead's most common action. |
| **Update dropdown** (per row, `@click.stop`) | Status selector ŌĆö Open / Under Review / Resolved / Closed. Changing the value PATCHes `status` on the row via the same endpoint. The `UpdateComplaintStatusRequest` rule `Rule::enum(ComplaintStatus::class)` rejects anything outside the four-case enum. | `complaints.manage` | The other most-common action ŌĆö status is the lifeblood of the queue. |
| **Pagination** | Standard Inertia pagination ŌĆö 20 per page, current range and total shown in the footer band. | `complaints.manage` | Keeps the table responsive for institutes accumulating complaints over time. |

> *Notes:* The investigator pool that fills the assigned-to dropdown is computed in `ComplaintController::index()` as **users who hold `complaints.manage` via the role-permission pivot, OR whose legacy `role` enum is one of `super_admin / ceo / hr_admin`**. The query is capped at 50 results and sorted by name. This is permissive ŌĆö it does not filter by department, does not exclude inactive users, and does not honour any "out of office" flag ŌĆö which is acceptable in the MVP because the pool is small (typically a handful of HR officers and the C-suite) and the assignment is informational, not a hard routing decision. The richer scoping (by department, by current load) is on the roadmap. The two `@click.stop` handlers on the per-row dropdowns are what stop the row-click handler from opening the detail panel when the manager just wants to change a status or a reviewer.

### Detail slide panel (opened from any row)

| Field / control | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference block** | Read-only ŌĆö 14px mono cobalt reference (e.g. `CMP-A4F2N7XQ`). | `complaints.manage` | The first thing the investigator quotes in any follow-up. |
| **Submitted By block** | Read-only ŌĆö the typed name, or italic "Anonymous" when the row was filed without a name (i.e. `submitted_by = 'anonymous'`). | Same | The first decision point for the investigator ŌĆö is there someone to call back? |
| **Status block** | Read-only `<StatusBadge>` ŌĆö the same pill that appears in the row, in the same colour scheme (red Open / amber Under Review / green Resolved / gray Closed). The inline status change happens in the row dropdown, not in the panel. | Same | Visual confirmation of the current state before any follow-up action. |
| **Submitted block** | Read-only ŌĆö formatted as `DD MMM YYYY` (e.g. `19 May 2026`). | Same | Age of the case at a glance ŌĆö paired in the investigator's head with whatever SLA they're working to. |
| **Details block** | Read-only ŌĆö the full body of the complaint, rendered with `whitespace-pre-line` so paragraph breaks from the submitter are preserved. | Same | The substance of the grievance. There is no edit affordance ŌĆö the body of a filed complaint is immutable in the MVP. |
| **Close** (X in the panel header) | Closes the panel; nothing persists. | Same | Standard escape. |

> *Notes:* The panel is **read-only by design in the MVP**. There is no comment thread, no investigator notes field, no resolution-summary box, no "mark confidential" toggle, no audit-action emission specific to this surface. The chapter prompt asked about each of those buttons ŌĆö none of them are wired today. The path to each is on the roadmap (see "What's planned next") and the parallel surface in Whistleblower (Ch 27) is the design pattern to follow when they land.

## The data behind it

CIHRMS stores a complaint in a **single table ŌĆö `complaints`** ŌĆö with no satellite tables, no thread table, no actions table, no evidence table, no subjects table. The intentional contrast with Whistleblower (which carries five tables) is one of severity proportionality: a workplace grievance does not warrant the segregated case-file infrastructure that a protected disclosure under Act 720 does.

The columns on the `complaints` row:

- **`id`** ŌĆö primary key.
- **`reference`** ŌĆö unique `CMP-XXXXXXXX` string generated by `ComplaintService::generateReference()` using `strtoupper(Str::random(8))`. The service loops until a non-collision is found ŌĆö a probabilistic uniqueness check (32^8 Ōēł 1.1 trillion combinations) backed by a DB unique index as the hard floor. The 12-character total length is asserted in the test suite (`expect(strlen($complaint->reference))->toBe(12)`).
- **`submitted_by`** ŌĆö `string`, defaults to `'anonymous'`. **This is a free text column, not a foreign key to `users`.** Even if a logged-in employee files the complaint, the service writes the typed name (or `'anonymous'`) ŌĆö there is no shadow column that links the row to a User id. This was a deliberate choice (see the comment in `DashboardService.php:395`: *"complaints have no employee_id FK by design (anonymous-friendly, submitted_by is a free string per Whistleblower Act)"*).
- **`assigned_to`** ŌĆö nullable foreign key to `users.id`, `nullOnDelete()`. Added in a follow-up migration (`2026_05_17_055805_add_assigned_to_to_complaints.php`). Indexed for register queries that filter or scope by assignee. The `Complaint::assignee()` relation lazy-loads the user (id + name only) for the resource.
- **`details`** ŌĆö `text`, the body of the grievance. Stored as plaintext (not encrypted), reflecting the lower-severity stance ŌĆö by contrast, Whistleblower's `description` is `encrypted` at rest.
- **`status`** ŌĆö `string`, defaults to `'open'`. Cast to the `ComplaintStatus` enum on read; only the four enum cases (`open`, `under_review`, `resolved`, `closed`) survive a write because the FormRequest gates the input through `Rule::enum(ComplaintStatus::class)`.
- **`created_at`, `updated_at`** ŌĆö standard Eloquent timestamps. The "Submitted" date on every surface is `created_at`; the "Last Updated" date on the track card is `updated_at`.
- **`deleted_at`** ŌĆö soft delete enabled via the `SoftDeletes` trait. Operationally, a deleted complaint is removed from every list but kept in the DB for recoverability.

What every reader of the screen needs to keep in mind:

- **Complaints are named-but-optional, not anonymous-by-default.** The text on the form ("Leave blank to remain anonymous") makes the choice explicit, and the default value when blank is the literal string `'anonymous'`. Compare to Whistleblower where the anonymity toggle defaults to ON, the row has *no* `submitted_by` column at all, and a tracking-code hash is the only re-entry handle. The two channels are deliberately opposite defaults.
- **There is no submitter-identity column for follow-up.** Even named complaints carry only the typed string. If a submitter writes "Akua M.", that is what the investigator sees ŌĆö there is no email field, no phone field, no `submitter_user_id`. A submitter who wants to be called back has to put their contact in the `details` body, which is acceptable in the MVP context but is a usability gap relative to Whistleblower's dedicated encrypted `submitter_contact` field. The richer contact intake is on the roadmap.
- **The reference is a server-generated random string, not a hashed token.** A submitter who knows another submitter's reference can look up status (the public track endpoint will happily render the resource). They cannot see the body of the complaint ŌĆö the public surface only shows reference, status, and two timestamps ŌĆö but they can see the case exists and what stage it is at. For a low-severity workplace grievance channel this is an acceptable surface; for a protected disclosure channel it would not be, which is precisely why Whistleblower uses a SHA-256-hashed tracking code with constant-work lookup.
- **The body is plaintext at rest.** `complaints.details` is a plain `text` column, no `encrypted` cast. This matches the channel's severity model (DPA Act 843 ┬¦17 lawful-basis processing rather than ┬¦39 sensitive-data treatment). When a complaint contains sensitive-personal-data content ŌĆö e.g. an allegation of harassment ŌĆö it should be escalated to Whistleblower, which carries the encrypted-at-rest envelope. The escalation pathway itself is not yet wired (see "Honest gaps").
- **Soft delete is recovery, not erasure.** A deleted complaint stays in the DB. DPA Act 843 ┬¦40 erasure requests are handled by the same separate redaction job that covers Employees and every other module.

## How it talks to other modules

- **No event is fired.** Unlike Employee (which fires `EmployeeCreated`) and Whistleblower (which fires `WhistleblowerReportSubmitted` and `WhistleblowerCaseClosed`), the Complaints submission service emits nothing. There is no `ComplaintSubmitted`, no `ComplaintStatusChanged`, no listener wiring. The Notifications module (Ch 16) has no hook to fan-out a status-change email or in-app banner ŌĆö and so today, when a complaint goes from Open to Resolved, the submitter is not told. The only way they learn is by hitting the public track endpoint with their reference. This is the **single largest functional gap** on the module and is item one on the Phase 2 follow-up list.
- **No notification class exists.** A `grep` over `app/Notifications` for "complaint" returns no files. There is no `ComplaintAcknowledged` mailable, no `ComplaintStatusUpdated` in-app notification. The fan-out surface is empty.
- **Dashboard (Ch 13)** ŌĆö `DashboardService::index()` exposes `openComplaints` as `Complaint::open()->count()` on the top-level snapshot, which the CEO/admin overview tile renders. `DashboardService::getDeptHeadSnapshot()` also exposes `open_complaints`, but **org-wide**, not scoped to the department head's team ŌĆö the inline comment explains why: complaints carry no `employee_id` FK, so the per-team scope cannot be computed. The dept-head dashboard surfaces the figure as a general indicator.
- **Audit Logs (Ch 24)** ŌĆö every authenticated mutation runs through the standard Laravel audit chain. The public `/complaints/track` GET is read-only and does not emit; the public-submission case for Whistleblower (which does write but deliberately bypasses the chain to avoid creating an identity trail) does not apply here because Complaints submissions require `auth + complaints.create`, so the user identity is already in the system.
- **Whistleblower (Ch 27) ŌĆö explicit distinction in code, verified.** Different table (`complaints` vs `whistleblower_reports`), different controller (`ComplaintController` vs `WhistleblowerController` + admin counterpart), different service (`ComplaintService` vs `WhistleblowerSubmissionService` + `WhistleblowerInvestigationService`), different policy posture (Complaints relies on permission middleware only ŌĆö there is no `ComplaintPolicy` class; Whistleblower carries a dedicated `WhistleblowerReportPolicy`), different permission tree (`complaints.create` + `complaints.manage` vs `whistleblower.investigate` + `whistleblower.view_all` + `whistleblower.manage`), different role seeding (hr_admin holds `complaints.manage` but is *deliberately not granted any whistleblower permission* by the seeder ŌĆö the audit trail of permission seeding records this absence), different anonymity model (named-but-optional with a free-string column vs anonymous-by-default with a hashed tracking code), different encryption posture (plaintext vs `encrypted` cast on every sensitive column), different intake surface (authenticated form inside the app vs un-authenticated `/whistleblower` page with no app chrome), and different referral semantics (Complaints closes with `Resolved` / `Closed`; Whistleblower closes with `closed_substantiated` / `closed_unsubstantiated` / `closed_referred` to CHRAJ / Auditor-General / Police). The two modules are not just logically distinct ŌĆö they share **no table, no controller, no service, no resource, no request, no model, no event, no policy, no enum.** A grep for `Complaint` in the `app/Models` directory returns one file; a grep for `Whistleblower` returns six. The separation is genuine.
- **Service Desk / Tickets (Ch 11)** ŌĆö adjacent but distinct: a *service* problem (broken laptop, slow VPN, blocked SSO login) goes through Tickets and is owned by IT; an *HR* grievance (unfair shift, interpersonal dispute, missed allowance) goes through Complaints and is owned by HR. The two share neither table nor permission tree (`tickets.create` / `tickets.manage` are entirely separate slugs from `complaints.create` / `complaints.manage`), and both surface on the dept-head dashboard as separate KPIs.
- **Sidebar (Layouts/AuthenticatedLayout.vue)** ŌĆö Complaints is *not* a sidebar item. Discoverability runs through the dept-head dashboard tile and direct URL navigation. The deliberate sidebar absence is a soft signal that this is a form-you-fill, not a system-you-operate.

## Standards touchpoints

- **ISO 10002 ŌĆö Customer satisfaction ┬Ę Guidelines for complaints handling in organisations.** ISO 10002 specifies that a complaints process should be **visible, accessible, responsive, charged-no-fees, confidential, and provide closing-the-loop notification**. The MVP delivers four of the six: visibility (the queue exists, the form is discoverable from the dept-head dashboard), accessibility (the form takes Ōēż 60 seconds to submit, plain English throughout), no-fees (it is an internal channel), and confidentiality (the submitter can stay anonymous; the body is read only by `complaints.manage` holders). The two unmet: **responsiveness** (no SLA timer, no overdue banner) and **closing-the-loop notification** (no event, no mail, no in-app fan-out ŌĆö the submitter only learns by polling the track endpoint). Both are on the roadmap. See Chapter 44.
- **Ghana Labour Act 2003 (Act 651) ┬¦64ŌĆō┬¦67 ŌĆö grievance procedures.** Act 651 ┬¦64 obliges every employer with 25+ workers to establish a grievance procedure with documented steps and time limits. The CIHRMS Complaints module is the documented internal step ŌĆö the form-of-record under the employer's procedure. **What is *not* yet captured:** the ┬¦65ŌĆō┬¦66 statutory time limits (the worker shall present the grievance "in the first instance, to the immediate superior" within a defined window, and so on through the escalation ladder) are not enforced anywhere in the schema or service today. Phase 2 work on the SLA timer and an explicit escalation-step field (`step_1_supervisor`, `step_2_dept_head`, `step_3_hr`, `step_4_chro`) is the operational expression of ┬¦65ŌĆō┬¦66. See Chapter 44.
- **Data Protection Act 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦17 ŌĆö lawful basis for processing personal data.** Complaints content is processed under the employment-contract lawful basis (┬¦17(c)) ŌĆö same as Employee personal data. The body is stored plaintext because the channel is not handling ┬¦39 sensitive-personal-data categories by design; when a complaint *does* contain sensitive content (a harassment allegation, a disability matter), the operational instruction is to escalate to Whistleblower, which carries the ┬¦39-grade encrypted-at-rest envelope. The escalation pathway is not yet wired (see "Honest gaps"). See Chapter 26 and Chapter 44.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦40 ŌĆö retention & erasure.** Complaints use `SoftDeletes` and are retained indefinitely in the MVP.
