| **User** (column) | The actor's `name` and `#user_id`. `ŌĆö` and `#?` when the row has been preserved past the user's deletion (the foreign key is `nullOnDelete`). | Read-only | Identity before action. The display-name first, the numeric id underneath, so a CEO who knows the people sees a name and an auditor who is reconciling against a user export sees the id. |
| **Method** (column) | The HTTP verb as a colour-coded pill ŌĆö slate for GET (mostly absent here; the middleware skips GETs), blue for POST, amber for PATCH/PUT, red for DELETE. Monospace, bold. | Read-only | The verb is the second-fastest read on the row. Colour-coding lets a scrolling auditor pick out deletes (rare, always interesting) at a glance. |
| **Path** (column) | The request path prefixed with `/`. Truncated to one line with the full path on hover via the `title` attribute and a `max-w-md` cap so a long URL doesn't break the table grid. | Read-only | The path is the resource: `/payroll-runs/45/approve` tells you both *what* was done and *to which* row. Pairing it with the action below is the canonical audit answer. |
| **Action** (column) | The Laravel **route name** (e.g. `payroll-runs.approve`, `disbursements.dispatch`, `dpa.fulfil`), captured by the middleware via `$request->route()?->getName()`. Falls back to `unnamed-action` for the rare route without a name. | Read-only | Route names are stable across URL changes, so a query that filtered on `payroll-runs.approve` keeps working even if the URL gets refactored to `payroll/runs/{id}/approve`. The audit register prefers the stable handle over the volatile URL. |
| **IP** (column) | The request's `ip()` value. Monospace, muted. | Read-only | The fourth-fastest read on the row. Useful for "was this from inside the network or from a public address?" without needing to leave the page. |
| **Row** (whole table row) | Read-only ŌĆö no click target, no inline edit, no inline delete. The hash chain makes mutation a chain-breaking incident, so the UI deliberately offers no mutating affordance. | Read-only | Append-only is a property the page enforces by simply not building the buttons. |
| **Pagination** (footer, when needed) | Standard Inertia pagination ŌĆö 50 per page, "Showing NŌĆōM of T" on the left, navigator on the right. | Anyone who can see the register | Keeps the table responsive on installations with millions of rows. The page size matches the AG pack's nightly export cadence so a daily auditor reads roughly one page per shift. |

> *Notes:* The `Verify Chain` button is the place an auditor will press first; we therefore left it as the unambiguous slate-bordered affordance rather than dressing it as a primary CTA. The "Export AG Pack" cobalt-gradient on the right is the *destination*, not the *verifier* ŌĆö keeping the two visually distinct stops a hurried user from clicking "Export" when they meant "Verify". The eyebrow tag reads "TAMPER-EVIDENT", not "TAMPER-PROOF": no system can stop a determined database administrator from rewriting a row with `UPDATE`; the chain merely guarantees that the rewrite is *detectable* on the next nightly verification.

## The data behind it

There is exactly one storage table ŌĆö `audit_logs` ŌĆö and one model ŌĆö `AuditLog` ŌĆö and one job ŌĆö `WriteAuditLog` ŌĆö and one middleware ŌĆö `AuditTrail` ŌĆö and one command ŌĆö `audit:verify-chain` (with `audit:backfill-chain` as its idempotent sibling). The whole chapter is the story of those five files.

### Schema

The base table was created by `2026_05_12_083700_create_audit_logs_table` and extended by `2026_05_25_000007_add_tamper_evident_audit_columns`.

| Column | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | bigint | PK. |
| `chain_position` | unsignedBigInteger, indexed, nullable | Monotonic 1-based counter assigned by `WriteAuditLog` inside a transaction. Nullable because legacy rows from before the May-25 migration begin life un-chained and are filled in by `audit:backfill-chain`. |
| `previous_hash` | char(64), nullable | The previous row's `row_hash` ŌĆö i.e. the link in the chain. Null on the genesis row. |
| `row_hash` | char(64), nullable | SHA-256 of this row's canonical JSON (including `previous_hash`). Filled in by `WriteAuditLog` after the row's id and timestamps are settled. |
| `user_id` | foreignId, nullable | `nullOnDelete` ŌĆö a deleted user does **not** erase their audit trail. The pair `(user_id, action)` lets you reconstruct an actor's history even after their account is gone. |
| `action` | varchar | Currently the route name; falls back to `unnamed-action`. A future refactor will let services pass an explicit domain event name (`payroll.calculated`, `loan.disbursed`) but the existing rows are stable. |
| `route_name` | varchar, nullable | The same route name, kept separately for query convenience and so a future widening of `action` (e.g. to include sub-events on a route) doesn't lose the original endpoint identity. |
| `method` | varchar(10) | HTTP verb. Only POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE are written; the middleware skips GET. |
| `path` | varchar | The request path with leading slash. |
| `ip_address` | varchar(45) | IPv4 or IPv6, captured via `$request->ip()`. |
| `user_agent` | text | The raw UA string, no normalisation. |
| `payload` | json, nullable | The request body with sensitive fields stripped and upload-file instances reduced to `{name, mime, size}` descriptors. Strings longer than 500 characters are truncated with `...`. |
| `created_at` / `updated_at` | timestamps | Standard. `updated_at` is essentially read-only ŌĆö anything touching it after the write is, by definition, tampering. |

Indices: `chain_position` on its own (the verifier walks the chain in order); the implicit primary-key index on `id`. There is no compound `(user_id, created_at)` index yet ŌĆö the gap analysis flagged it as a Phase-2 add once row counts exceed about ten million per installation.

### Tamper-evidence: how the chain actually works

The hash chain is **real** and **enforced at every write**, not a Phase-2 plan. The mechanism, in five steps:

1. **The middleware (`AuditTrail`) captures the request** after the response has been generated ŌĆö so an exception that aborts the request also aborts the log, which is correct because a 500-response is not a state change. The middleware skips unauthenticated requests and skips non-mutating verbs (GET, HEAD, OPTIONS).
2. **The middleware sanitises the payload** before dispatching: strips `password`, `password_confirmation`, `current_password`, `token`, `_token` (so the captured row never carries credentials), reduces any `UploadedFile` to `{__file: true, name, mime, size}` (so the queued job is serialisable), and truncates any string over 500 characters with `...` (so a malicious user can't inflate the table by pasting megabyte payloads).
3. **The middleware dispatches `WriteAuditLog` to the `audit` queue.** The job is a `ShouldQueue` implementation with `$tries = 3`. Putting the write behind a queue is what keeps the request-response cycle from paying a database transaction cost on every mutation; the trade-off is that a queue worker outage produces a backlog, not a data loss, but Wave 1 (task W1.24.b) adds a Horizon supervisor alert if the `audit` queue depth exceeds 1,000 pending jobs.
4. **The job opens a database transaction, takes a `lockForUpdate()` on the latest row, computes `chain_position = latest.chain_position + 1` and `previous_hash = latest.row_hash`, creates the row with a null `row_hash`, and then computes the SHA-256 of the row's canonical JSON (including the `id`, `chain_position`, `created_at` and the just-computed `previous_hash`) and saves the row with `saveQuietly()` so the hash assignment itself does not produce a recursive audit entry.** The `lockForUpdate` is what gives the chain a total order even under concurrent writes ŌĆö a parallel `WriteAuditLog` from another request will block on the row-level lock until the first transaction commits, then read the new tail and link off it.
5. **The verifier (`audit:verify-chain`) walks every row in `chain_position` order**, asserts that the positions are sequential, that each row's `previous_hash` equals the prior row's `row_hash`, and re-computes the SHA-256 of the canonical JSON and compares it constant-time with `hash_equals`. Any mismatch breaks the loop, the command prints `Audit chain BROKEN at position X (audit_logs.id=Y): {reason}` to stderr, exits non-zero, and ŌĆö if invoked with `--notify` ŌĆö sends `AuditChainBroken` to every `super_admin` via mail + database.

The canonical JSON used by the hash is fixed in `AuditLog::canonicalJson()`. The field order, the timestamp format (ISO-8601 with `toIso8601String()`), and the `JSON_UNESCAPED_SLASHES | JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE` flags are load-bearing: any change to that method is a chain-breaking change and must be paired with a re-hash migration. The model carries a comment to that effect.

### What the chain protects against

- **Direct database UPDATE on an `audit_logs` row** ŌĆö say a malicious DBA changes the `path` on the row that records a fraudulent payroll approval. The next `audit:verify-chain` run re-hashes the row, computes a SHA-256 that no longer matches the stored `row_hash`, and breaks the chain at that position. The Pest test `verify-chain command detects a tampered row` proves this end-to-end.
- **Insertion of a new row in the middle of the chain.** The `chain_position` column is a monotonic counter; a row inserted at position 7.5 would have to lie about its position, and either it claims `chain_position = 7` (breaks uniqueness in the index and shows up as a duplicate) or it claims `chain_position = 8` (and now the row that used to be position 8 doesn't link back to it via `previous_hash`). Either way the verifier breaks.
- **Deletion of a row.** The verifier asserts `chain_position` is sequential starting at 1 (or at the `--from` value); a gap is detected on the next position-check.
- **Re-writing the chain end-to-end with new hashes** (the only mathematically valid tampering path). This requires the attacker to re-hash every row from the tampered position to the present, and ŌĆö crucially ŌĆö to do so without the chain being verified between the tamper and their re-hash. The nightly schedule narrows that window to a single calendar day; pairing the verification with off-site replication (Phase 3) collapses it to seconds.

### What the chain does **not** protect against

- **A bug in `AuditLog::canonicalJson()` that drops a field silently.** The canonical JSON is the contract; if a developer adds a column to `audit_logs` and forgets to add it to the canonical JSON, the column will be invisible to the hash and therefore freely mutable post-write. This is why the model comment is explicit and why a Phase-2 test will assert that every fillable column is named in the canonical JSON.
- **Compromise of the queue worker.** A worker writing fake rows can compose a valid chain ŌĆö the chain proves no rows have been *altered* after the fact, not that the rows were *honestly written* in the first place. The next layer of defence is the route middleware: every audit row's `action` corresponds to a route name that exists in the codebase, and a fake row claiming `action = payroll.approve` is detectable by cross-referencing the `payroll_runs.audit_log_id` column when it is present.
- **Clock skew.** `created_at` is part of the hash; if the application server's clock jumps, two adjacent rows can carry timestamps that look out of order to a human reader without breaking the hash. Wave 1 (task W1.24.c) adds an NTP-drift assertion to the verifier so a sustained skew surfaces as a chain warning rather than a silent timeline corruption.

### Backfill of legacy rows

Installations that upgraded across the May-25 migration have `audit_logs` rows that pre-date the chain columns ŌĆö `chain_position`, `previous_hash`, and `row_hash` are all null on those rows. The `audit:backfill-chain` command walks them in `(created_at, id)` order, assigns sequential positions, and computes hashes off the previous row's hash, exactly as a live write would. The command is idempotent (running it on an intact chain reports "no gaps") and supports `--dry-run` for change-control review. It also gracefully extends a partial chain ŌĆö if some rows are already hashed and a legacy row is found after them, the legacy row is chained off the tail rather than restarting the chain from genesis. The four Pest tests in `AuditChainBackfillTest` lock these behaviours in.

## How it talks to other modules

The audit log is a passive sink: it does not push to anywhere; it accumulates. But it is touched by almost every other module in the system, in one of three ways:

- **Implicit (the default).** Every state-changing route inside the `Route::middleware(['auth', 'audit'])` group in `routes/web.php` writes here automatically. There is no per-controller wiring; adding `'audit'` to a route group is the entire contract. The block at line 199 covers profile, employees, departments, leave, tickets, payroll runs, payments, audit-log search itself (no, not really ŌĆö it's a GET, so it skips), reports, announcements, notifications, AI assistant, integrations, webhooks, the Auditor-General Report Pack, the whistleblower investigator dashboard, the DPA fulfilment lane, and the SSO provider admin pages. A second smaller `'auth', 'audit'` group at line 1057 covers SSO provider admin specifically (kept separate so the routes can sit visually alongside the SSO controllers).
- **Explicit, via the per-row `audit_trail` JSON.** Some modules keep their own per-row timeline in a JSON column on the same row ŌĆö `data_subject_requests.audit_trail` (Chapter 26) and `whistleblower_actions` (Chapter 27) are the two examples. These rows give a human-readable, contextual timeline scoped to a single case that the audit register cannot match (the register is system-wide and unsorted by case). The per-row trail and the sealed chain are **both** written for the same action ŌĆö the per-row trail by the service, the sealed chain by the middleware ŌĆö so the auditor can choose either lens.
- **Explicit, via foreign keys back into `audit_logs`.** A handful of high-stakes tables carry an `audit_log_id` pointer at the moment of a critical action ŌĆö e.g. `payroll_runs.approval_audit_log_id` (Phase 2) points at the sealed row that recorded the dual-control approve. This pattern lets the run page render "Approved by Joseph Nadi ┬Ę audit row #12,341" without a join through user-action heuristics, and lets a forensic audit trace from a payroll row back to its sealed proof.

The eight modules that pin themselves to the audit chain most heavily, in order of how often they appear in a typical day's rows:

1. **Payroll Engine (Chapter 19)** ŌĆö every Draft / Calculate / Approve / Mark-Paid / Reverse transition; every statutory return generation; every GhIPSS bank-file regeneration.
2. **Disbursements (Chapter 22)** ŌĆö every GhIPSS dispatch, every MoMo provider callback, every reconciliation entry.
3. **Loans (Chapter 21)** ŌĆö every Apply / Approve / Disburse / Waive on `loan_accounts`.
4. **Finance Hub (Chapter 20)** ŌĆö every journal-entry post (the JV refusing to balance is an audit row in its own right), every Chart-of-Accounts mutation, every bank-reconciliation match.
5. **Identity & Ghana Card (Chapter 25)** ŌĆö every verification submission and outcome; every re-verification reminder; every provider failure with the failure reason verbatim in the payload.
6. **DPA & Privacy (Chapter 26)** ŌĆö every Acknowledge / Fulfil / Reject on the DPO queue; every export-bundle generation with the SHA-256 of the bundle in the payload.
7. **Whistleblower (Chapter 27)** ŌĆö every investigator triage, every internal action, every onward referral. The case *content* is segregated and does not appear in this register; only the *fact* of the action does.
8. **Identity & Access (Chapter 25's sibling)** ŌĆö every SSO provider admin change; every password change; every 2FA enrolment or reset; every role grant or revocation.

### The nightly schedule

The chain is verified once a day, at 03:00, by `routes/console.php`:

```php
Schedule::command('audit:verify-chain --notify')->dailyAt('03:00')->withoutOverlapping();
```

The time is chosen on purpose ŌĆö late enough that the day's writes have settled, early enough that a broken-chain alert is in the super-admin's inbox by the time HR walks in. `--notify` makes the command dispatch `AuditChainBroken` to every user with `role = super_admin` (note: the role is read from the legacy `users.role` enum, not the DB-backed `roles` pivot; tightening this to also consider DB-grants is a Phase-2 task). The notification ships via mail and database channels ŌĆö deliberately not Slack or SMS, because a broken chain is a high-severity but not user-facing event and the audit trail itself is the primary record of who saw the alert and when.

The notification body names the broken position, the `audit_logs.id`, the failure reason ("row_hash mismatch (data tampered)", "previous_hash mismatch", or "expected chain_position N, got M"), and the count of rows verified before the break. It also pre-types the recovery command: `php artisan audit:verify-chain --from={broken_position - 5}` so an investigator can replay the surrounding rows without computing the offset themselves.

### The Auditor-General Report Pack

When the institute's external auditor ŌĆö under the Auditor-General Act 2000 (Act 584) ŌĆö asks for the annual evidence pack, the operator goes to `/reports/auditor-general`, enters the fiscal year, and clicks Generate. The pack is built by `AuditorGeneralReportPack::generate()` and bundles fourteen artefacts into a single ZIP:

1. `MANIFEST.md` ŌĆö an index with the SHA-256 of every other file in the pack (so any tampering between generation and submission is detectable).
2. `payroll/runs.csv` and `payroll/run_lines.csv` ŌĆö every approved/paid run with full per-employee granularity.
3. `statutory/PAYE-{year}.csv`, `SSNIT-{year}.csv`, `TIER2-{year}.csv`, `NHIA-{year}.csv` ŌĆö concatenated statutory returns.
4. `statutory/bank_files/` ŌĆö verbatim copies of the GhIPSS disbursement files.
5. `identity/verifications.csv` ŌĆö Ghana Card register with the PIN masked to `GHA-ŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆó-N`.
6. `loans/accounts.csv` and `loans/outstanding.csv` ŌĆö full loan ledger plus year-end open balances.
7. `offboarding/cases.csv` ŌĆö exits and final settlements.
8. `whistleblower/summary.csv` ŌĆö **counts only**, segregated by Whistleblower Act ┬¦10.
9. `audit/chain_verification.txt` ŌĆö the literal stdout of `php artisan audit:verify-chain`, with the total row count, the first and last chain position, the last row's hash, and the exit code.

Generation requires `statutory.export` and a fresh 2FA challenge (`2fa:fresh` middleware on the generate route). Download requires `statutory.export` and is restricted to filenames matching `/^AG-\d{4}-\d{8}-\d{6}\.zip$/` to prevent path traversal. The pack itself is sealed by the manifest's SHA-256 of every file, so the auditor can verify on their machine that nothing changed between download and presentation. See the route block in `routes/web.php` lines 644ŌĆō652.

## Standards touchpoints

- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦28 ŌĆö security of personal data.** ┬¦28(1) requires the data controller to take "appropriate technical and organisational measures" to prevent unauthorised processing, loss, or destruction. The tamper-evident chain is the system's principal technical measure for ┬¦28(1)(c) "ability to detect intrusion or interference"; the daily verification with super-admin notification is the ┬¦28(1)(d) "process of regularly testing" requirement; the masking of payload fields (`password`, `_token`) is the ┬¦28(1)(b) minimum-necessary principle as applied to logs. See Chapter 26 for the full DPA mapping and Chapter 44 for the standards register.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦29 ŌĆö security breach notification.** A broken chain is a "security breach" under ┬¦29(2) and the `AuditChainBroken` notification is the first link in the breach-notification chain ŌĆö the DPO (the auditor role) is in the recipient set because the role seeder grants `audit.view` to auditor and the auditor role doubles as the DPO under Chapter 26.
- **Cybersecurity Act 2020 (Act 1038) ┬¦41 ŌĆö record-keeping by critical information infrastructure.** ┬¦41 obliges designated CII operators to keep records of all significant security events for a period prescribed by the Cyber Security Authority. CIHRMS does not yet self-designate as a CII operator, but the audit register is built to that standard ŌĆö sealed, append-only, queryable, and exportable.
- **ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.12.4 ŌĆö logging and monitoring.** A.12.4.1 (event logging), A.12.4.2 (protection of log information), A.12.4.3 (administrator and operator logs) all map directly to this chapter. The middleware-captured trail is A.12.4.1; the SHA-256 chain is the protection of A.12.4.2; the explicit super-admin and CEO grants are A.12.4.3.
- **ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.5.7 ŌĆö threat intelligence** *(indirect)*. A broken-chain notification is a threat intelligence signal in its own right; routing it to super-admins via mail and database channels is the minimum response posture.
- **NIST SP 800-92 (Guide to Computer Security Log Management) ┬¦4 ŌĆö log integrity protection.** The hash-chain pattern is named in ┬¦4.4 as the standard mechanism; this chapter implements it directly.
- **Auditor-General Act 2000 (Act 584) ┬¦13 ŌĆö access to records.** The Auditor-General Report Pack is the operational answer to ┬¦13's "all books, records, returns, reports". The pack is sealed by SHA-256 manifest so a presented copy can be cryptographically tied back to the source.
- **Whistleblower Act 2006 (Act 720) ┬¦10 ŌĆö confidentiality.** The audit register records the *fact* that an investigator triaged a case but never the content; the AG pack ships counts only. This segregation is built into the AG pack's `writeWhistleblowerSummary()` method, which selects `category, severity, status, count(*)` and nothing else.
- **Public Financial Management Act 2016 (Act 921) ┬¦52 ŌĆö record-keeping by accounting officers.** Payroll, disbursements, and loans all settle into journal vouchers that are themselves audit-logged on post; the chain is what gives ┬¦52's "complete and accurate records" its technical teeth.

## What's planned next

Phase 2 lands four things directly on this module:

1. **Action vocabulary widening.** Today the `action` column is the route name. Phase 2 introduces an explicit `audit_event` parameter on a small service-layer helper (`AuditLogger::record('payroll.calculated', $payload)`) so service-level events that are not 1:1 with a route (a scheduled retry, an internal queue handover, a soft-delete cascade) can be captured with a stable name. The existing route-name path stays as the default.
2. **Auditor-friendly detail page.** The current page is a register only ŌĆö there is no per-row detail. Phase 2 adds `/audit-logs/{id}` with a single-row view that pretty-prints the JSON payload, decodes the route name into the file:line of the handler (via a route-name ŌåÆ controller-action map), shows the row's hash and the previous and next row in the chain, and offers a one-click "Verify this row" button that re-hashes the row and the surrounding two and reports the result. The detail page is what makes this module usable to non-developer auditors during a live audit visit.
3. **Per-row foreign keys.** A small number of high-stakes tables (`payroll_runs`, `loan_accounts`, `data_subject_requests`, `offboarding_settlements`) get an `audit_log_id` pointer to the sealed row that recorded their last state-changing action. This collapses "what audit row recorded this approval?" from a free-text search to a single join, and is the foundation of the forensic-audit feature described in Chapter 47.
4. **Off-site sealed replication.** The daily verification narrows the tamper-detection window to 24 hours. Phase 3 ships the chain to an off-site append-only object store (S3 Object Lock or equivalent) every fifteen minutes so the window collapses to that interval, and any divergence between the local chain and the replicated chain is itself a sealed event.

Phase 3 then extends the model into two areas that the gap analysis flagged as government-grade requirements: (a) **integration with the Cyber Security Authority's incident-reporting workflow** so a broken-chain incident automatically opens a CSA case under Act 1038 ┬¦59; and (b) **a write-only WORM mode** in which a deployment can be configured to refuse `UPDATE` or `DELETE` on `audit_logs` at the database privilege level, leaving the verifier as a belt-and-braces second line of defence rather than the primary one.

<!-- ===== 25_identity_ghana_card.md ===== -->

# Chapter 25 ŌĆö Identity Verification & Ghana Card

> *In one paragraph.* Identity Verification is the module that turns a typed-in Ghana Card number into a verified, time-bounded, audit-grade record that other CIHRMS modules can rely on before they move money. It is the single most important Phase-1 control: the gap analysis flagged Ghana Card / NIA integration as *missing*, and this chapter is what closes that gap. The module accepts a Ghana Card PIN (the `GHA-NNNNNNNNN-N` value printed on every Ghanaian's national ID card), runs it through a pluggable verification provider (real NIA endpoint, third-party KYC aggregator, or a manual-upload fallback), stores the outcome on an `identity_verifications` row that keeps the raw PIN *encrypted* and a SHA-256 *hash* alongside for duplicate detection, fires a domain event so downstream listeners (webhook fan-out, audit log) can react, and exposes a single helper ŌĆö `Employee::hasUsableIdentity()` ŌĆö that the Payroll Engine in Chapter 19 uses as its first gate before computing any pay line. Every verification is time-stamped, attributed to the HR officer who performed it, set to expire twelve months out, and surfaced in the Auditor-General Report Pack (Chapter 24) as a masked register.

## Where to find it

- **Sidebar location:** **Governance** group ŌåÆ **Ghana Card Verification** (top-level item under the Identity Register heading). The page lives at `/identity` and is rendered by `IdentityVerificationController::index` into `resources/js/Pages/Identity/Index.vue`. The masthead carries an editorial-sovereign "IDENTITY REGISTER ┬Ę GHANA CARD" eyebrow with the legal anchor "NIA-aligned register under Act 750" surfaced in the subhead, so the page never lets you forget what statute it answers to.
- **Roles that see it:**
    - **super_admin** and **ceo** ŌĆö every verification, every employee, every field. The `before()` hook on `IdentityVerificationPolicy` short-circuits all checks for super_admin; CEO mirrors the same posture via the `null` permission grant in `RolePermissionSeeder` (which means "all permissions").
    - **hr_admin** ŌĆö full read + write. Holds both `identity.view` and `identity.verify`, so HR is the role that actually submits cards to the provider and reads the register.
    - **auditor** ŌĆö read-only. Holds `identity.view` only; can browse the register and pull the masked CSV from the Auditor-General pack, but cannot trigger a new verification. This is the segregation-of-duties posture the Data Protection Officer needs.
    - **finance_officer**, **manager**, **dept_head**, **it_support**, **employee** ŌĆö no register access. Employees do see their *own* verification row through the policy's `view` method (`$row->employee?->user_id === $user->id`), which means the Profile portal in Chapter 16 could surface the green "verified" tick to the employee themselves; the Identity Register page itself is not on their sidebar.
- **Related modules:** Employees (Ch 3) ŌĆö the `national_id` field on the employee row is what gets verified, and the encrypted Ghana Card number is back-filled onto the employee row on a successful first verification; Payroll Engine (Ch 19) ŌĆö `PayrollService::calculate()` calls `Employee::hasUsableIdentity()` as Gate 1 before it computes a payroll line, and skipped lines carry the explicit reason "Identity unverified ŌĆö Ghana Card validation required."; Audit Logs (Ch 24) ŌĆö every successful verification and every duplicate detection is event-sourced and surfaces in the Auditor-General Report Pack as `identity/verifications.csv`; DPA & Privacy (Ch 26) ŌĆö the Ghana Card number is sensitive personal data under Act 843 ┬¦17, so it is encrypted at rest, masked on screen and in CSVs, included verbatim in the data-subject export, and retained for seven years on the SSNIT statute (Act 766 ┬¦92) so an employee right-to-erasure request must hold this row back; Webhooks (Ch 27) ŌĆö `FanOutWebhooks::handleIdentityVerified` ships the verification event to subscriber tenants on the `identity.verified` topic; Standards (Ch 44) ŌĆö every legal anchor surfaced in this chapter is forwarded there; Roadmap (Ch 46) ŌĆö the remaining gaps (employee-edit hook, loan/disbursement gates, biometric template hashing) live there.

## The screens

![Identity Register ŌĆö masthead, submit form, biometric capture, register table](../assets/screenshots/25_identity_ghana_card/index.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Editorial-sovereign masthead ŌĆö `verified_user` icon, "IDENTITY REGISTER ┬Ę GHANA CARD" eyebrow, page title "Ghana Card Verification", and the cobalt "Submit Verification" CTA that scroll-jumps to the form. The subhead names the statute ("NIA-aligned register under Act 750"), the cryptographic posture ("SHA-256 hashed lookup"), and the downstream consequence ("payroll disbursement gated on verified records") in a single line. ┬Ę ŌØĘ "Submit a new verification" card ŌĆö three-column grid with Employee ID (number input), Ghana Card number (placeholder `GHA-123456789-1`, regex-validated client- and server-side), and the cobalt "Verify" submit button. Below the grid: a "Capture biometric photo" button that opens the live camera modal, a fallback "Or upload scan" file picker that accepts JPG / PNG / PDF up to 5 MB, and a secondary chip that shows the chosen file's name with an X to clear. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Register table ŌĆö Employee (name + employee_no), Card (masked `GHA-ŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆó-N`), Provider (e.g. "NIA Official Verification System", "Manual Ghana Card Upload"), Status badge, Verified at, Expires. The raw card number is *never* rendered ŌĆö the Resource explicitly strips it. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Pagination ŌĆö 25 rows per page, `withQueryString()` so filters survive paging.*

![Biometric capture modal ŌĆö live camera with face + card framing guide](../assets/screenshots/25_identity_ghana_card/biometric_capture.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Eyebrow "Biometric capture" and dynamic title "Hold Ghana Card next to face" (switches to "Confirm photo" once captured). The component pulls the user-facing camera (`facingMode: 'user'`) at 1280├Ś960 ideal and shows a dashed gold framing rectangle inset 8% from each edge ŌĆö wide enough to fit a face and the card together. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Live stage with the `getUserMedia` video feed and the framing overlay. Capture compresses to JPEG at quality 0.9 and emits a `File` named `biometric-{timestamp}.jpg`. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Action row ŌĆö Cancel / Capture while live, Retake / Use this photo once captured. Camera permission errors surface with a clear "Allow camera permission" message and a Try-again button ŌĆö no silent failures. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ The captured frame becomes the `evidence` field on the form submission; the controller stores it under the `identity_evidence/` disk and pins the path on the `IdentityVerification` row.*

> The two screenshot files referenced above will be captured in Wave 1 (task W1.25). Until then the build will substitute a "missing image" placeholder ŌĆö that is expected and does not break the build.

## Every button, every action

### Identity Register page

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Submit Verification** (cobalt CTA, top right) | Anchors to `#identity-verify` ŌĆö the form sits on the same page rather than behind a slide panel, because this is the single action this page is for. | `identity.verify` (hr_admin, super_admin, ceo) | Verification is the *only* mutating action this module exposes; demoting it to a popover would be theatre. The CTA stays visible from any scroll position. |
| **Employee ID** | Numeric input that maps to `employees.id`. Validated server-side as `required | integer | exists:employees,id` ŌĆö a nonexistent id returns a 422 before the provider is even called. | Same | The PIN is verified *against an employee* ŌĆö there is no anonymous lookup. The integer id rather than employee_no keeps the call cheap (PK lookup) and unambiguous across departments. |
| **Ghana Card number** | Free-text input with placeholder `GHA-123456789-1`. Client-side `required` only; server-side `regex:/^GHA-\d{9}-\d$/i` is what actually gates. Whitespace and case are normalized (`strtoupper(preg_replace('/\s+/', '', trim($x)))`) before hashing or sending to the provider, so `  gha-123456789-1  ` and `GHA-123456789-1` produce the same hash. | Same | The NIA's canonical Ghana Card format is exactly this ŌĆö three letters, a dash, nine digits, a dash, a check digit. The regex is the gate that stops malformed PINs reaching the provider at all. The case-insensitive `i` flag lets HR type lowercase without re-typing. |
| **Capture biometric photo** | Opens the `BiometricCapture` modal, requests camera permission, and on capture emits a JPEG `File` that populates `form.evidence`. | Same | Many MDAs need a face-and-card composite as part of their internal evidence trail. Doing it in-browser means no extra hardware; doing it in JPEG (not raw) means the upload stays under 5 MB even at 1280├Ś960. |
| **Or upload scan** (file picker) | Alternative to the live capture ŌĆö accepts `.jpg / .jpeg / .png / .pdf`, max 5,120 KB (`mimes:jpg,jpeg,png,pdf | max:5120`). Mutually exclusive with the captured photo (the same `form.evidence` field). | Same | Some environments have no working webcam (think the dept head submitting from a CCTV-locked office). A PDF scan path keeps the module unblocked. |
| **Captured-file chip** | Displays the chosen file's name in a secondary-coloured pill with a `├Ś` to clear. Calls `clearCapture()` which nulls `form.evidence` and the displayed name. | Same | One-click undo on the wrong file is faster than re-opening the file picker. |
| **Verify** (submit) | POSTs to `/identity` with `forceFormData: true` (because the evidence field is multipart). On success the form resets and a green flash carries "Identity verified successfully."; on failure a red flash carries the provider's failure reason verbatim. | `identity.verify` enforced both at the form-request level (`VerifyIdentityRequest::authorize()`) and at the route middleware (`permission:identity.verify`). The double-gate is intentional. | Belt-and-braces ŌĆö the FormRequest stops the call before validation runs, the middleware stops it before the FormRequest is constructed, and either alone would be enough to refuse. Re-running an identical verification is allowed (it produces a fresh row) because re-verification at the 11-month mark is the official re-verification flow. |
| **Register table** | Read-only list of every `IdentityVerification` row visible to the current user (super_admin / `identity.view` see all; everyone else sees only their own employee's rows). Sorted `latest()`. | `identity.view` for the bulk view; any user for their own row | The auditor's primary lens ŌĆö "show me the verified-identity register for this tenant." The masked column means the raw PIN is never on screen even for super_admin. |
| **Pagination** | Standard Inertia pagination, 25 per page, query string preserved. | Anyone with the view | At a 5,000-employee MDA this is the page size that keeps the first paint under 200 ms. |
| **Stats** (computed server-side, not yet surfaced on the page) | Four numbers ŌĆö verified count, pending count, failed count, count of *active* employees with no usable verification. They are calculated by the controller and passed in `props.stats` but the current `Index.vue` template doesn't render them as cards. The component does compute a forward-compatible `rejectedCount` that falls back through `props.stats?.rejected ?? props.stats?.failed`. | Same as the page itself | The numbers are wired and waiting ŌĆö surfacing them as stat-cards is the Wave-1 polish task (W1.25.a). The most useful one is "unverified active employees" because that is the live ghost-worker risk surface. |

> *Notes:* The form intentionally does *not* expose a provider dropdown. The provider is selected globally via `config('identity.driver')`, defaulting to `manual_upload`. This is deliberate: an HR officer should not be able to "downgrade" a verification to manual to bypass a stricter provider, and an MDA's choice of provider is a configuration decision (set in `.env` against the `IDENTITY_PROVIDER` key), not a per-record one.

## The data behind it

There is exactly one storage table ŌĆö `identity_verifications` ŌĆö and one immutable cast on the model that makes the rest of the privacy story tractable.

### Schema (`2026_05_25_000006_create_identity_verifications`)

| Column | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | bigint | PK |
| `employee_id` | foreign | Cascade-on-delete. Points at the verified employee; one employee can have many verifications over time. |
| `provider` | varchar(32) | Slug ŌĆö `nia_official`, `third_party_kyc`, or `manual_upload`. Cast to `IdentityProviderKind` enum on the model. |
| `ghana_card_number` | varchar | **Encrypted via the model cast (`'encrypted'`).** At rest, this column is ciphertext; reads transparently decrypt. The model's `$hidden = ['ghana_card_number']` strips it from any JSON serialisation by default. |
| `ghana_card_hash` | varchar(64), indexed | **SHA-256 fingerprint of the normalized PIN.** This is the duplicate-detection key ŌĆö comparing two cards across two employees is a single indexed equality check that never decrypts anything. |
| `status` | varchar(16) | One of `pending`, `verified`, `failed`, `expired`, `disputed` (cast to `IdentityVerificationStatus` enum). `expired` and `disputed` exist in the enum but are not yet written by any code path ŌĆö they are reserved for the re-verification job (`Expired`) and the dispute workflow (`Disputed`). |
| `verified_at` | timestamp | Set on success; null otherwise. |
| `verified_by` | foreign(users) | Null-on-delete. The HR officer who submitted the verification (`$request->user()`). Null when the verification came from the async job rather than a UI submit. |
| `expires_at` | timestamp | Twelve months from `verified_at` by default. Sourced from the provider's `VerificationResult::expiresAt` (each provider returns `+12 months` from `now()` on success). |
| `evidence_path` | varchar | Storage path under `identity_evidence/` if the HR officer attached a biometric photo or PDF scan. Otherwise null. |
| `raw_response` | json | Whatever the provider sent back. For `nia_official` that's the full NIA JSON body; for `third_party_kyc` it's the vendor payload plus a `vendor` key; for `manual_upload` it's `{'normalized': '...', 'mode': 'manual', 'note': 'Pending senior-officer manual approval.'}`. |
| `failure_reason` | text | The provider's literal failure message when `status = failed`. Surfaced in the flash error and on the Auditor-General CSV. |
| `created_at` / `updated_at` | timestamps | Standard. |
| `deleted_at` | timestamp | Soft delete ŌĆö a redacted verification still leaves the row visible for the SSNIT 7-year retention rule (Act 766 ┬¦92). |

Indices: `(employee_id, status)` for the "is this employee currently verified?" lookup; `(status, expires_at)` for the `identity:expiring` scanner.

### What is verified, exactly

Three things, and **not** four:

1. **The Ghana Card PIN format.** Every provider, including `manual_upload`, refuses anything that doesn't match `GHA-NNNNNNNNN-N` after normalization. Malformed PIN never gets a verified row.
2. **The PIN against the chosen authority.** What "authority" means depends on the provider ŌĆö see the table below.
3. **The personal payload (full name, date of birth, phone).** The service constructs a payload from the employee row (`personalPayload()`) and passes it to the provider; the NIA provider sends `pin`, `full_name`, and `date_of_birth` in its request body, so a mismatch surfaces as a "matched: false" with a `reason` such as "PIN found but biographic data does not match".

What is **not** verified yet:

- **Biometric match (fingerprint / facial recognition).** The `BiometricCapture` component captures a photo, but the captured image is stored as audit evidence only ŌĆö *no fingerprint or facial-recognition match is computed*. The photo is a chain-of-custody artefact, not a biometric template. This is by design for the MVP: hashing a real biometric template requires an enrolled template from the NIA's IVS that is not available to third-party MDAs without an MoU.
- **Card photo OCR or chip read.** The MVP does not read the printed photo, the optional MRZ, or the chip on the card. A future card-scanner integration is on the Phase-1 roadmap (Chapter 46).

### Provider matrix

| Driver | Class | Endpoint | Auth | What it actually does | When you'd use it |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `nia_official` | `NiaOfficialProvider` | `POST {NIA_BASE_URL}/verify` ŌĆö default `https://api.nia.gov.gh/verify` | Bearer token (`NIA_API_KEY`) | Sends `{pin, full_name, date_of_birth}`, expects `{matched: bool, reason?: string}`. On a 200 + `matched=true` returns success with a 12-month expiry. On non-2xx, transport error, or `matched=false` returns failure with the NIA reason surfaced verbatim. 8-second timeout (configurable via `NIA_TIMEOUT`). | Production, after the MDA has signed the NIA institutional MoU and has been issued a shared-secret API key. **No live NIA endpoint is hit from any test or seed** ŌĆö the request shape is fully covered by an HTTP-faked test suite. |
| `third_party_kyc` | `ThirdPartyKycProvider` | `POST {KYC_BASE_URL}/ghana/card` | `X-API-KEY` header (`KYC_API_KEY`) | Generic adapter for uqudo, Smile ID, Youverify, or any aggregator that re-publishes the NIA data. Sends `{card_number, first_name, last_name}`, expects `{data: {verified: bool}}` or `{verified: bool}`. 10-second timeout. | Pilot phase before the official NIA MoU is finalised, or as a redundant secondary check for high-value approvals. |
| `manual_upload` | `ManualUploadProvider` | None ŌĆö no network call | None | Regex-validates the PIN against `GHA-NNNNNNNNN-N` and returns success pending senior-officer manual approval. The 12-month expiry is set anyway, so a manual verification still ages out. The `raw_response` JSON records `{mode: 'manual', note: 'Pending senior-officer manual approval.'}` so the auditor can tell which rows came from this path. | The default. Used in dev, staging, and any MDA that has not yet provisioned an `IDENTITY_PROVIDER` and `NIA_API_KEY`. Acceptable for the MVP because the *PayrollService* still refuses to pay an employee whose latest verification is `failed` or `expired`. |

The active provider is chosen at the container layer in `IdentityServiceProvider::register()`. The choice is `env('IDENTITY_PROVIDER', 'manual_upload')` ŌĆö so the default posture out of the box is manual-upload, and an MDA flips to `nia_official` only after the API key is provisioned.

### What gets stored versus what gets seen

| Surface | Sees | Does not see |
|---|---|---|
| The `identity_verifications` table at rest | Ciphertext PIN + SHA-256 hash | Plain PIN |
| The `IdentityVerificationResource` (used by the page and the API) | Masked tail (`GHA-ŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆó-N`) | Plain PIN |
| The Auditor-General CSV (`identity/verifications.csv`) | Masked tail (same mask format) | Plain PIN |
| The data-subject export (Chapter 26) | Just the last digit (`card_tail`) | Plain PIN |
| Any JSON serialisation of the model | Nothing ŌĆö the field is in `$hidden` | Plain PIN |
| The `IdentityExpiringReminder` mail | Only the `expires_at` date | Plain PIN |
| The captured biometric JPEG | Whatever the user pointed the camera at | n/a |

The plain PIN is decryptable only inside PHP, on the model accessor ŌĆö there is no UI surface, API surface, CSV surface, or notification surface that emits it.

## How it talks to other modules

- **`IdentityVerified` event** ŌåÆ fired at the end of every successful `IdentityVerificationService::verify()` call. One listener picks it up: `FanOutWebhooks::handleIdentityVerified` ships an `identity.verified` envelope (`{verification_id, employee_id, provider, verified_at}`) to every external webhook subscriber tenant. The event is *not* hooked into `RecordAnalyticsEvent` yet ŌĆö that's a Phase-1 polish item.
- **`DuplicateIdentityDetected` event** ŌåÆ fired by `detectDuplicates()` after a successful verification, when the same `ghana_card_hash` already maps to a different employee with a verified row. The payload is `{cardHash, employees[]}` (where `employees` is the list of all employees sharing the hash, including the current one). **There is no listener wired for this event yet** ŌĆö it is the leading indicator of a ghost-worker fraud attempt, and the audit-log / alerting wiring lives in the Phase-1 backlog. The event is dispatched, captured by the test suite, and ready for a listener.
- **Employees (Ch 3)** ŌĆö on a successful verification, `IdentityVerificationService` back-fills the employee's `national_id` field if it was empty: `if ($result->success && empty($employee->national_id)) { $employee->forceFill(['national_id' => $ghanaCardNumber])->save(); }`. This means subsequent payroll and disbursement modules can read the PIN off the employee row without re-querying the verification table. The reverse direction ŌĆö re-verifying when the employee edits their `national_id` in the Profile portal ŌĆö is *not yet hooked up*; that's the `VerifyEmployeeIdentity` job, which exists and works on a queued path but is not dispatched anywhere automatically.
- **Payroll Engine (Ch 19)** ŌĆö `PayrollService::calculate()` calls `Employee::hasUsableIdentity()` as Gate 1. The helper returns `true` iff the employee has at least one `IdentityVerification` with `status = verified` and either no expiry or an expiry in the future. A `false` result causes the line to be skipped with `skip_reason = "Identity unverified ŌĆö Ghana Card validation required."`. The skipped row is still persisted, still appears in the Lines tab in amber, and still counts against the `skipped_count` so the run's headcount reconciles. **This is the only place in the codebase where `hasUsableIdentity()` is called** ŌĆö see the gap analysis below.
- **Audit Logs (Ch 24)** ŌĆö the Auditor-General Report Pack pulls every `identity_verifications` row into `identity/verifications.csv`, with the masked tail format (`GHA-ŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆóŌĆó-N`), the provider, the status, the verified-at and expires-at timestamps, and the failure reason. The plain PIN is never written to the export.
- **DPA & Privacy (Ch 26)** ŌĆö `DataSubjectExportBuilder::identityVerifications()` includes the masked tail (`substr($v->ghana_card_number, -1)`) so the data subject sees their own verification history without the export becoming a target for re-identification. `ErasureService` *holds back* `identity_verifications` rows on a right-to-erasure request, citing "National Pensions Act 2008 (Act 766) ┬¦92 ŌĆö 7-year retention" ŌĆö because SSNIT's statutory retention rule overrides the DPA ┬¦40 right within the retention window.
- **Webhooks (Ch 27)** ŌĆö the `identity.verified` topic is one of four event topics fanned out by `FanOutWebhooks`. Subscribers can react to "this employee just got a verified Ghana Card" in real time ŌĆö useful for an external GIFMIS bridge or a partner KYC tracker.
- **Public API v1 (Ch 28)** ŌĆö not yet exposed. The verification register has no API route. A read-only `GET /api/v1/identity/verifications` is a one-day Phase-1 task; the Resource and Policy are already in place.

### The expiring scanner

`php artisan identity:expiring --window=30` is a queueable command that:

1. Queries every `IdentityVerification` where `status = verified` and `expires_at` is inside the next N days (default 30).
2. Sends each employee an `IdentityExpiringReminder` notification (mail + database channels) with the days-remaining count and a link back to `/profile`.
3. Logs how many it notified.

The command is idempotent on the mail channel (Laravel doesn't de-dupe across days), and chatty on the database channel (one row per scan, so HR sees the cadence). It is wired to be run nightly by the system scheduler in production; the schedule entry is on the Wave-1 backlog.

### Cross-module triggers that *should* fire but do not yet

The prompt asks where high-risk approvals require `hasUsableIdentity()`. The honest answer is:

- **Payroll (Ch 19)** ŌĆö *yes*, enforced in `PayrollService::calculate()`. This is the gate.
- **Loans (Ch 21)** ŌĆö *not yet*. `LoanService` does not call `hasUsableIdentity()` before approving or disbursing a loan. The Phase-1 roadmap calls for this gate; the code change is two lines.
- **Disbursements (Ch 22)** ŌĆö *indirectly*. Payroll disbursements pass through the identity gate because Payroll already gates the calculate step. Off-cycle disbursements (an ad-hoc allowance, a per-diem advance, a one-off settlement) do not yet check identity directly. The Phase-1 roadmap adds a `Disbursement::requiresIdentity()` predicate that the `BatchDisbursementService` will honour.
- **Off-boarding (Chapter to follow)** ŌĆö *not yet*. Final-settlement payouts inherit Payroll's gate when the settlement is routed through a payroll run; standalone settlements bypass the check.

This is the most important honesty note in this chapter: the gap analysis (8 days old, dated 2026-05-13) called Ghana Card / NIA integration *missing*; today it is *shipped* end-to-end as a verification module but *partially wired* into the modules that should be gated by it. Payroll is gated. The other three are not.

## Standards touchpoints

The Identity module's posture is anchored against three statutes and one international standard. Every name below is hand-written, with a one-line description, and forwarded to **Chapter 44 ŌĆö Standards & Statute Index**.

- **National Identification Authority Act, 2006 (Act 707) ŌĆö Ghana Card mandate** ŌĆö Act 707 establishes the NIA and mandates the Ghana Card as the country's primary national identification document. The CIHRMS Identity module's PIN-format gate (`/^GHA-\d{9}-\d$/`), the NIA-Official provider (which targets `https://api.nia.gov.gh` by default), and the 12-month re-verification window are the operational implementation of the Act's identity-assurance expectation for payroll-bearing institutions. See Chapter 44.
- **National Identity Register Act, 2008 (Act 750) ŌĆö Register operating posture** ŌĆö Act 750 governs the operation of the National Identity Register; the page masthead names "NIA-aligned register under Act 750" as the explicit anchor, and the encrypted-at-rest + masked-on-screen + duplicate-detected posture is the operational reading of the Act's confidentiality and accuracy expectations. See Chapter 44.
- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦17 ŌĆö Lawful basis for sensitive personal data** ŌĆö biometric and identity data are *sensitive personal data* under the Act. The module relies on the employment-contract basis combined with the legal-obligation basis (PAYE, SSNIT, Tier-2 returns require a verified identity), and the encryption-at-rest + masking-everywhere + audit-trail posture is the operational reading of ┬¦17's "appropriate safeguards" clause. See Chapter 26 and Chapter 44.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦39 ŌĆö Security of personal data** ŌĆö the SHA-256 hash for duplicate detection (no PII compared in plaintext), the encrypted column cast, the `$hidden` strip on the model, the resource-layer masking, the policy-layer access control (`identity.view` is a separate permission grant), and the immutable audit trail constitute the ┬¦39 "appropriate technical and organisational measures" defence. See Chapter 26 and Chapter 44.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦40 ŌĆö Right to erasure** ŌĆö `ErasureService` explicitly holds `identity_verifications` rows back from erasure for seven years under SSNIT's Act 766 ┬¦92 retention rule, and the held-back log entry names the statute so the auditor and the data subject both see *why* the row was retained. See Chapter 26 and Chapter 44.
- **National Pensions Act, 2008 (Act 766) ┬¦92 ŌĆö Seven-year SSNIT retention** ŌĆö the retention statute that justifies the held-back posture above. See Chapter 44.
- **Cybersecurity Act, 2020 (Act 1038) ŌĆö Registration with CSA for handling biometrics** ŌĆö the Act requires institutions that handle biometric data to register with the Cyber Security Authority. The MVP captures biometric photos as audit evidence only (no template extraction, no template storage, no biometric matching), but the moment a future release extracts a fingerprint or facial-recognition template, the MDA's CSA registration becomes load-bearing. The current posture is "biometric photo as chain-of-custody artefact, not biometric template" ŌĆö and the chapter records that explicitly for the auditor. See Chapter 44.
- **ISO/IEC 27001:2022 A.9 ŌĆö Access control** ŌĆö the layered access control here (policy + middleware + FormRequest authorize + per-resource policy `view`) is the operational reading of A.9.1.1 ("access control policy") and A.9.2.3 ("management of privileged access rights"). The CEO + super_admin pattern, with auditor segregated to read-only and HR's verify capability gated by a dedicated permission, is the same SoD model used by the payroll engine. See Chapter 44.
- **ISO/IEC 29115 ŌĆö Entity authentication assurance** ŌĆö the four-level assurance framework. The CIHRMS NIA-Official provider operates at LoA-3 (high-assurance, government-source verification with cryptographic auth); the third-party-KYC provider operates at LoA-2 (substantial assurance, redistributed authoritative source); the manual-upload provider operates at LoA-1 (some confidence, format-checked self-declaration pending officer approval). Each row's `provider` column records which assurance level applied. See Chapter 44.

## What's planned next

Phase 1 of the government-grade roadmap (see Chapter 46) closes the six gaps still visible in this module: **(1)** wire `hasUsableIdentity()` into `LoanService::approve` and `LoanService::disburse` so high-value loans cannot issue against an unverified employee; **(2)** add a `Disbursement::requiresIdentity()` predicate on the off-cycle disbursement path so the gate is not Payroll-only; **(3)** add a `Listener` for `DuplicateIdentityDetected` that writes to the immutable audit log and pages the on-call HR officer ŌĆö duplicate detection fires today, but nothing reacts to it; **(4)** dispatch `VerifyEmployeeIdentity` automatically when an employee edits their `national_id` in the Profile portal, so the verified state cannot get out of sync with the stored PIN; **(5)** surface the four stat-cards (`verified`, `pending`, `failed`, `unverified_employees`) on the register page ŌĆö they are computed by the controller and waiting for the template; **(6)** wire the `identity:expiring` artisan command into the production scheduler at 02:00 daily, so the 30-day reminder fires without an operator. Phase 2 adds biometric template extraction (with the matching CSA registration), card photo OCR / MRZ read, and an "Identity Disputed" workflow that uses the `Disputed` enum case the model already supports. Phase 3 promotes the NIA-Official provider from a hand-rolled HTTP client to the certified NIA IVS SDK once the institutional MoU is in place ŌĆö the contract is provider-pluggable, so the migration is a one-class swap, not a refactor.

<!-- ===== 26_dpa_privacy.md ===== -->

# Chapter 26 ŌĆö Data Protection (Act 843) & Privacy

> *In one paragraph.* The Privacy module is CIHRMS's statutory answer to Ghana's Data Protection Act 2012 (Act 843). Any data subject ŌĆö whether they still log in, never logged in, or had their account purged years ago ŌĆö can file an Access, Rectification, Erasure, Portability, Objection, or Information request and CIHRM Ghana has thirty days under ┬¦22 to respond. The codebase carries the full lifecycle in two halves: a **public-facing DPA portal** at `/dpa` for people who don't (or no longer) have a login, gated by an emailed magic-link verification; and an **authenticated Privacy module** at `/privacy` for the people who do, surfaced from the Profile portal. Both feed the same `DPO Queue` at `/admin/privacy`, where the Auditor ŌĆö who doubles as the Data Protection Officer to keep HR out of erasure decisions about its own employees ŌĆö acknowledges, fulfils, or rejects each request with a written statutory basis. Fulfilment produces a tamper-evident ZIP export with a SHA-256 hash; erasure tombstones PII while preserving rows the law forces us to keep (payroll six years, SSNIT seven years, the audit chain forever). The whole lifecycle is itself audit-logged, both inside the `data_subject_requests` row's own `audit_trail` JSON and in the tamper-evident chain from Chapter 24.

## Where to find it

- **Sidebar location:**
    - **Admin** group ŌåÆ **DPA Requests** (`policy` icon, visible only when `privacy.fulfill` is granted). This is the DPO's queue.
    - **There is no sidebar entry for the subject self-service page in the MVP.** Employees reach `/privacy` from the Profile portal (Chapter 16) or by direct URL; this is a known nav gap and is listed in *What's planned next* below.
- **Public portal:** `/dpa` is **unauthenticated** and reachable from the institute home page footer ("Privacy Charter" link), throttled to ten requests per minute per IP. It is the only mechanism for ex-employees, failed applicants, and members of the public to exercise their Act 843 rights.
- **Roles that see it:**
    - **super_admin** ŌĆö bypasses every policy check via `DataSubjectRequestPolicy::before()`; sees and can fulfil any request, and is the only role that can sign off on `privacy.erase` (the destructive tombstoning step).
    - **auditor** ŌĆö the default Data Protection Officer in the seeded role matrix. Holds `privacy.fulfill`, sees the entire DPO queue, can acknowledge, fulfil non-erasure requests, and reject with a statutory citation. The seeder explicitly notes that auditor (not HR) gets the lane "to avoid conflict of interest where HR processes their own employees' erasure requests".
    - **Every authenticated user** ŌĆö has the inherent right to file a request about themselves and to withdraw it before fulfilment; no permission is required. They never see anyone else's request.
    - **Anyone with an email address** ŌĆö can file via the public `/dpa` portal without ever having had a CIHRMS account, provided they verify their address via the emailed magic link.
- **Related modules:** Employees (Chapter 3) ŌĆö the data subject of every employment-related request, and the source for most of the export bundle; Audit Logs (Chapter 24) ŌĆö every transition on a DSR row appends a tamper-evident entry, and the `12-audit_log_entries.json` slice of the export pack draws from that chain; Identity (Chapter 25) ŌĆö Ghana Card verifications are listed in the export and tombstoned-around (kept for the seven-year SSNIT retention floor) on erasure; Profile Portal (Chapter 16) ŌĆö the in-app self-service entry point to `/privacy/my`; Whistleblower (separately) ŌĆö content from cases where the subject is a respondent is **explicitly excluded** from exports under Whistleblower Act 2006 (Act 720), and that exclusion is recorded on the manifest cover sheet.

## The screens

![DPO queue ŌĆö overdue banner, SLA ring, type bars, register](../assets/screenshots/26_dpa_privacy/dpo_queue.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Overdue alert banner ŌĆö appears only when one or more requests have passed their thirty-day target. Counts and a single-click "Show overdue" jump filter the table to just those rows. The banner is the most prominent visual element on the page and is what an external auditor will look at first. ┬Ę ŌØĘ SLA compliance ring ŌĆö percent of open requests still within the ┬¦22 window, colour-shifted from cobalt (Ōēź90 percent) through navy, gold, and rose (below 40 percent). Average age of open queue in days printed underneath. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Composition bars ŌĆö six rows, one per Act 843 right, sorted by volume. Clicking a bar filters the register below. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Status / type filter strip + register table ŌĆö every row colour-codes the left border by request type, badges the status, and shows days remaining or days overdue tabular-numbered for at-a-glance scanning. ┬Ę ŌØ║ Legal footer reminding the DPO that every decision is audit-logged and that erasure is two-factor gated.*

![DPO case file ŌĆö subject statement, fulfil/reject actions, erasure receipt, audit trail](../assets/screenshots/26_dpa_privacy/case_show.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Subject statement ŌĆö the verbatim text the subject submitted, plus the optional rectification details or objection-purpose blocks when relevant. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Two-column action pane ŌĆö "Fulfil" (cobalt, drafts the decision summary, twenty-character minimum) and "Reject" (rose, requires a statutory_basis citation in addition to the summary). The Fulfil button is labelled "Fulfil (2FA required)" because the route is gated by `2fa:fresh` ŌĆö the DPO must have completed a 2FA challenge within the last few minutes, matching the posture used on payroll reversals and bad-debt write-offs. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Outcome block ŌĆö once closed, the decision summary, rejection basis (if rejected), and SHA-256 hash of the export bundle (if any). ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Erasure receipt ŌĆö for erasure fulfilments only, lists every field tombstoned and every row held back under a named statute (Income Tax Act ┬¦43 six-year hold, Pensions Act ┬¦92 seven-year hold, audit-chain integrity). ┬Ę ŌØ║ Audit trail ŌĆö the per-request `audit_trail` JSON rendered chronologically, every entry tagged with action, actor id, and ISO-8601 timestamp.*

![My Privacy Requests ŌĆö education panel, request register, slide-panel filer](../assets/screenshots/26_dpa_privacy/my_requests.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Education panel ŌĆö six-card grid restating every Act 843 right in plain English with an icon. The cards are read-only and exist purely to let the data subject pick the correct request type without consulting a lawyer. The line "We respond within 30 days. The first request in any 12-month period is free of charge." captures the ┬¦22 timeline and the ┬¦24 fee position. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Request register ŌĆö one row per request the subject has filed, with reference, type, submission date, statutory deadline, status badge, and a "Download ZIP" link when the fulfilled bundle is available. ┬Ę ŌØĖ "File Request" slide panel ŌĆö cobalt CTA opens a right-hand panel with a single-select for the right being exercised and a free-text statement (ten character minimum). The Rectification choice reveals an extra "What needs correcting?" textarea; the Objection choice reveals a "Which processing do you object to?" textarea. Both extras map to the matching server-side columns.*

![Public DPA portal ŌĆö submit form, email verification page, track-by-reference](../assets/screenshots/26_dpa_privacy/public_dpa.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Submit form (`/dpa`) ŌĆö name, email, request-type dropdown, and statement textarea. No CAPTCHA in the MVP; the form is throttled to ten POSTs per IP per minute and the request is invisible to the DPO queue until the subject clicks the emailed verification link. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Confirmation page (`/dpa/confirmation`) ŌĆö appears immediately after submit, tells the subject to check their inbox, shows the assigned reference number, and links to the tracker. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Verification result page (`/dpa/verified`) ŌĆö the click target from the emailed magic link. Green check on success (request joins the queue, the thirty-day clock starts now, not at submission), rose icon on failure (token already used, request withdrawn, or fabricated). ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Track page (`/dpa/track`) ŌĆö public endpoint that requires both the reference and the original submission email to look up a request; we never confirm a reference exists when the email doesn't match, so a leaked reference number on its own gives an attacker nothing.*

> The four screenshot files referenced above will be captured in Wave 2 (task W2.26). Until then the build will substitute a "missing image" placeholder ŌĆö that is expected and does not break the build.

## Every button, every action

### DPO Queue (`/admin/privacy`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Overdue** (top right, outlined) | Sets the status filter to `overdue` and re-queries. | `privacy.fulfill` | Single-click jump to the most legally exposed slice of the queue. An auditor checking compliance will hit this button first. |
| **Triage New** (cobalt CTA, top right) | Sets the status filter to `submitted` ŌĆö the queue of unstarted requests. | Same | The DPO's daily morning workflow: see what landed overnight. |
| **Overdue alert banner** | Appears only when `stats.overdue > 0`. Shows the count and links to the same filtered view. | Same | A 30-day SLA breach under Act 843 ┬¦22 is the single highest-stakes failure mode of the module ŌĆö it gets pinned to the top of the page in rose-and-gold gradient until cleared. |
| **SLA ring** | Read-only display of `within_sla_pct` (open requests still within the ┬¦22 window) and `avg_age_days`. Colour-shifts from cobalt (Ōēź90 %) to navy to gold to rose (below 40 %). | Anyone with access to the page | First answer the head of compliance wants on this screen ŌĆö "is the queue healthy?" |
| **Type bar** (per row in the composition panel) | Clicking a bar toggles the `request_type` filter to that type, or back to all if it was already that type. | Same | Lets the DPO pivot from "show me everything" to "show me only Erasure requests this quarter" in one click. |
| **Status dropdown** | Filters the register by `data_subject_requests.status`. Re-queries on change. | Same | The most-used filter ŌĆö typically pinned to `submitted` or `overdue`. |
| **Type dropdown** | Filters the register by `data_subject_requests.request_type`. | Same | Same use as the type bars, accessible as a normal form control for screen readers and keyboards. |
| **Clear** (appears only when a filter is set) | Resets both dropdowns and re-queries. | Same | One-click undo. |
| **Open** (every row, right column) | Navigates to the case file at `/admin/privacy/{id}`. | Same | The whole-row hover state plus a discrete cobalt link ŌĆö same RBAC pattern used everywhere in the app. |
| **Pagination** | Twenty-five rows per page (controller-side), with the standard Inertia paginator. | Same | Keeps the page responsive for institutes that file many requests per quarter. |

> *Notes:* The page hydrates from `PrivacyController::adminIndex`, which assembles seven KPI tiles (`open`, `overdue`, `this_month`, `fulfilled_ytd`, `rejected_ytd`, `within_sla_pct`, `avg_age_days`, plus `total_all_time`) and two distribution maps (`typeBreakdown` and `statusBreakdown`) in a single query pass ŌĆö there is no N+1 between the ring, the bars, and the register. The within-SLA percentage is computed in PHP against the open set so it always agrees with the table you're looking at, not a cached snapshot.

### DPO case file (`/admin/privacy/{id}`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Breadcrumb** (top left) | Links back to the DPO queue. | `privacy.fulfill` | Standard. |
| **Subject statement / Rectification details / Objection purpose** (read-only) | The verbatim text the subject submitted. Rectification details and objection purpose only render when populated. | Same | The lawful basis for any decision on a DPA request is what the subject actually asked for ŌĆö it must be visible exactly as written, not paraphrased. |
| **Acknowledge & assign to me** (only when `status === submitted`) | Posts to `/admin/privacy/{id}/acknowledge`. Sets status to `acknowledged`, assigns the request to the calling user, stamps `acknowledged_at`, and appends an `acknowledged` entry to the audit trail. | Same | Act 843 ┬¦22 contemplates an acknowledgement phase that is distinct from fulfilment. Recording it explicitly is what an auditor needs to see when reconstructing a timeline. |
| **Fulfil (2FA required)** (cobalt, requires a summary Ōēź20 chars) | Posts to `/admin/privacy/{id}/fulfill`. For Access / Portability / Information requests, builds the export bundle, hashes it with SHA-256, and stamps the row with the export path, hash, and generation timestamp. For Erasure requests, runs `ErasureService::erase()` to tombstone PII fields and record the held-back rows. For Rectification / Objection requests, just records the decision summary. In every case, status flips to `fulfilled` and the `DataSubjectRequestFulfilled` event fires. | `privacy.fulfill` for Access/Rectification/Portability/Objection/Information; **`privacy.erase`** for Erasure | Two-factor gate (`2fa:fresh`) makes a stolen session unable to drain personal data or destroy it. Erasure is gated separately from the other rights because tombstoning is irreversible. |
| **Reject** (rose, requires statutory_basis + summary) | Posts to `/admin/privacy/{id}/reject`. Status flips to `rejected`, the rejection basis is stored on the row, and the audit trail records the basis. | `privacy.fulfill` | Act 843 ┬¦27 enumerates statutory exemptions (national security, ongoing criminal investigation, regulated archive, etc.). The basis is **required, minimum five characters** and is shown back to the subject as part of the decision ŌĆö a rejection without a written statutory citation is a ┬¦22 violation in itself. |
| **Outcome block** (read-only, after closure) | Shows decision summary, rejection basis (if rejected), and ŌĆö for fulfilled Access/Portability/Information requests ŌĆö the export-generated timestamp and SHA-256 hash. | Same | Tamper evidence: re-hashing the ZIP at any later date must produce the same digest. Anyone challenging the export pack can verify it byte-for-byte. |
| **Erasure receipt** (for Erasure fulfilments) | Two-column list: "Redacted" (every table + every field tombstoned, plus skill/document delete counts) and "Held back under statutory retention" (every table where rows survived under which Act and until when). | Same | Closing the legal loop. The subject can see exactly what was destroyed and the auditor can see exactly what was preserved and why. |
| **Audit trail** (read-only) | Chronological list of every `appendAuditEntry()` call on the row ŌĆö `submitted`, `acknowledged`, `export_built` (with hash), `erasure_executed` (with tombstone summary), `fulfilled`, `rejected`, `withdrawn_by_subject`, `public_submitted`, `verified_by_token`. | Same | Per-request micro-audit, complementing the global tamper-evident chain in Chapter 24. The two stores are independent ŌĆö corrupting one does not corrupt the other. |

> *Notes:* The case-file page hydrates from `PrivacyController::adminShow`, which eager-loads `subject`, `assignee`, and `decider`. The `DataSubjectRequestResource` adds three computed booleans on the way out (`has_export`, `is_overdue`, and the `can` array of policy decisions) so the Vue page never has to know about policy logic. The verification_token is never sent to the client ŌĆö it is in the model's `$hidden` array ŌĆö so even if a DPO accidentally shared a screenshot of the JSON payload, the magic-link secret would not leak.

### My Privacy Requests (`/privacy`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **File Request** (cobalt CTA, top right) | Opens the right-hand slide panel for filing a new request. | Any authenticated user | One-click entry to the most-used action on the page. |
| **Request-type select** (inside the panel) | Six options ŌĆö Access, Rectification, Erasure, Portability, Objection, Information. The Rectification choice reveals a "What needs correcting?" textarea; the Objection choice reveals a "Which processing do you object to?" textarea. | Same | Plain-English labels for each Act 843 right, with conditional follow-up fields so the subject doesn't see questions they don't need to answer. |
| **Statement** (textarea, required, min 10 chars, max 5000) | Free-text description of the request. | Same | The subject's own words are the legal anchor for the request ŌĆö the controller stores them verbatim in `subject_statement`. |
| **Submit request** | Posts to `/privacy`. The service creates a `data_subject_requests` row with a `DSR-YYYY-NNNNN` reference, status `submitted`, target completion thirty days hence, and an `audit_trail` entry. The `DataSubjectRequestSubmitted` event fires (see below). The flash toast shows the reference number. | Same | Single-step file ŌĆö no email confirmation needed for authenticated users because identity is already established. |
| **Withdraw** (per-row, only when not terminal) | After a JS confirm, posts to `/privacy/{id}/withdraw`. Status flips to `withdrawn`, `completed_at` is stamped, and the audit trail records `withdrawn_by_subject`. | The subject (and only the subject ŌĆö `DataSubjectRequestPolicy::withdraw` enforces this server-side) | A subject changing their mind is the only legitimate path to a `withdrawn` status. The DPO cannot withdraw on the subject's behalf ŌĆö they must reject with a statutory basis instead. |
| **Download ZIP** (per-row, only when `export_path` is set) | Streams the export bundle via `/privacy/{id}/download`. Authorisation is `DataSubjectRequestPolicy::downloadExport` ŌĆö only the subject themselves can download via this URL, even the DPO must regenerate to get a fresh copy. | The subject | Lets the data subject pick up their bundle without emailing it. The download is a 404 if the file no longer exists on disk (the storage retention for export ZIPs is a known gap ŌĆö see *What's planned next*). |
| **Education panel** (top of page) | Six cards listing the six Act 843 rights with one-sentence explanations. | Same | Plain-English advocate ŌĆö most data subjects have never heard of ┬¦17 and don't know what they're entitled to. The cards exist purely to surface that. |
| **Target by** (per row) | Renders the statutory deadline, with days remaining in subdued grey, or "overdue" in rose if past. | Same | Same legal anchor as the DPO sees on their queue ŌĆö both sides of the table see the same clock. |

> *Notes:* The page caps at the most-recent fifty requests with no pagination by design ŌĆö for the median data subject this is a list of one to three rows, and the cap exists to keep the screen from blowing up if someone files repeatedly. The `requests` prop is sent as a flat resolved collection (not a paginator-shaped object) because the page never needed pagination; see the comment in `PrivacyController::myRequests`.

### Public DPA portal (`/dpa`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full name + Email** | Identifies the subject. Validated as `required`, `email`, max 255 chars, max 120 for name. | Anyone | Public submissions don't have a `subject_user_id` ŌĆö these two columns plus the verification token are how we link the request back to a real person. |
| **Request type** | Same six-option enum as the authenticated form. | Anyone | Same as above. |
| **Statement** (textarea, min 10 chars, max 5000) | The subject's request in their own words. | Anyone | Same legal anchor. |
| **Submit request** | Posts to `/dpa`, throttled to ten requests per IP per minute. The service writes a row with status `pending_verification`, generates a 64-char hex `verification_token`, and emails the subject a magic-link `DpaVerificationLink` notification. The page redirects to the confirmation screen with the reference in the query string. | Anyone | The verification step is what stops the form from becoming a denial-of-service vector against the DPO queue ŌĆö until the subject clicks the link, the request is invisible to the queue and the ┬¦22 clock has not started. |
| **Verification link** (in the email) | Visiting `/dpa/verify?token={hex}` flips the matching request from `pending_verification` to `submitted`, stamps `verified_at`, **resets the thirty-day SLA clock to now**, and dispatches the `DataSubjectRequestSubmitted` event. The Vue page shows a green confirmation. | Anyone with the token | The SLA reset is deliberate ŌĆö Act 843 ┬¦22 starts the clock when the controller becomes obliged to act, which in our model is verification. Without this reset, a slow inbox would burn the subject's response window. |
| **Track form** (`/dpa/track`) | Looks up a request by **both** reference and email. Mismatched email returns `not_found` without revealing whether the reference exists. | Anyone | Lets a subject without an account watch progress. The dual-key lookup keeps a stray reference number from leaking case details. |

> *Notes:* The whole public lane is wrapped in `throttle:10,1` at the route level ŌĆö ten requests per minute per IP across submit, verify, track, and the confirmation page combined. The Notification is sent via the anonymous-notifiable pattern (`Notification::route('mail', $email)->notify(...)`) because public submitters have no User row to attach a `notifiable` to. The email contains both the verification link and the track-by-reference URL, so the subject has everything they need in the inbox.

## The data behind it

A `data_subject_requests` row is one piece of legal correspondence ŌĆö every column exists to answer a specific Act 843 question:

- **Identity of the subject.** Either `subject_user_id` (authenticated submission) **or** `subject_email` + `subject_full_name` + `verification_token` (public submission). The two paths cannot mix on the same row.
- **What they want.** `request_type` is one of six enum values (`access`, `rectification`, `erasure`, `portability`, `objection`, `information`) and `subject_statement` is the verbatim text. `rectification_details` and `objection_purpose` are the type-specific extras.
- **Where in the lifecycle.** `status` walks through ten possible values ŌĆö `pending_verification` (public-only, pre-magic-link), `submitted`, `acknowledged`, `in_review`, `pending_subject`, `fulfilled`, `partially_fulfilled`, `rejected`, `withdrawn`, `overdue` ŌĆö with `isTerminal()` carving out the last four as final.
- **Timeline.** `submitted_at`, `target_completion_date` (always submission + 30 days, reset on public verification), `acknowledged_at`, `completed_at`. `daysRemaining()` and `isOverdue()` are computed on the model so the API and the UI agree.
- **Who handled it.** `assigned_to` (the DPO once acknowledged), `decided_by` (the DPO who fulfilled or rejected).
- **What was decided.** `decision_summary` (visible to the subject, minimum twenty chars), `rejection_basis` (required on reject, minimum five chars, a statutory citation).
- **Tamper-evident artefacts.** `export_path` (filesystem path of the ZIP), `export_sha256` (the hash baked into the manifest), `export_generated_at`. The `tombstone_log` JSON, written only for Erasure fulfilments, lists every redacted field and every held-back row with the statute that kept it.
