- **Per-request audit.** `audit_trail` is a JSON array appended via `DataSubjectRequest::appendAuditEntry()` on every state change ŌĆö `submitted`, `acknowledged`, `export_built`, `erasure_executed`, `fulfilled`, `rejected`, `withdrawn_by_subject`, `public_submitted`, `verified_by_token`. This is the per-row micro-audit; it complements the global tamper-evident chain (Chapter 24) and does not depend on it.

The reference number `DSR-YYYY-NNNNN` is allocated by `DataSubjectRequestService::nextReference()` ŌĆö a `count() + 1` per year (a known concurrency gap; the system-wide `SequenceService` pattern documented elsewhere in the audit pack is not yet wired here). Soft delete is enabled on the table but is not used by the lifecycle ŌĆö a withdrawn or rejected request stays visible with its terminal status; we never silently disappear correspondence.

What every reader of the queue needs to keep in mind:

- **Pending-verification rows are invisible to the queue.** `markOverdueRequests()` ignores them and so does the default DPO view, so spam from the public form never reaches the auditor.
- **The thirty-day clock starts at verification, not at submission, for public requests.** This is recorded explicitly in `verifyPublic()` ŌĆö the `submitted_at` column is rewritten on the verify step.
- **Authenticated submissions skip verification.** They start at `submitted` and the clock runs from `submitted_at` immediately, because identity is already established by the login session.
- **The verification token is hidden from the model.** It is in `$hidden`, so any JSON response (including the DPO's own admin show page) never carries it. Anyone with the token can flip a `pending_verification` row to `submitted` ŌĆö that is by design but it is also the only thing the token can do.
- **Erasure cannot delete what the law requires us to keep.** `ErasureService` tombstones identifying fields on the User and Employee rows, deletes employee skills and avatar paths outright, and counts every row still inside a statutory hold (six years for payroll under Income Tax Act 2015 ┬¦43, seven years for SSNIT under Pensions Act 2008 ┬¦92, indefinitely for the audit chain). The held-back rows still exist but no longer identify the data subject because the joining `users.name`, `users.email`, and `employees.*` columns have been stamped with `[ERASED YYYY-MM-DD ┬Ę DSR-XXXX-NNNNN]`.

## What an export looks like

A fulfilled Access, Portability, or Information request produces a ZIP at `storage/app/dsr-exports/{reference}.zip`. Inside:

- **`00-account.json`** ŌĆö the User row (id, name, email, staff_id, role, 2FA enrolment flag, WhatsApp phone, notification preferences, created_at).
- **`01-employee.json`** ŌĆö the Employee row (employee number, department, position, hire date, status, gender, DOB, Ghana Card PIN, SSNIT, TIN, address, phone, emergency contact block, bank block, mobile money block, salary, current grade, current step).
- **`02-leave_requests.json`** ŌĆö every leave request the employee filed.
- **`03-payroll_lines.json`** ŌĆö every payroll line, with period reference, basic, gross, PAYE, SSNIT tier 1, Tier 2, net, status.
- **`04-attendance.json`** ŌĆö every attendance summary row (date, status, first in, last out, hours worked).
- **`05-performance_reviews.json`** ŌĆö every performance review with cycle id, type, overall rating, status, submitted-at, and comments.
- **`06-loan_accounts.json`** ŌĆö every loan account.
- **`07-skills.json`** ŌĆö every skill row.
- **`08-identity_verifications.json`** ŌĆö every Ghana Card check (provider, status, verified-at, expires-at, plus the last character of the card number only).
- **`09-tickets_filed.json`** / **`10-complaints_filed.json`** / **`11-job_applications.json`** ŌĆö anything the subject submitted into the system.
- **`12-audit_log_entries.json`** ŌĆö the most-recent five thousand audit-log entries where the subject was the actor (capped to keep the export reasonable).
- **A CSV companion for every JSON** ŌĆö the same data flattened to comma-separated values so the subject can open it in Excel, Numbers, or LibreOffice without help.
- **`MANIFEST.md`** ŌĆö a Markdown cover sheet listing every file with a per-file SHA-256 hash and byte count. It also lists three categories of statutory exclusions: whistleblower content (Act 720 ┬¦10 anonymity supersedes Act 843), 2FA secrets and recovery codes (excluded because disclosing them would weaken the subject's own account security), and other users' data the subject may have viewed (because the export is data **about** the subject, not data the subject has seen).

The ZIP's own SHA-256 hash is computed in `DataSubjectExportBuilder::buildFor()` and stored on the `data_subject_requests` row, so anyone questioning the bundle three years later can re-hash the file and verify it byte-for-byte.

## Consent ŌĆö what's tracked, what's not

The MVP tracks consent in exactly one place: **WhatsApp messaging**. Meta requires explicit opt-in before a phone number can receive template messages, so `users.whatsapp_consent_at` exists as the timestamp of opt-in, paired with `users.whatsapp_phone` and a `notification_channels` JSON map keyed by channel slug. Slack, email, and in-app are governed by the same JSON map, but the implicit-consent posture for email (it is the employee's contractual contact channel) means there is no per-channel timestamp.

There is no general-purpose consent ledger. Performance-review participation, leave routing, payroll processing, biometric attendance, and Ghana Card verification all run on the **employment-contract lawful basis** under Act 843 ┬¦20(1)(b) ŌĆö consent is not required and is not tracked. This is the correct posture for an internal HRMS, but it is also why the system has no answer to "withdraw consent for X processing"; the closest workflow is an **Objection** request via `/privacy`, which the DPO then reviews against the contractual basis.

Cookie / browser-tracking consent is **not present** in the MVP. There is no analytics cookie banner, no tracking pixel, and no third-party advertising tag ŌĆö the app is a single-tenant institute deployment, not a public marketing site ŌĆö so the European-style cookie banner has no statutory hook to require it. This is documented here as a gap rather than a feature, because some institutes will want a consent banner when they deploy to a `.gov.gh` subdomain that also serves public content (the recruitment portal at `/careers`, the whistleblower portal at `/whistleblower`, the public DPA portal at `/dpa`).

## How it talks to other modules

- **`DataSubjectRequestSubmitted` event** ŌåÆ fires on every authenticated submission and on every successful public verification. Today it has no listeners wired by default; the audit log captures the transition via the per-row `audit_trail`, and the DPO sees the row in their queue on next page load. Hook point for future notifications to the assigned DPO is in place.
- **`DataSubjectRequestFulfilled` event** ŌåÆ fires on fulfilment with the fresh model attached. Same posture ŌĆö the row records the fulfilment in its own audit trail, and the event is available for downstream listeners (e.g. a future "your data is ready" notification).
- **Employees (Chapter 3)** ŌåÆ the export builder reads every joined table (leave, payroll, attendance, performance, identity, skills) from the Employee record. Erasure tombstones rotate around the same row, redacting personal identifiers while keeping employment numbers needed for statutory retention.
- **Audit Logs (Chapter 24)** ŌåÆ every state transition on a DSR row also writes an entry to the global tamper-evident chain via the standard `AuditTrail` middleware. The per-request `audit_trail` JSON is the local-view supplement; the chain is the legally significant ledger.
- **Identity (Chapter 25)** ŌåÆ `IdentityVerification` rows for the subject's Ghana Card are listed in `08-identity_verifications.json` and are explicitly enumerated in the erasure held-back log under Pensions Act ┬¦92's seven-year retention.
- **Whistleblower (Act 720, separate chapter)** ŌåÆ content where the subject is named as a respondent (not as the submitter) is excluded from the export and noted on the manifest. The exclusion is statutory, not configurable.
- **2FA (Chapter 25 / authentication chapter)** ŌåÆ the fulfil action is behind `2fa:fresh`, requiring a fresh second-factor challenge within the rolling window used by `payroll.reverse`, `loans.disburse`, and `ar_invoices.write_off`. Erasure additionally requires the `privacy.erase` permission, which is held only by super_admin in the seeded matrix.
- **Profile portal (Chapter 16)** ŌåÆ the in-app entry to `/privacy/my`. The portal is the only place a non-super_admin currently sees the link, because the sidebar entry exists only for the DPO admin view.

## Standards touchpoints

- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦┬¦17ŌĆō22 ŌĆö data-subject rights.** Every enum case in `DataSubjectRequestType` maps to a numbered section: ┬¦17 access, ┬¦18 rectification, ┬¦19 erasure, ┬¦20 objection, ┬¦21 portability, ┬¦22 information. The thirty-day SLA constant in `DataSubjectRequestService::SLA_DAYS` is the ┬¦22 timeline. The "first request in any 12-month period is free of charge" text on the My Privacy page is the ┬¦24 fee posture. The rejection workflow requires a statutory citation in `rejection_basis`; the seeded example on the case-file UI is "Act 843 ┬¦27(e) ŌĆö public-interest archive", one of the named exemptions.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦28 ŌĆö record of processing.** The `audit_trail` JSON on every DSR row is itself a record-of-processing artefact for the privacy module, supplementing the global audit chain.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦39 ŌĆö sensitive personal data.** Ghana Card numbers, SSNIT numbers, TINs, biometric attendance records, and bank details are exported in full to the subject (data **about** them) but are masked to the last four digits everywhere else in the UI ŌĆö see Chapter 3's Ghana Compliance card and Chapter 25's biometric notes. Salary is gated behind `employees.view_salary` and is omitted from API responses entirely for users without the permission; it **is** included in the subject's own export because the subject is entitled to know what we pay them.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦40 ŌĆö right to erasure** (referenced from Chapter 3). `ErasureService` is the redaction workflow. Statutory holds are reported on the `tombstone_log`. Soft delete on the Employees table is **not** erasure ŌĆö clicking Delete only removes a row from active lists; the ┬¦40 workflow requires the formal Erasure request through the Privacy module.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦44 ŌĆö security-breach notification.** Act 843 ┬¦44 obliges the controller to notify the Data Protection Commission of any security compromise affecting personal data. **There is no breach-notification workflow in the MVP.** The audit chain (Chapter 24) detects tamper attempts on the audit log and notifies super_admins by mail, which is a precursor to a breach-response capability but is not itself a ┬¦44 notification record. This is the largest single gap in the privacy module and is on the Phase 4 roadmap below.
- **Cross-border transfer controls (Act 843 ┬¦47).** The MVP runs in a single jurisdiction; there is no cross-border data-transfer workflow because there is no place to transfer to. When the institute deploys cloud storage outside Ghana (Microsoft 365 EU regions, Google Drive multi-region), the integration metadata in `integrations` records the provider region but does not yet capture an adequacy-decision flag ŌĆö that is also Phase 4 work.
- **Data Protection Commission registration evidence.** The DPC operates a controller-registration register under Act 843 ┬¦46. **The MVP does not carry any DPC registration metadata in code** ŌĆö no `dpc_registration_number`, no `dpc_renewal_date`, no DPO contact block in the public-facing footer beyond what is hard-coded in Vue. An organisation deploying CIHRMS will need to display its DPC certificate separately; the codebase has no opinion about where. This is a known documentation rather than a code gap.
- **GDPR parity (EU 2016/679).** The six rights mirror Articles 15 (access), 16 (rectification), 17 (erasure), 18 (restriction ŌĆö closest analogue is our Objection), 20 (portability), and 21 (object to processing). The thirty-day SLA matches Article 12(3). The records-of-processing audit trail satisfies Article 30. There is no Data Protection Impact Assessment workflow (Article 35) in the MVP. There is no breach-notification workflow (Article 33's seventy-two-hour clock), tracking the same gap as Act 843 ┬¦44 above.
- **Cybersecurity Act, 2020 (Act 1038).** The Cyber Security Authority requires significant security incidents to be notified ŌĆö the same gap as above applies. The audit-chain verification command (`audit:verify-chain --notify`, scheduled daily at 03:00) is the closest existing capability.
- **ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.18.1 ŌĆö compliance with legal requirements.** The privacy module is the principal artefact for A.18.1.4 ("privacy and protection of personally identifiable information"). The per-request audit trail, the tamper-evident export hashes, and the statutory-hold reporting are the controls. The forward chapter on standards (Chapter 44) maps every control to a section here.

Forward to Chapter 44 for the full standards-benchmark matrix.

## What's planned next

Five things are on the Phase 4 roadmap (see the gap analysis and Chapter 46):

1. **Breach-notification workflow.** A `breach_incidents` table + a `BreachReport` lifecycle (`detected ŌåÆ triaged ŌåÆ notified_dpc ŌåÆ resolved`) with a seventy-two-hour SLA target, the equivalent of the existing DPO queue but for ┬¦44 / GDPR Art. 33 events. The chain-verification job already runs daily; it will gain a "raise a draft breach incident" outcome when a mismatch is detected.
2. **DPC registration metadata in code.** A small `compliance_registrations` table for the institute's DPC certificate number, renewal date, and DPO contact block, surfaced in the public site footer and the case-file header so subjects can verify they are dealing with a registered controller. Once landed, this also feeds the Standards chapter's evidence pack.
3. **Schedule the nightly overdue sweep.** `DataSubjectRequestService::markOverdueRequests()` is implemented and tested, but the call is **not yet wired into `routes/console.php`** ŌĆö today an open request only flips to `overdue` when someone visits the DPO queue and the `is_overdue()` predicate runs in-resource. A `Schedule::call` entry at 00:10 daily will close that gap. (The function returns the row count so the same scheduler can also gold-line an alert when an overdue spike happens.)
4. **Retention policies + auto-purge.** Today no policy auto-purges anything ŌĆö soft-deleted employees, applicants who withdrew, terminated users' tokens, expired payment intents (with one finance-side exception that runs nightly) all accumulate indefinitely. A `data_retention_policies` table keyed by `table_name + statute + retention_years` with a console command `privacy:purge-expired` will give the DPO a daily redaction pass for everything outside a statutory hold. Erasure exports' own ZIP files also need a TTL ŌĆö at present they live in `storage/app/dsr-exports/{reference}.zip` forever.
5. **Subject-side sidebar nav.** Today the `/privacy/my` page has no sidebar entry ŌĆö subjects reach it through the Profile portal (Chapter 16) or by direct URL. A "My Privacy" tile in the user-menu dropdown will close the discoverability gap so every employee knows the workflow exists, not just the ones who already know to look.

Phase 4 work brings the privacy module from compliant-on-the-rights-themselves to compliant-end-to-end, including the institute's own ┬¦44 / ┬¦46 obligations as a controller.

<!-- ===== 27_whistleblower.md ===== -->

# Chapter 27 ŌĆö Whistleblower (Confidential Disclosure Channel)

> *In one paragraph.* This is the high-severity, statutorily-protected reporting channel that CIHRMS runs under the **Whistleblower Act 2006 (Act 720)** of Ghana. Anyone ŌĆö staff, contractor, vendor, or a member of the public ŌĆö can disclose corruption, fraud, harassment, mismanagement, or other improper conduct without first creating an account, without giving their name, and without leaving an identity trail on the report row. The submitter receives a single one-time **tracking code**; the system stores only the SHA-256 hash of that code, so even a database administrator cannot recover it. On the other side a small, deliberately segregated group of investigators triages the case, runs interviews and document reviews, and either substantiates it, dismisses it, or refers it onward to **CHRAJ**, the **Auditor-General**, or the **Police**. The whole flow ŌĆö submission, triage, investigation actions, two-way submitter messaging, closure ŌĆö is recorded in append-only `whistleblower_actions` rows that double as the case's tamper-evident audit timeline. This channel is intentionally distinct from the day-to-day Complaints module in Chapter 12: Complaints handle workplace grievances through HR; Whistleblower handles the kinds of allegations that, by law, must bypass HR entirely.

## Where to find it

- **Public entry point:** `/whistleblower` ŌĆö a stripped-down, **un-authenticated** page reachable without logging in. It carries no sidebar, no app shell, no logged-in chrome; it deliberately looks unlike the rest of CIHRMS so a worried submitter knows they are on the privacy-hardened lane. A companion `/whistleblower/track` page accepts the tracking code and shows case status plus the message thread with the investigator.
- **Admin entry point:** Sidebar ŌåÆ **System** group ŌåÆ **Whistleblower** (flag icon, rose accent on the masthead). Routed at `/admin/whistleblower`. The System group is reachable to the holders of `whistleblower.investigate`, `whistleblower.view_all`, `whistleblower.manage`, or any other system-level permission such as `users.manage` / `privacy.fulfill`.
- **Roles that see it:**
    - **auditor** ŌĆö by seed the auditor role holds both `whistleblower.view_all` (read-only oversight lane) and `whistleblower.investigate` (segregated investigator). This is the default investigator profile out of the box because auditor sits outside the HR chain.
    - **super_admin** and **ceo** ŌĆö receive the wildcard `*` permission and can therefore see the dashboard, but the policy comment makes clear that the *intent* is that even super-admin should hold `whistleblower.manage` explicitly to read case content (no `before()` shortcut in `WhistleblowerReportPolicy`).
    - **hr_admin** ŌĆö **deliberately not granted any whistleblower permission** by the role seeder. The whole point of Act 720 is that HR must not be in the routing path, because retaliation pressure typically flows through HR. The audit trail of permission seeding records this absence.
    - **Everyone else (employee, manager, dept_head, finance_officer, it_support)** ŌĆö can submit through the public page like anyone else but never see the investigator dashboard.
- **Related modules:** **Complaints (Ch 12)** ŌĆö sibling channel for everyday workplace issues; the two are intentionally distinct tables, distinct policies, distinct permissions. **Audit Logs (Ch 24)** ŌĆö every investigator action is mirrored into the per-case `whistleblower_actions` timeline. **Identity (Ch 25)** ŌĆö the anonymity guarantees described below are partly cross-checked there. **DPA (Ch 26)** ŌĆö disclosure content counts as sensitive personal data under DPA Act 843 ┬¦39 and is therefore encrypted at rest. **Auditor-General Report Pack (Ch 24)** ŌĆö the AG pack pulls **counts only** from this module; the body of every case stays inside the investigator lane.

## The screens

![Public submission form ŌĆö confidential disclosure landing page](../assets/screenshots/27_whistleblower/submit.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Minimal header ŌĆö a navy shield, the CIHRM Ghana mark, and a single link to "Track an existing case". No sidebar, no logged-in chrome, no analytics beacons; the page is deliberately reduced to its function. ┬Ę ŌØĘ "Your protection under the law" panel ŌĆö cites Act 720 by name, names the criminal offence of retaliation, and explicitly tells the submitter that anonymous submissions carry no name, IP, or contact, and that they will receive a tracking code. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Anonymity toggle ŌĆö a prominent checkbox defaulted to **anonymous**; opting out reveals a single contact field (encrypted at rest, visible only to the investigator). ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Category select fed by the `WhistleblowerCategory` enum (corruption, fraud, harassment, discrimination, retaliation, safety, environmental, conflict of interest, mismanagement, criminal offence, other). ┬Ę ŌØ║ Description textarea with a live character counter and the words "encrypted" stamped on the count itself.*

![Confirmation screen ŌĆö tracking code shown ONCE](../assets/screenshots/27_whistleblower/confirmation.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ "Your disclosure has been received" with the case number in monospace (e.g. `WB-2026-00001`). ┬Ę ŌØĘ The plaintext tracking code in a large `XXXX-XXXX-XXXX` block with a one-click "Copy" button ŌĆö this is the only moment the code is on screen; refreshing the page or coming back later will not bring it back. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Amber save-it-now warning ŌĆö the system explains in plain words that the code is not recoverable. If the submitter loses it, the case becomes unreachable from their side (by design).*

![Track-a-case form ŌĆö tracking-code lookup](../assets/screenshots/27_whistleblower/track.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Single input ŌĆö the tracking code, monospace, centred, auto-uppercased. Service-side normalisation strips spaces and dashes before hashing, so `WK4F-XR9P-2QHM` and `WK4FXR9P2QHM` map to the same row. ┬Ę ŌØĘ A line that reads "Your identity is never linked to the code" ŌĆö this is the literal database truth: the row has no `user_id`, no IP, no user agent, no session id. ┬Ę ŌØĖ A small "Make a new disclosure" link back to `/whistleblower` for someone who came in to follow up but realised they need to file a fresh case.*

![Submitter status screen ŌĆö redacted view of the case](../assets/screenshots/27_whistleblower/status.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Case header ŌĆö case number, status pill (`Submitted`, `Triaged`, `Investigating`, `Evidence Gathering`, `Closed ŌĆö Substantiated/Unsubstantiated/Referred`, or `Withdrawn`), category label, received/triaged/closed dates. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Closure-summary panel ŌĆö only present when the case is closed; this is the **only** investigator-authored content the submitter ever sees beyond chat messages. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Secure thread ŌĆö investigator messages are rendered on a tinted background, the submitter's on plain. The reply form appears only while the case is still in an open status. The submitter's reply re-authenticates by re-supplying the tracking code; they do not stay logged in via session.*

![Investigator dashboard ŌĆö Whistleblower Office register](../assets/screenshots/27_whistleblower/admin_index.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Masthead stamp ŌĆö "Segregated investigation ┬Ę Act 720" in rose, the live-open count in a rose pill, and the title "Whistleblower Office". ┬Ę ŌØĘ Four stat tiles ŌĆö Open Register, Awaiting Triage, Critical Open, Closed YTD. ┬Ę ŌØĖ SLA / urgency banner ŌĆö appears when there is any critical-open case or any submission older than three days that has not been triaged; jumps the investigator straight to a filtered view. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Severity-composition bars and top-categories panel ŌĆö both clickable filter shortcuts; the severity panel also surfaces the **anonymous filings %** ribbon (a confidence proxy ŌĆö high anonymous % means the channel is being trusted). ┬Ę ŌØ║ Register table ŌĆö case number, one-line summary, category icon, severity pill, status pill, received date with age in days, assigned investigator initials (or "Unassigned" in amber).*

![Case detail ŌĆö investigator workspace with triage, actions, thread, evidence](../assets/screenshots/27_whistleblower/admin_show.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Triage panel ŌĆö only shown while the case is still in `submitted` status; severity selector, "assign to" dropdown (defaults to "Myself"), optional notes, **2FA-fresh required by middleware** before the request lands. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Tab bar ŌĆö Case detail ┬Ę Actions ┬Ę Submitter thread ┬Ę Evidence. Case detail renders the decrypted summary, description, desired outcome, incident date/location, named subjects. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Log-action panel ŌĆö picks an `InvestigationActionType` (Interview, Document review, Site visit, Evidence added, Finding recorded, **Referred to CHRAJ**, **Referred to Auditor-General**, **Referred to Police**), optionally changes status, and on a "closed_*" choice prompts for a closure summary. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Submitter thread ŌĆö every outbound message is logged as an action; every inbound message arrives without any user identity attached.*

> The six screenshot files referenced above will be captured in Wave 1 (task W1.27). Until then the build will substitute a "missing image" placeholder ŌĆö that is expected and does not break the build.

## Every button, every action

### Public submission form (`/whistleblower`)

| Field / control | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **"Track an existing case ŌåÆ"** (top-right link) | Navigates to `/whistleblower/track`. | Anyone, no auth | The page-of-entry for follow-up ŌĆö keeps it one click from the submission page. |
| **Anonymity toggle** (defaults to ON) | When ON, the server stores `is_anonymous = true` and forces `submitter_user_id` to NULL even if the request came from a logged-in browser. When OFF, the contact field becomes required. | Anyone | The single most-important control on the page ŌĆö and the most consequential default in the whole module. |
| **Contact (email or phone)** | Stored encrypted via the Laravel `encrypted` cast on `submitter_contact`. Visible only to the investigator viewing the case. | Required when anonymity is OFF; rejected otherwise | A genuine "let me follow up" lane for submitters who want a direct line, without that being the default behaviour. |
| **Category select** | Required. Backed by the `WhistleblowerCategory` enum (11 values). | Anyone | Drives the dashboard's category composition view and the AG pack's aggregate counts. |
| **Short title** | Required, max 255 chars. **Not encrypted** ŌĆö this is the only field used in the investigator register listing. | Anyone | Investigators need to be able to scan a table of cases without decrypting hundreds of rows on every page load. The advice on the placeholder is "a one-line summary the investigator will see in the dashboard". |
| **Description** | Required, 30ŌĆō20,000 characters. Encrypted at rest. The live counter reads "N / 20000 ŌĆö encrypted" to make the guarantee visible while typing. | Anyone | The substance of the disclosure. The 30-char floor stops accidental empty submissions; the 20k ceiling protects the encryption envelope. |
| **Incident date** | Optional, must be on or before today. | Anyone | Helps the investigator triangulate. |
| **Incident location** | Optional, encrypted, max 500 chars. | Anyone | "Office, department, location" ŌĆö same encryption envelope as description. |
| **People involved (subjects)** | Up to 10 rows of (label, role-in-incident). `subject_label` and `role_context` are encrypted; an optional `linked_employee_id` can pin the subject to a known Employee record. | Anyone | Investigators need to know who. The encrypted-by-default behaviour means even the admin DB user can't pull a list of "people named in whistleblower cases" with a raw query. |
| **Desired outcome** | Optional, max 5,000 chars, encrypted. | Anyone | Lets the submitter frame what resolution would look like ŌĆö useful especially when the case is about retaliation. |
| **Attach evidence** | Up to 10 files, 10 MB each. Accepts `pdf, jpg, jpeg, png, docx, xlsx, txt, mp3, mp4`. Stored on the **`local`** disk under `whistleblower/{report_id}/...` ŌĆö never on the public disk. The `uploaded_by` is NULL on anonymous submissions. | Anyone | Documentary evidence is half the point of a serious disclosure. Local-disk storage means the files are not web-served; a separate signed-URL download flow is the planned next step (see "What's planned next"). |
| **Submit disclosure** (cobalt CTA) | Posts to `POST /whistleblower`, rate-limited at **6 requests per IP per minute** by the route group's `throttle:6,1` middleware. | Anyone | The whole flow's commit. Six per minute is generous for a real submitter and tight enough to defeat trivial flood attacks. |

> *Notes:* The route group carries **no CAPTCHA** in the MVP ŌĆö the 6/min throttle is the only abuse defence. Adding hCaptcha or a Ghana-friendly alternative is on the roadmap (see "What's planned next"). The submission service runs inside a `DB::transaction` so an evidence-upload failure rolls back the report row, the subject rows, and any partial evidence rows together ŌĆö there are no orphan disclosures.

### Confirmation page (`/whistleblower/confirmation`)

| Element | Behaviour | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Case number** | Displayed in monospace as `WB-YYYY-NNNNN`. Computed by `WhistleblowerSubmissionService::nextCaseNumber()` from a per-year count. | The case number is fine to share ŌĆö it carries no PII and is what the submitter will quote if they later phone or email a regulator. |
| **Tracking code** | Displayed once, in a copy-to-clipboard widget, in 12-char grouped form (`XXXX-XXXX-XXXX`). Format: Crockford-style base32 (no ambiguous I/L/O/U/0/1 ŌĆö i.e. you can write it down without misreading it). Source of entropy: `random_int` over a 29-character alphabet, 12 characters ŌåÆ ~58 bits. | The plaintext value is held only in the **session flash** for the redirect; nothing logs it, nothing persists it. Reloading the page after the flash key has been consumed shows an empty value. |
| **Amber "save your code" notice** | Explains in plain English that the code cannot be recovered. | The system genuinely cannot recover it ŌĆö only the SHA-256 hash exists in `tracking_token_hash`. This is the same one-way guarantee a properly-built password store gives. |
| **"Track this case ŌåÆ"** link | Navigates to `/whistleblower/track`. | The submitter is encouraged to bookmark this page itself. |

### Track lookup (`/whistleblower/track`)

| Field / control | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tracking code input** | Required, 8ŌĆō32 chars. The service normalises by uppercasing and stripping whitespace and dashes before hashing, so `wk4f xr9p 2qhm` and `WK4F-XR9P-2QHM` are equivalent. | Anyone with a valid code | Tolerant of how people write codes down on a piece of paper. |
| **Check status** (cobalt CTA) | Posts to `POST /whistleblower/track`, which calls `WhistleblowerReport::findByTrackingCode()` ŌĆö a single SHA-256-keyed lookup. | Anyone with a valid code | Constant-work lookup (no fuzzy match). Wrong codes return the same form with a generic error; the system never reveals whether a code was once valid. |
| **Make a new disclosure** link | Back to the submission form. | Anyone | One-click escape if someone landed here by mistake. |

### Submitter status screen (`/whistleblower/track` POST result)

| Element | Behaviour | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Case header** | Case number, status pill, category label, received/triaged/closed dates. | Driven by `TrackingStatusResource` ŌĆö a deliberately *thin* JSON shape that omits investigator names, internal IDs, severity, and any other-party PII. The submitter sees the case the investigator has, not the investigator. |
| **Closure summary panel** | Shown only when the case is in a closed status; carries the investigator's plain-English summary. | This is the one place the investigator can address the submitter at scale; encrypted at rest like everything else. |
| **Message thread** | Inbound messages (from the submitter) on a plain row; outbound messages (from the investigator) on a tinted row. No names are attached on either side in the submitter's view. | The thread is keyed by `report_id`; the submitter proves authorship by knowing the tracking code, not by holding a session. |
| **Reply form** | Visible only while the case is in one of the open statuses (`submitted / triaged / investigating / evidence_gathering`). Posts to `POST /whistleblower/track/reply` with the tracking code re-supplied in the request body. | The reply form **does not store a `posted_by` user_id** ŌĆö even if a logged-in employee is on the same browser, the message is stored with `posted_by = NULL` to preserve anonymity. |

### Investigator dashboard (`/admin/whistleblower`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Register / Awaiting Triage / Critical Open / Closed YTD** stat tiles | Live counts. "Open" excludes the four closed/withdrawn statuses; "Awaiting Triage" is anything still in `submitted`; "Critical Open" is `severity = critical` + `status open`; "Closed YTD" closes within the current year. | `whistleblower.investigate` or `whistleblower.view_all` | The four numbers a triage lead checks first thing every morning. |
| **SLA / urgency banner** | Appears when either `critical_open > 0` or `overdue_triage > 0` (anything still in `submitted` after 3 days). A button jumps to a pre-filtered register. | Same | The whistleblower channel cannot afford a "we forgot to look at it" failure mode. |
| **Severity composition** | Horizontal bar chart over Critical / High / Medium / Low; clicking a bar filters the register. Includes the "Anonymous filings %" ribbon below. | Same | The composition is the channel's health signal ŌĆö too few critical opens may mean a real issue; too many anonymous filings means trust is working. |
| **Top categories** | Card grid of the six most-reported categories with colour-coded icons (e.g. red for corruption, amber for safety, magenta for harassment, navy for procurement). Clicking a category filters the register. | Same | Patterns matter ŌĆö three Mismanagement cases in one quarter is worth a closer look. |
| **Filter strip** (Status / Severity / Category / Clear) | Query-string-backed filters that re-fire the listing immediately. | Same | Standard register affordances. |
| **Register row click ŌåÆ "Open ŌåÆ"** | Navigates to `/admin/whistleblower/{report}`. | An investigator can view a case if they hold `whistleblower.manage` or `whistleblower.view_all`; otherwise only if `assigned_investigator_id === user.id`. | Segregation of duty: investigators see their cases, not each other's. |
| **Anonymous-filing flag** (per row) | A small "Anonymous filing" pill under the case title plus an eye-off icon on the row avatar tile. | Same | The investigator should *know* whether they can pick up the phone or not. |
| **Confidentiality footer** | A red shield-lock line reminding the user that every action is audit-logged under Act 720. | Same | Friction-free reminder of the legal regime they are operating under. |

> *Notes:* The dashboard query is permission-aware ŌĆö holders of `whistleblower.manage` and `whistleblower.view_all` see every case; holders of `whistleblower.investigate` only see cases where they are the assigned investigator. This is the same RBAC scoping pattern Employees uses for department heads, only stricter.

### Case detail (`/admin/whistleblower/{report}`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Triage panel** (only while `status = submitted`) | Three fields ŌĆö Severity (Low/Medium/High/Critical), "Assign investigator" (default "Myself", otherwise picks from super_admin / ceo / auditor / hr_admin pool), optional Notes. Submits to `POST /admin/whistleblower/{id}/triage` which is **gated by `2fa:fresh`** middleware. | `whistleblower.investigate` | Triage is a state change that decides whether the case enters the investigation pipeline. The 2FA-fresh gate makes sure a stolen session can't quietly classify a case as Low and stash it. |
| **Case detail tab** (default) | Decrypted summary, description, desired outcome, incident date/location, and the list of named subjects. Submitter contact is shown **only** if `is_anonymous = false` on the report. | Investigator assigned to the case, or `whistleblower.manage`, or `whistleblower.view_all` | The complete picture for the assigned investigator. The encrypted-at-rest cast decrypts on read via Eloquent ŌĆö investigators see plaintext, the database holds ciphertext. |
| **Log investigation action panel** (Actions tab) | Picks an action type from the `InvestigationActionType` enum (Interview, Document review, Site visit, Evidence added, Finding recorded, **Referred to CHRAJ**, **Referred to Auditor-General**, **Referred to Police**, Status changed, Message sent), accepts encrypted notes, and optionally chains a status change in the same submit. When the new status is a closed one, a closure-summary textarea appears (visible to the submitter via tracking code). | Same | The Actions tab is the case's append-only timeline ŌĆö every state change writes a row here, and nothing in the schema lets a row be edited or deleted from the UI. |
| **Action timeline** (below the form) | Each action rendered as a left-bordered card with action label, timestamp, investigator name, and decrypted notes. | Same | The case's narrative ŌĆö what was done, when, by whom. |
| **Submitter thread tab** | The same two-way thread the submitter sees, but with the investigator's name attached to outbound messages and the word "Submitter" on inbound. Reply form posts a fresh outbound message and logs a `MessageSent` action. | Same | Closes the loop with the (still anonymous) submitter. |
| **Evidence tab** | Lists each uploaded file with original filename, mime type, size, upload date, and optional caption. There is **no download button** wired in the MVP UI ŌĆö the files exist on the local disk but the read path is not yet built (see "What's planned next"). | Same | The reference is there; the controlled-download surface is roadmapped. |
| **Reassign (assign endpoint)** | A separate `POST /admin/whistleblower/{report}/assign` endpoint (currently called from a future "reassign" button) ŌĆö moves the case to a different investigator, gated by **`whistleblower.manage` + `2fa:fresh`**. | `whistleblower.manage` | When the assigned investigator goes on leave or steps away from a case, an admin can re-route it without needing to log into the database. |

> *Notes:* Closing a case is one-way. The investigation service throws `DomainException('Cannot re-open a closed case via status change. Open a new related case.')` if anyone tries to flip a closed status back to an open one. The matching UI in the action form does not even offer "Close ŌåÆ Investigating" as a destination ŌĆö the only legitimate way to revisit a closed allegation is to open a fresh, related case. This is deliberate: a case file that can move backwards is a case file that can be quietly rewritten.

## The data behind it

CIHRMS stores a whistleblower case across five tables, all pinned to the `whistleblower_reports` row:

- **`whistleblower_reports`** ŌĆö one row per disclosure. Public-safe fields (case number, category, severity, status, subject summary, incident date, intake source, timestamps) stay as plaintext columns; everything that touches the substance (`description`, `desired_outcome`, `incident_location`, `submitter_contact`, `closure_summary`) is cast `encrypted` and lives in the database as Laravel ciphertext keyed by `APP_KEY`. The row carries `tracking_token_hash` (SHA-256, 64 hex chars), `is_anonymous` (defaults true), and `submitter_user_id` (always NULL when anonymous ŌĆö enforced both at the service layer and again at test time). Soft-delete is on, which means destruction is operationally a flag flip; physical destruction is reserved to operational/legal review.
- **`whistleblower_subjects`** ŌĆö zero or more named people or entities per report. `subject_label` and `role_context` are both encrypted. An optional `linked_employee_id` lets the investigator pin the subject to a known Employee for cross-referencing during interviews.
- **`whistleblower_evidence`** ŌĆö zero or more files per report. The file content lives under `storage/app/whistleblower/{report_id}/` on the **local** disk (not the public disk). The original filename and mime type are plaintext for indexing; `caption` is encrypted. `uploaded_by` is NULL for anonymous uploads.
- **`whistleblower_actions`** ŌĆö the append-only investigator timeline. Every triage, status change, interview, document review, site visit, referral (CHRAJ, Auditor-General, Police), finding, and outbound message writes one row here. The `notes` field is encrypted; the `meta` JSON column carries machine-readable context such as `{from: 'submitted', to: 'triaged'}` for status changes or `{reassigned_to: 17}` for assignments. There is no `updated_at`-driven edit path; the table is treated as write-once by the service layer.
- **`whistleblower_messages`** ŌĆö the two-way thread. `direction = 'inbound'` from the submitter (`posted_by = NULL`); `direction = 'outbound'` from the investigator (`posted_by = user.id`). The `body` is encrypted; `read_at` is set when the *other party* reads the message ŌĆö though the read-receipt write is not yet wired in the MVP UI.

What every reader of the screen needs to keep in mind:

- **The tracking code is irrecoverable by design.** The database holds `tracking_token_hash`, never the plaintext. The intake service flashes the plaintext through the redirect session for a single display, then it is gone. There is no "email me my code" lane, no "I lost my code, can you reset it" lane, and **no will-be** such lane: building one would defeat the channel's entire promise. A submitter who loses the code can still file a new case, but the original case is unreachable from their side.
- **Anonymous means anonymous on the row.** When `is_anonymous = true` the service forces `submitter_user_id = NULL` even if the request came from a logged-in browser (proven by the test `it('forces submitter_user_id to NULL on anonymous submissions even if a user is passed')`). There is no shadow column that "actually" records the user ŌĆö there is just no place to record it.
- **The report row carries no IP address, no user agent, no session id, no fingerprint, no referrer, no analytics beacon, and no request id.** The route group is wrapped in `throttle:6,1` (Laravel's standard rate limiter), which evaluates against the client IP **at request time** for the limiter cache key but does not persist that IP anywhere durable. The Laravel application log captures stack traces and HTTP context the same way it does for every route ŌĆö there is no whistleblower-specific log-suppression filter in the MVP, which is a soft anonymity concern (see "Honest gaps").
- **Case content does not appear in the Auditor-General Report Pack.** The AG pack pulls *aggregate counts only* ŌĆö counts by category, severity, and status. The body of a case is statutorily segregated; if the AG wants a specific case, they go through the designated investigator and CHRAJ, not through the executive report.
- **Closure is one-way.** `WhistleblowerInvestigationService::changeStatus()` throws if anyone tries to move a closed case back to an open status. The only honest path is opening a new, related case ŌĆö and the related-case linkage itself is a roadmap item (see "What's planned next").

## How it talks to other modules

- **`WhistleblowerReportSubmitted` event** ŌåÆ fired at the end of every successful intake, before the transaction returns. No listeners are wired in the MVP ŌĆö the event exists so that downstream notifications (an SMS to the duty investigator, a Slack/Teams whisper, a CHRAJ webhook) can be added without touching the submission service. The event carries only the model ŌĆö there is no plaintext leakage in the event payload.
- **`WhistleblowerCaseClosed` event** ŌåÆ fired at the moment the investigation service closes the case. Same shape, same listener-less posture; the event is the hook point.
- **Audit Logs (Ch 24)** ŌåÆ the chapter-24 audit chain captures **every authenticated mutation** in the system. The triage / log-action / message / assign endpoints all run through the standard Laravel mutation path and are therefore covered by the global audit chain. **Important caveat:** the public submission endpoint runs anonymously and is deliberately *not* writing to the global audit chain ŌĆö that would create exactly the identity trail the channel exists to avoid. Instead, the **`whistleblower_actions` table is the case-internal append-only audit log**, and the investigation service is the only code path that writes to it.
- **Auditor-General Report Pack (Ch 24)** ŌåÆ `AuditorGeneralReportPack::writeWhistleblowerSummary()` emits a CSV of `(category, severity, status, count)` for the report period. There is no case-content leakage from this surface; the AG asks the designated investigator if they need a specific case.
- **Identity (Ch 25)** ŌåÆ the anonymity guarantees described above are a row-level promise; the cross-module identity model in Chapter 25 confirms there is no shadow identity attached via session, browser fingerprint, or device id at the application layer.
- **DPA Act 843 (Ch 26)** ŌåÆ disclosure content is **sensitive personal data** under ┬¦39. The encrypted-at-rest cast is the application-layer control; the retention schedule (currently soft-delete forever) sits on the DPA roadmap for hardening to a defined retention window.
- **Complaints (Ch 12)** ŌåÆ distinct module, distinct table, distinct permission tree. Complaints uses `complaints.*` permissions which are seeded to `hr_admin`, `manager`, `dept_head`, every employee, and several other roles ŌĆö i.e. HR is *in* the Complaints chain. Whistleblower uses `whistleblower.*` permissions which are seeded **only to `auditor` by default**, with `super_admin` and `ceo` inheriting via wildcard. The two channels are designed not to share routing.
- **Sidebar (Layouts/AuthenticatedLayout.vue)** ŌåÆ the "Whistleblower" item sits in the **System** section of the sidebar, alongside DPA Requests and Audit Logs. It is rendered with a red shield iconography to signal severity, not the standard cobalt module accent.

## Standards touchpoints

- **Whistleblower Act 2006 (Act 720) ┬¦3 ŌĆö protected disclosures.** The eleven `WhistleblowerCategory` enum cases are the operational expression of ┬¦3's qualifying-disclosure list ŌĆö corruption, fraud, criminal offence, mismanagement, harassment, discrimination, retaliation, safety, environmental, conflict of interest, and an "other" lane. The submission form's legal-notice panel cites Act 720 by name. See Chapter 44.
- **Whistleblower Act 2006 ┬¦10 ŌĆö independent oversight & CHRAJ referral.** The `InvestigationActionType::ReferralChraj` action is the operational hand-off ŌĆö every "Referred to CHRAJ" decision writes a `whistleblower_actions` row with the case number and the investigator's identity, and a closure of `closed_referred` records the outcome on the case row. The actual CHRAJ submission (paper, email, or API once a CHRAJ endpoint exists) is **manual today** ŌĆö the system records that the referral happened; it does not yet courier the case file to CHRAJ automatically. See Chapter 44.
- **Whistleblower Act 2006 ┬¦11 ŌĆö protection from retaliation.** Surfaced in the channel through the dedicated **`Retaliation`** category and the deliberate exclusion of `hr_admin` from any whistleblower permission. Retaliation cases are first-class disclosures in their own right; the segregation keeps them off any HR investigator's queue. **Note:** there is no automatic flag that *links* a retaliation report to a previously filed disclosure by the same submitter ŌĆö that link is the human investigator's job and the per-case timeline is the place they record it.
- **CHRAJ Act 1993 (Act 456).** The CHRAJ referral action type explicitly names the Commission for Human Rights and Administrative Justice. The pack of referral actions (CHRAJ / Auditor-General / Police) covers the three statutory escalation paths Ghanaian whistleblowing law contemplates. See Chapter 44.
- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦39 ŌĆö sensitive personal data.** All free-text disclosure content (`description`, `desired_outcome`, `incident_location`, `submitter_contact`, `closure_summary`, `subject_label`, `role_context`, action `notes`, evidence `caption`, message `body`) is cast `encrypted` and stored as ciphertext keyed by `APP_KEY`. The test suite asserts the ciphertext is not the plaintext on the raw DB row. See Chapter 26 and Chapter 44.
- **Data Protection Act ┬¦40 ŌĆö retention & erasure.** Reports use `SoftDeletes` and are retained indefinitely in the MVP. A defined retention schedule keyed to case status (e.g. closed-unsubstantiated ŌåÆ purge after N years) is roadmapped. See Chapter 26.
- **ISO 37002 ŌĆö Whistleblowing management systems.** The module is structured around the four ISO-37002 phases: **receiving** (public form with anonymity default), **assessing** (triage with severity + assignment), **addressing** (action timeline + thread + referral types), and **concluding** (closure with substantiated/unsubstantiated/referred outcomes plus a submitter-visible summary). The 2FA-fresh gate on triage and assign maps to ISO-37002's "secure handling of reports". See Chapter 44.

## What's planned next

The gap analysis (eight days old at the time of writing) marked the Whistleblower module as a Phase 2 priority; the present chapter documents the surface shipped in PR-and-merge cycle around 2026-05-29. Five concrete next steps are on the immediate roadmap:

1. **Evidence download surface.** Files are stored, listed, and metadata is shown ŌĆö but the controlled-download endpoint (with policy gate, audit-action emission, and short-TTL signed URL) is not yet wired in the investigator UI. This is the single largest functional gap on the admin side and is item one on the Phase 2 follow-up list.
2. **Anonymity hardening ŌĆö log scrubbing & egress controls.** Today the standard Laravel request log, error log, and APM trace will capture client IP and user-agent for `/whistleblower` POSTs the same way they do for any route. A dedicated middleware that strips the request from the audit/log pipeline for the public whistleblower routes, plus a CAPTCHA (hCaptcha or Ghana-specific alternative) on the submission form, are the two paired changes. The 6/min throttle remains as the floor.
3. **Submitter-side related-case linking.** Today a submitter who returns with new evidence on a closed case must open a fresh, unlinked case. A "this is related to WB-2026-NNNNN" lane on the submission form, plus a `parent_report_id` column, would let an investigator see the family of cases without breaking the one-way-closure rule.
4. **Read-receipt wiring.** The `whistleblower_messages.read_at` column exists; nothing currently writes to it. The next iteration sets it on the investigator's first view of an inbound message, and on the submitter's status-page load for outbound messages.
5. **Defined retention schedule (DPA ┬¦40).** The MVP retains everything via soft-delete forever. The Phase 2 retention work brings each case status under a defined window ŌĆö typically multiple years for substantiated cases, shorter for unsubstantiated ŌĆö with a quiet, audit-logged purge job. The CHRAJ referral path adds a "transfer of custody" hook that lets the institute drop its retention once CHRAJ accepts the file, so the case content lives in the regulator's system rather than ours.

<!-- ===== 28_governance.md ===== -->

# Chapter 28 ŌĆö Governance (Policy Register, Acknowledgements, Certifications, Incident Reports)

> *In one paragraph.* Governance is the lane CIHRMS uses for the institute-wide internal-control surface ŌĆö the **policy register** (HR, finance, IT, compliance, safety, conduct), the **acknowledgement ledger** that proves every member of staff has read each published policy, the **certifications register** that tracks the professional credentials staff hold and warns before they lapse, and the **incident-reports** thread where any employee can lodge a grievance, an improvement suggestion, or a workplace-safety concern into a private "circle" of reviewers. It is intentionally narrower than the legally-protected channel in Chapter 27 ŌĆö Whistleblower is for statute-grade allegations of corruption, fraud, harassment and the like; this module is the everyday governance shelf. The four sub-surfaces share one Vue page tree (`resources/js/Pages/Governance/...`), one controller pair (`GovernanceController` + `IncidentReportController`), and one permission group (`governance.*` plus the single `incidents.review` permission), but they map to four distinct migration files and four distinct model trees underneath.

## Where to find it

- **Sidebar location:** **Governance** group (building-with-columns icon, deep-indigo `#3949ab` masthead accent). Four child items:
    - **Overview** (`governance.index`) ŌĆö landing register: hero banner, KPI tiles, your-compliance ring, category-bars panel, policy cards.
    - **Manage** (`governance.manage`) ŌĆö only rendered when the viewer holds `governance.manage`; the lifecycle workspace for drafting, versioning, and publishing.
    - **Certifications** (`governance.certifications.index`) ŌĆö the cross-organisation certifications register with the expiry-reminder controls.
    - **Incident Reports** (`incidents.index`) ŌĆö the private grievance / suggestion / safety thread tree.
  The "Compose Incident" tile is also pinned in the Quick Actions menu and deep-links into `/governance/incidents?new=1` which auto-opens the New Report panel.
- **Roles that see it:**
    - **super_admin** and **ceo** ŌĆö every surface, every field (super-admin via the `before()` shortcut in `GovernancePolicy`; ceo via the wildcard role grant).
    - **hr_admin** ŌĆö the canonical policy owner: holds `governance.view`, `governance.manage`, `governance.acknowledge`, **and** `governance.cert_manage`. They draft, version, publish, retire policies and run the certifications register.
    - **manager / dept_head / employee / finance_officer / it_support / auditor** ŌĆö all hold `governance.view` and `governance.acknowledge`. They see the register, read every published policy, and sign acknowledgement against any policy they have not yet acknowledged. They do **not** see the Manage page (the sidebar entry is hidden, and the controller route is middleware-gated on `governance.manage`).
    - **incidents.review holders** ŌĆö by seeder design, **no role gets this permission by default**. The intent (`IncidentPermissionsSeeder` comment) is that super-admin or hr-admin grants it explicitly via the RBAC UI to the CEO plus chosen executives ŌĆö typically the head of HR, the company secretary, and one independent non-executive. Only holders of this permission appear in the "Assign reviewer" picker on an incident.
- **Related modules:** **Whistleblower (Ch 27)** ŌĆö the statute-grade lane for corruption / fraud / Act 720 disclosures; this chapter's Incident Reports lane is the everyday companion. **Audit Logs (Ch 24)** ŌĆö every mutation on this page (policy create/update/publish, certification add, incident assign/close) flows through the global Laravel audit pipeline. **DPA (Ch 26)** ŌĆö `signed_full_name`, `ip_address` and `user_agent` captured on every acknowledgement count as personal data and are kept under the same retention rules as the wider audit chain. **Reports & Analytics (Ch 31)** ŌĆö every Governance event (`PolicyPublished`, `PolicyAcknowledged`, `CertificationExpiring`, `CertificationExpired`) is registered against `RecordAnalyticsEvent` in `AppServiceProvider`, so the analytics dashboard counts them automatically. **Standards (Ch 44)** ŌĆö the King IV / ISO 31000 / ISO 37001 mapping for this module lives in the standards index.

## The screens

![Governance Overview ŌĆö editorial masthead, hero banner, KPI tiles, compliance ring, category bars, policy cards](../assets/screenshots/28_governance/overview.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Editorial-Sovereign masthead ŌĆö the page generates its own "Vol. X ┬Ę No. NNN" edition stamp (year offset from 2023 in Roman numerals, then day-of-year) so the register reads like an institutional bulletin rather than a CRUD list. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Indigo hero banner ŌĆö when the viewer has pending acknowledgements the headline reads "*N* policies awaiting your acknowledgement" in gold; otherwise "All caught up". Right side shows three counter chips (Total, Pending you, Certs) keyed to the cobalt / gold / sky palette. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Four KPI tiles ŌĆö Total policies, Published, Pending you, Certs expiring (30 days). ┬Ę ŌØ╣ "Your compliance" doughnut ring ŌĆö personal acknowledged-vs-published ratio, colour-shifts from gold ŌåÆ cobalt ŌåÆ cyan as the rate climbs through 80% and 100%. ┬Ę ŌØ║ "Policies by category" bars panel ŌĆö sorted descending, each bar tinted with the category's brand accent (magenta HR, navy Finance, cyan IT, gold Compliance, emerald Safety, deep-navy Conduct, sky Other); clicking a bar filters the register below in place. ┬Ę ŌØ╗ Cert teaser footer ŌĆö Total / Expiring 30d / Expired tiles, all three deep-link into the certifications register. ┬Ę ŌØ╝ Filter strip ŌĆö title-or-summary search, category dropdown, "Pending mine only" checkbox, one-click Clear. ┬Ę ŌØĮ Policy cards grid ŌĆö two columns on desktop, one on mobile; each card carries a left-edge accent stripe in its category colour, the icon tile, two pills (category + your-ack status), title, summary, and a footer with the current version + effective-from date.*

*Callouts: ŌØČ "Manage Policies" header ŌĆö masthead, an indigo "+ New Policy" CTA top-right. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Table ŌĆö title (linked to the policy detail), monospace slug, category label, owner name, current-version label (`v3` or "no version"), status (`ACTIVE` in emerald or `archived` in grey). ┬Ę ŌØĖ "New Policy" slide panel (large size) ŌĆö title (max 200), optional auto-derived slug, required category select, optional summary (max 1000), and an initial-body markdown editor pre-filled with `# Policy body` so the very first version is never empty.*

Once signed it flips to a green "You have acknowledged this version" card. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Right column "Version History" rail ŌĆö every version listed, the current one carrying a primary-coloured left bar; drafts read "draft" in italics; each entry shows the changelog line so the cumulative story is one glance away.*

## Every button, every action

### Governance Overview (`/governance`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Certifications** (header outline button) | Navigates to `/governance/certifications`. | Anyone with `governance.view` | Certifications carry the same compliance weight as policies ŌĆö the side-by-side prominence on the masthead is intentional. |
| **Manage Policies** (gradient CTA) | Navigates to `/governance/manage`. Only rendered when the viewer holds `governance.manage`. | `governance.manage` (hr_admin, super_admin, ceo) | Keeps the read surface uncluttered for the 95% of users who only ever acknowledge. |
| **"Show me" pending banner button** | Sets the front-end filter `onlyPendingMine = true` ŌĆö no round-trip. | Anyone with pending acks | One-click triage: the amber banner is the page's most attention-grabbing element when you have outstanding signatures. |
| **KPI tiles** | Read-only. Numbers come from the same controller render ŌĆö total/published/pending/30-day-expiring counts. | Anyone with `governance.view` | The five-second answer to "where do I stand on this?". |
| **"Your compliance" doughnut ring** | Read-only. The ring stroke transitions colour at 80% (goldŌåÆcobalt) and 100% (cobaltŌåÆcyan), so a perfect compliance state is visually distinct from "doing fine but not done". | Anyone with `governance.view` | Personal accountability surfacing ŌĆö the page is honest about *your* outstanding signatures, not just the institute's. |
| **Category bar buttons** | Each row in the "Policies by category" panel is a toggle: click sets the filter to that category, click again clears. The active bar stays full opacity, the others dim to 40%. | Anyone with `governance.view` | Filtering by reading-list category is the most common navigation intent ŌĆö turns the analytics chart into a navigation control. |
| **Cert teaser tiles** (Total / Expiring 30d / Expired) | Each is a `Link` to `/governance/certifications`. | Anyone with `governance.view` | The certifications register is the second-most-frequented sub-page; teasing the numbers next to a one-click hop is faster than making the user open the sidebar. |
| **Search box** | Free-text filter on title + summary, client-side, no round-trip ŌĆö the whole register is delivered on one Inertia render. | Anyone with `governance.view` | The register is small (tens of rows in normal use); a client-side filter feels instant where a server round-trip would not. |
| **Category dropdown** | Sets the same filter the bar buttons drive. | Same | Two paths to the same intent ŌĆö a power-user uses the dropdown, a glance-user clicks the bar. |
| **Pending mine only** checkbox | Filters to rows where `my_ack_status === 'pending'`. | Anyone with `governance.view` | The "Show me" banner button writes this directly; this is the manual equivalent. |
| **Clear** (appears only when a filter is set) | Resets all three filter inputs. | Same | Standard escape. |
| **Policy card click** | Navigates to `/governance/policies/{id}`. | `governance.view` | Whole-card hit target; the body of the card and the "Open ŌåÆ" affordance both navigate to the same place. |

> *Notes:* The whole page hydrates from one Inertia render ŌĆö `GovernanceController::index` returns the policies collection (with `owner` and `currentVersion` eager-loaded), the pending-ack id list (via `GovernanceService::pendingAcksFor()`), the category distribution histogram, the personal compliance stats, and the certifications telemetry. There is no N+1 on the cards. The compliance percentage is computed as `(published_count - pending_for_me) / published_count` ŌĆö when no policies are published the rate displays as 100% rather than divide-by-zero. The editorial masthead's edition number is purely cosmetic: it's derived live from `new Date()`, not stored.

### Manage Policies (`/governance/manage`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ŌåÉ Governance** | Back to overview. | `governance.manage` | Standard back affordance. |
| **+ New Policy** (gradient CTA) | Opens the "Create Policy" slide panel (large). | `governance.manage` | Drafts are the start of the lifecycle. |
| **Title** | Required, max 200. | `governance.manage` | Saved to `policies.title`. |
| **Slug** | Optional, max 200, must be unique on `policies.slug`. When blank, derived server-side from the title via `Str::slug()` so the URL never collides. | Same | Manual entry is offered for the data-migration case; in normal operation HR leaves it blank. |
| **Category** | Required, must be one of the seven `PolicyCategory` enum values (HR, Finance, IT, Compliance, Safety, Conduct, Other). | Same | Drives the masthead colour, the icon, the bars panel, and the category filter. |
| **Summary** | Optional, max 1000. | Same | Renders on the card on the overview; not on the detail page. |
| **Initial Body** | Optional, free-form markdown. When blank, the service inserts `# Draft policy body` so the very first version has at least one heading. | Same | Every published policy must have a version row; this is the cleanest way to guarantee that on create. |
| **Create Draft** | POST `/governance/policies`. On success the panel closes and a toast flashes. | Same | The whole create is wrapped in `DB::transaction` ŌĆö the policy row, the version-1 row, and the `PolicyDrafted` event are all-or-nothing. |
| **Title link in the table** | Navigates to `/governance/policies/{id}`. | Same | Open the detail page to start adding/publishing versions. |
| **Slug column** | Read-only monospace display of the slug. | Same | Visual confirmation of how the URL was derived. |
| **Status column** | `ACTIVE` (emerald) or `archived` (grey), based on `is_active`. | Same | A single source of truth for "is this in force or shelved?". |

> *Notes:* The Manage page is the only place a policy can be deleted, and even then only via the underlying `SoftDeletes` trait ŌĆö there is no UI button for delete in the MVP; the standard archival pattern is to flip `is_active` to false through `updatePolicy`. Phase 1 wires a dedicated retire/archive button into this table.

### Policy Detail (`/governance/policies/{id}`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ŌåÉ All Policies** | Back to overview. | `governance.view` | Standard. |
| **Rendered body** (main column) | Read-only HTML rendered from the current version's markdown body via a lightweight inline renderer (handles `#`, `##`, `-` bullets, and paragraph breaks). | `governance.view` | Inertia keeps the markdown server-side, but a full markdown-pipeline npm dependency was deferred to Phase 1 ŌĆö the inline renderer covers what HR actually writes today. |
| **Typed-signature input** | Required, max 120, must match the viewer's account name when normalised (lowercase + trimmed). On mismatch the request aborts with a 422 "Typed signature does not match the account name on file." Captures `ip_address` and `user_agent` server-side from the request. | `governance.acknowledge` (every seeded role) | The typed-signature requirement is the application-layer "intent to sign" gesture ŌĆö it stops anyone confirming a policy by accidentally clicking a button. |
| **Acknowledge** (cobalt CTA) | POST `/governance/versions/{version}/ack`. On success the panel flips to green "You have acknowledged this version" without a page reload. | Same | The whole flow's commit. |
| **Version History rail** | Read-only list of every version with version number, published date (or "draft"), and the changelog line. Current version carries a left bar accent in primary colour. | `governance.view` | Auditors and HR alike need to see the full lineage at a glance ŌĆö when was v2 published, what did the changelog say, who signed when. |

> *Notes:* `acknowledge()` is idempotent ŌĆö if a user posts the same acknowledgement twice the service returns the existing row rather than throwing or inserting a duplicate. The unique key `(policy_version_id, user_id)` on `policy_acknowledgements` is the database-side belt to the service's braces. `PolicyAcknowledged` is dispatched once per first-time signature; the analytics listener increments the acknowledgement-rate KPI on the Reports dashboard (Ch 31).

### Certifications (`/governance/certifications`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ŌåÉ Governance** | Back to overview. | `governance.view` | Standard. |
| **Send Reminders Now** | POST `/governance/certifications/dispatch-reminders`. Loops over every certification with an expiry inside the next 30 days that has not yet had a reminder, dispatches `CertificationExpiring` (or `CertificationExpired` if already past), and stamps `reminder_sent_at`. | `governance.cert_manage` | Manual trigger for the back-office. The scheduled job (`governance:certification-reminders`, Artisan command) runs the same code on a cron in production. |
| **+ Add Certification** | Opens the "Add Certification" slide panel. | `governance.cert_manage` | The only path to a new row in the MVP ŌĆö there is no employee-self-add flow on this surface yet (the Learning module in Ch 7 adds rows via the `CertificationIssued` event when a course completes). |
| **Employee** select | Required. Populated with all `Employee::active()` rows (`employee_no ŌĆö position`). | `governance.cert_manage` | Tying a certification to a specific employee is mandatory ŌĆö every row needs an owner. |
| **Certification Name** | Required, max 200. | Same | Plain-text label of the credential ŌĆö "PMP", "ACCA Level III", "First Aid at Work", etc. |
| **Issuer** | Optional, max 200. | Same | Awarding body. |
| **Credential ID** | Optional, max 120. | Same | The reference number printed on the certificate; rendered monospace in the table. |
| **Issued / Expires dates** | Both optional. Server-side `expires_at` must be `after_or_equal:issued_at`. | Same | Expiry drives the traffic-lights and reminder pipeline; nullable on issued for legacy imports. |
| **Verification URL** | Optional, must be a valid URL, max 500 chars. | Same | The link to the issuer's online verification page where applicable. |
| **Add Certification** (gradient CTA) | POST `/governance/certifications`. On success closes the panel and refreshes. | `governance.cert_manage` | Standard commit. |
| **Row "Reminded" column** | Read-only; renders the last-sent reminder date, or `ŌĆö` when none has been sent. | Anyone viewing the table | Lets the operator see whether a reminder went out without opening the audit log. |

> *Notes:* When a non-managing user (e.g. a regular employee) hits `/governance/certifications`, the controller scopes the query to `where('employee_id', $request->user()->employee?->id ?? 0)` ŌĆö they see only their own rows. The "Add Certification" CTA and the employee picker are both hidden in that case because `props.employees` arrives empty (the `canManage` computed in the Vue page checks for that). When neither salary-style PII protection nor confidentiality is in play, the table itself is the source of truth ŌĆö `GovernanceCertificationResource` includes a `days_to_expiry` computed attribute that the row's status colour reads.

### Incident Reports ŌĆö directory (`/governance/incidents`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **New Report** (gradient CTA) | Opens the "New Incident Report" slide panel (large). | Anyone whose account has an `Employee` row (`IncidentReportPolicy::create`) | The whole lane only exists for employees ŌĆö a `User` without an employee profile cannot submit (`create()` throws `ValidationException` server-side). |
| **Category pills** | Filter the list to one of `Grievance`, `Improvement`, `Safety`, `Other` (or All). Drives `router.get` with `preserveState: true, replace: true`. | Anyone viewing | The four categories are the operational expression of the `IncidentCategory` enum; the colour-coded badges keep the directory readable at a glance. |
| **Status pills** | Filter to one of `Open`, `In Review`, `Closed` (or All). | Same | The full lifecycle (`Open` ŌåÆ `InReview` on first assignment ŌåÆ `Closed` ŌåÆ optional re-open back to `InReview`). |
| **Search input** | Title-substring search, debounced 380 ms. | Same | Most lookups are by title fragment; the body is not searched in the MVP (full-text search is a Phase 2 roadmap item). |
| **Report card click** | Navigates to `/governance/incidents/{id}`. | `IncidentReportPolicy::view` ŌĆö only the submitter and any current assignee (`isInCircle()`) | The circle is enforced server-side; even a manager who has not been assigned to a report cannot read it. |
| **Empty state** | Plain "No reports in this view." block. | Same | Honest empty state ŌĆö no fake placeholder cards. |

> *Notes:* The list is scoped by `IncidentReport::scopeVisibleTo($user)` ŌĆö a report is visible to the user when they are the submitter (joined through `employees.user_id`) **or** they are a currently-active assignee (joined through `incident_report_assignees` with `removed_at IS NULL`). The pagination is a standard Laravel `paginate(20)->withQueryString()` so deep-links carry the filter state.

### Incident Reports ŌĆö submission panel

| Field | Validation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Category** | Required radio, one of the four enum values. | Defaults to `grievance` so the most-common path takes zero clicks. |
| **Title** | Required, 6ŌĆō180 chars. | The only field shown in the directory; the placeholder reminds the submitter "make this scannable for a reviewer". |
| **Details** | Required, 20ŌĆō10 000 chars. | The body of the report; rendered with whitespace preserved on the detail page. |
| **Attachments** | Optional, up to three files, 10 MB each. Accepted MIME: `.pdf, .png, .jpg, .jpeg, .doc, .docx`. | Stored on the `incidents` disk under `{report_id}/uuid-original-name.ext`; the disk is configured outside the public web root so files are never directly fetchable. The download endpoint is policy-gated via `IncidentReportPolicy::downloadAttachment`. |
| **Submit Privately** (gradient CTA) | POST `/governance/incidents`. On success redirects to the new report's detail page with a "Your report has been submitted privately." flash. | Same `DB::transaction` envelope as policies ŌĆö the report, the attachments, and the initial status are all-or-nothing. |

### Incident Reports ŌĆö detail (`/governance/incidents/{id}`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ŌåÉ Back to reports** | Back to the directory. | Anyone in the circle | Standard. |
| **Assign** | Opens the "Assign Reviewer" slide panel ŌĆö lists every user who holds the `incidents.review` permission either by role or by the per-user `permissions` JSON column. Selecting one assigns them via POST `/governance/incidents/{id}/assign`. | The submitter or any current assignee (`IncidentReportPolicy::assign`) | The submitter is allowed to invite a reviewer into their own report ŌĆö important when the report is about a specific person and the submitter wants a particular non-default investigator on it. |
| **Reviewer Remove** link | DELETE `/governance/incidents/{id}/assign/{user}`. Confirms with a browser dialog because removal is immediate ("they will lose access immediately"). | Same | Removal is a soft pivot update (`removed_at` is set, the row is preserved) ŌĆö the audit trail of who was on the case is intact even after they leave the circle. |
| **Close** (green CTA) | POST `/governance/incidents/{id}/close`. Sets status to `Closed`, stamps `closed_at` and `closed_by_id`, and records the optional resolution note (entered through the `CloseIncidentReportRequest`). | Holders of `incidents.review` (route is middleware-gated on `permission:incidents.review`) AND who are current assignees (policy check) ŌĆö both gates are belt + braces | Closure is a deliberate, named act ŌĆö the policy check makes sure only people in the circle can close, the middleware check is the defence-in-depth fence around the route. |
| **Reopen** (amber CTA) | POST `/governance/incidents/{id}/reopen`. Sets status back to `InReview` and clears the closure fields. Confirms with a browser dialog. | Same | Closure is reversible in this lane ŌĆö Whistleblower's one-way closure is the higher-severity contrast. |
| **Reply form** (only when not closed) | POST `/governance/incidents/{id}/messages`. Body required, max 10 000; up to three attachments at 10 MB each. | Anyone in the circle whose status check passes (`postMessage()` policy) | The thread is the case's working journal ŌĆö every assignee + the submitter can post, the locked-while-closed rule means the audit timeline does not drift after closure. |
| **Attachment download** | GET `/governance/incidents/attachments/{attachment}/download`. Streams the file through the controller's `Storage::disk('incidents')->download(...)`. | Anyone who can view the parent report | Disk-gated, policy-gated, never publicly resolvable. |

> *Notes:* The first assignment auto-bumps a report from `Open` to `InReview` (`IncidentReportService::assign()`), giving the submitter a visible signal that the case has been picked up. The `eligibleReviewers()` query is deliberately permissive ŌĆö it returns users with `incidents.review` granted *either* via a role or via the per-user `permissions` JSON, which is the pattern the RBAC chapter (Ch 24) uses for per-individual grants. Re-assigning a previously-removed reviewer reuses the same pivot row by clearing `removed_at` ŌĆö there is at most one row per (report, user).

## The data behind it

The Governance module spans seven tables across three feature areas:

- **Policy register** ŌĆö three tables created in one migration (`2026_05_30_000001_create_policies_tables`):
    - `policies` ŌĆö title (200), unique slug (200), `category` enum (HR, Finance, IT, Compliance, Safety, Conduct, Other), free-text `summary`, `owner_user_id` FK to the policy steward, `is_active` flag, `current_version_id` self-deferred FK to the published version, soft-deletes. Indexed on `category` and `is_active`.
    - `policy_versions` ŌĆö every revision of every policy. Carries `version_number`, `body` (longtext markdown), `effective_from` / `effective_to` (date), free-text `changelog`, and the audit pair `published_by` / `published_at`. Unique key on `(policy_id, version_number)`.
    - `policy_acknowledgements` ŌĆö the per-user, per-version ledger of who signed what. Carries `acknowledged_at`, `ip_address` (45 chars ŌĆö IPv6-safe), `user_agent` (text), and `signed_full_name` (120). Unique key on `(policy_version_id, user_id)`.
- **Certifications register** ŌĆö one table (`certifications`, created in `2026_05_20_000003_create_certifications_table` and extended in `2026_05_30_000002_add_reminder_sent_at_to_certifications`):
    - `certifications` ŌĆö `employee_id` FK, optional `course_id` FK (back-link to the Learning module's course catalogue when the cert came from an internal course completion), `name`, `issuer`, `credential_id`, `issued_at`, `expires_at`, optional `document_path` for an uploaded scan of the certificate, optional `verification_url`, soft-deletes. Added column `reminder_sent_at` (timestamp, indexed) ŌĆö the one-shot stamp the expiry pipeline writes so a row is not reminded twice.
- **Incident reports** ŌĆö four tables in one migration (`2026_05_18_000001_create_incident_reports_tables`):
    - `incident_reports` ŌĆö `employee_id` FK to the submitter, `category` enum (grievance / improvement / safety / other), `title` (180), text `body`, `status` enum (open / in_review / closed) defaulting to open, `closed_at` / `closed_by_id` / `resolution_note` triplet that's all-NULL on open cases. Soft-deletes. Indexes on `(employee_id, status)` and `(status, created_at)`.
    - `incident_report_assignees` ŌĆö pivot between `incident_reports` and `users`. Carries `assigned_at`, `assigned_by_id`, and a nullable `removed_at`. Primary key is the pair `(incident_report_id, user_id)` ŌĆö re-assignment after removal updates the existing row rather than inserting a duplicate.
    - `incident_report_messages` ŌĆö the thread. `author_id` FK to `users`, body text, indexed on `(incident_report_id, created_at)` for the per-case timeline scan.
    - `incident_report_attachments` ŌĆö polymorphic on `(attachable_type, attachable_id)` so an attachment can hang off either the report itself or any of its messages. Carries `file_path`, `original_name`, `mime_type`, `size_bytes`, `uploaded_by_id`.

What every reader of the screen needs to keep in mind:

- **Acknowledgement is per-version, not per-policy.** Publishing v2 of a policy that everyone signed at v1 immediately resets every staff member's `my_ack_status` for that policy back to `pending`.
