The Service computes `pendingAcksFor($user)` by checking the existence of a row on `policy_acknowledgements` keyed to the *current* version id only ŌĆö the trail of older signatures stays in the database, but only the current version counts towards "compliant".
- **Published versions are append-only.** Once a `policy_versions` row has `published_at` set, the publish service refuses to publish it again (`DomainException: "Version N is already published."`). The way to correct a published version is to add a new version, not to mutate the published one. Publishing the new version stamps `effective_to = effective_from - 1 day` on the outgoing version so the historical record has a clean handover date.
- **Incident-report visibility is server-side circle membership, not a role check.** A super-admin who has not been assigned to an incident cannot read it ŌĆö the `IncidentReportPolicy` deliberately does not implement a `before()` shortcut. The "circle" is the union of the submitter and the *currently-active* assignees, and removing an assignee removes their access in the same request.
- **The signed name must match the account name.** `AcknowledgePolicyRequest::passedValidation()` lowercases and trims both strings and aborts the request with HTTP 422 on mismatch. This is the application-layer "intent to sign" guard ŌĆö it stops a user clicking through an acknowledgement with a placeholder string.
- **Reminders are one-shot per cert.** `reminder_sent_at` is set the first time a cert enters the 30-day window. A new reminder for the same cert is not sent automatically; the operator triggers a fresh sweep via "Send Reminders Now" on the certifications page (or by re-running the `governance:certification-reminders` Artisan command) only after manually clearing the timestamp, which is a known limitation called out below.

## How it talks to other modules

- **`PolicyDrafted` / `PolicyVersionAdded` / `PolicyPublished` / `PolicyAcknowledged` events** ŌåÆ dispatched at the end of each lifecycle transition. All four are registered in `AppServiceProvider` against `RecordAnalyticsEvent` so they roll up into the Reports & Analytics module's compliance KPIs (Ch 31). No notification listeners are wired in the MVP ŌĆö the events are the hook points for a future "your policy ack rate is below threshold" digest.
- **`CertificationExpiring` / `CertificationExpired` events** ŌåÆ dispatched by `GovernanceService::dispatchExpiryReminders($daysAhead)` (called from the controller's "Send Reminders Now" button and from the `DispatchCertificationReminders` Artisan command). Both events are registered against `RecordAnalyticsEvent` so the dashboards count them. No notification listener is wired in the MVP ŌĆö the lapsed-cert email/SMS path is on the Phase 1 roadmap; today the operator sees lapses on the register page itself.
- **Incident-report listener fleet** ŌåÆ five auto-discovered listeners live under `app/Listeners/Incident/`: `NotifyAssignee` (writes a `Notification` row when a reviewer joins the circle), `NotifyUnassigned` (when they leave), `NotifyMessageRecipients` (when a thread reply lands), `NotifySubmitterOnClose` (when the case is closed), and `NotifyCircleOnReopen` (when it returns to in-review). These are wired through Laravel 11's typed-parameter listener auto-discovery, not through explicit `Event::listen` in the provider ŌĆö registering them twice is a known footgun the provider's source comment flags out loud.
- **Employees (Ch 3)** ŌåÆ every incident report joins via `employee_id` to the submitter's Employee row. The "submitter has no employee profile" failure mode is caught in `IncidentReportService::create()` with a ValidationException, so a staff account that exists without an employee row (a rare data-migration artefact) gets a clean "you must have an employee profile to submit an incident report" error rather than a 500.
- **Learning (Ch 7) ŌĆö bidirectional certifications link** ŌåÆ completing an internal course that has a `confers_certificate` config emits a `CertificationIssued` event that the Learning module's listener turns into a `certifications` row pinned by `course_id`. From the certifications register's point of view such rows look identical to manually-entered ones; from the Learning module's point of view they are how a course completion becomes a tracked credential.
- **Whistleblower (Ch 27)** ŌåÆ the two modules deliberately share no data and almost no permissions. Incident Reports is for ordinary workplace concerns where the submitter is willing to be named; Whistleblower is the Act-720-grade lane with one-way tracking-code anonymity. The sidebar groups them apart (Governance vs System) and the seeders never grant `whistleblower.*` permissions to anyone who automatically gets `incidents.review`.
- **DPA (Ch 26)** ŌåÆ policy acknowledgements record `ip_address`, `user_agent`, and the typed `signed_full_name` ŌĆö all three count as personal data under the DPA Act. The retention rule today is "kept indefinitely while the user account exists" via the cascading `policy_acknowledgements.user_id` FK. A defined retention schedule for ex-employee acknowledgements is a Phase 2 follow-up.
- **Audit Logs (Ch 24)** ŌåÆ the global Laravel audit chain captures every authenticated mutation on this module (policy create/update/publish, ack post, certification add/update/delete, incident assign/unassign/post/close/reopen). The incident messaging thread is *not* mirrored into the global chain row-by-row ŌĆö the per-report `incident_report_messages` table is the per-case audit timeline by design.

## Standards touchpoints

- **King IV Principle 5 ŌĆö Governance of risk** (also paralleled in the Ghana Code of Best Practice for Public Sector). The Governance module is the operational expression of the principle's "policy framework" requirement: a versioned policy register, owner accountability (`owner_user_id`), a documented publication date (`effective_from`), and proof-of-knowledge (the acknowledgement ledger). What is **not** yet shipped in the MVP and remains explicitly mapped to this principle in the roadmap is the **risk register** itself (a top-N risks table with owner, likelihood, impact, treatment, status) ŌĆö see "Honest gaps" below. See Chapter 44.
- **King IV Principle 13 ŌĆö Compliance.** The acknowledgement ledger is the audit-grade evidence of policy reading ŌĆö typed signature, captured IP, captured user agent, immutable timestamp. The `signed_full_name` match against the account name is the application-layer integrity check. See Chapter 44.
- **ISO 31000 ŌĆö Risk management framework.** The four-state policy lifecycle (draft ŌåÆ published ŌåÆ effective ŌåÆ archived) maps to ISO 31000's "establish, implement, monitor, review" loop, but only the policy half ŌĆö the **risk identification + treatment side of the framework is not built in the MVP** and is the single biggest gap against this standard. See Chapter 44 and "Honest gaps".
- **ISO 37001 ŌĆö Anti-bribery management systems.** The `Conduct` category is the home for the code-of-conduct policy and any anti-bribery policy the institute publishes; the typed-signature acknowledgement of those policies is the evidence ISO 37001 ┬¦7.3 (awareness) requires. Anti-bribery-specific case routing is **not** built ŌĆö those concerns route through the Whistleblower channel (Ch 27), not this module. See Chapter 44.
- **Public Financial Management Act, 2016 (Act 921) ŌĆö internal controls.** The `Finance` policy category is where the institute publishes its procurement policy, the budgetary-control policy, the expenditure-authorisation policy, and the related internal-control suite the Act expects. The acknowledgement ledger then proves every finance officer has read the current version. The financial-control surface that **enforces** those policies (segregation of duties, approval thresholds, dual control) lives in the Finance module (Ch F1ŌĆōF5), not here. See Chapter 44.
- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦17ŌĆō18 ŌĆö lawful basis & data minimisation.** The `Compliance` category is where the institute's data-protection policy is published; the `signed_full_name` / `ip_address` / `user_agent` captured on acknowledgement are personal-data fields processed under the employment-contract basis. See Chapter 26 and Chapter 44.
- **Labour Act, 2003 (Act 651) ┬¦63 ŌĆö grievance procedure.** The Incident Reports lane is the everyday grievance channel the Labour Act anticipates ŌĆö a submitter raises a `grievance` category report, the case is assigned to a reviewer, a private thread documents the back-and-forth, and the closure note records the outcome. The reviewers (assignees) are deliberately not bound to be HR ŌĆö any user the institute grants `incidents.review` can be a reviewer, which lets a small institute give the role to a respected non-HR senior colleague where the grievance is *about* HR. See Chapter 44.

## Honest gaps

The Governance module today is deliberately scoped to the policy + acknowledgement + certification + incident-thread surface that PR-and-merge cycle around 2026-05-30 shipped. The chapter is intentionally short because the module is intentionally short. Five concrete gaps are open against the standards mapping above:

1. **No risk register.** The "risk register" sub-page King IV and ISO 31000 both call for ŌĆö a table of top organisational risks with owner, likelihood, impact, treatment, residual, status, and review cycle ŌĆö is **not built** in the MVP. The current Governance module covers policies, not risks. A `risks` table and a "Risk Register" sidebar entry are first-class items on the Phase 2 roadmap; until then the institute records its risk register out-of-band (typically in the board pack).
2. **No board-pack assembly surface.** There is no "Board Pack" button that bundles the current policy versions, the open-incident summary, the certification-expiry list, and the (eventual) risk register into one PDF for board distribution. Board members today receive these surfaces individually. The pack assembly is Phase 2 work and depends on item 1 above landing first.
3. **No committee meeting tracker.** There is no `committees` / `committee_meetings` / `agenda_items` data model. The institute schedules committee meetings out-of-band (calendar invites, shared minutes documents). Building a meeting tracker + minute-publication flow is Phase 2 ŌĆö the Incident Reports thread is structurally similar enough that it could be the seed for it.
4. **Reminders are one-shot per cert, not a cadence.** `reminder_sent_at` is set the first time a certification enters the 30-day window. The system does not currently send a 7-day-out or 1-day-out follow-up. Implementing a multi-tier cadence (30/7/1) plus a post-expiry "your cert lapsed" alert is a small Phase 1 add ŌĆö the table already has all the fields it needs.
5. **No expiry notifications wired beyond analytics.** `CertificationExpiring` and `CertificationExpired` events are dispatched and the analytics dashboard counts them, but no email/SMS listener turns them into a personal "your cert is lapsing" message. Today the operator sees lapses on the register page or via the manual "Send Reminders Now" button; the staff member sees nothing automatic. Wiring a notification listener is a one-class change and is on the Phase 1 list.

## What's planned next

Phase 1 of the governance build-out (4ŌĆō6 weeks, planned for the cycle following the 2026-05-30 PR) lands four things directly on this module:

1. **Risk register table + page.** A new `risks` table (id, title, description, owner_user_id, likelihood enum, impact enum, residual rating, treatment text, status enum, next-review date), a `/governance/risks` register page that mirrors the policy-register layout (KPI tiles, severity-grid panel, filter strip, card grid), and a "Risk owner sign-off" acknowledgement flow modelled on the policy-acknowledgement ledger.
2. **Multi-tier certification reminder cadence.** Extend `DispatchCertificationReminders` to honour a configurable cadence (30/7/1 by default), persist the cadence stamps to a `certification_reminders` child table rather than a single column, and wire the `CertificationExpiring` / `CertificationExpired` events to a `Notification` listener so the certificate holder is told personally ŌĆö not just the operator.
3. **Markdown rendering upgrade.** Replace the lightweight inline renderer in `Show.vue` with a server-side parse to sanitised HTML (Laravel `commonmark` package) so policy authors can use tables, blockquotes, ordered lists, and code blocks. Today's renderer covers headings, bullets and paragraphs only.
4. **Board-pack assembler v0.** A single "Generate Board Pack" button on the Governance overview that produces a PDF bundle of: the current policy register snapshot, the certifications-expiring-next-90-days table, the open-incident-reports summary (counts only ŌĆö no body), and a placeholder section for the risk register (filled in once item 1 above lands). The Auditor-General report pack (Ch 24) is the template; the board pack is a friendlier-shaped cousin.

Phase 2 adds the committee meeting tracker (item 3 in the gaps list), the formal retention schedule for ex-employee acknowledgements (item from the DPA cross-reference), and an "Anti-bribery declaration" recurring acknowledgement flow tied to the ISO 37001 ┬¦7.3 awareness requirement ŌĆö a once-a-year forced re-sign on the code-of-conduct policy, with the cadence enforced server-side rather than relying on HR to re-publish the policy.

<!-- ===== 29_kiosk.md ===== -->

# Chapter 29 ŌĆö Kiosk (Shared-Device Attendance Terminal)

> *In one paragraph.* The Kiosk is the single-purpose, no-login attendance terminal that turns any browser ŌĆö a wall-mounted tablet at reception, a low-spec laptop at a guard post, a refurbished desktop in the factory loading bay ŌĆö into a clock-in / clock-out station that any employee can walk up to and use without having to remember a password. There is no session, no sidebar, no app shell; the page identifies the employee with two pieces of public information (their employee number and a fragment of their name), confirms the match face-to-screen, records the punch through the same `AttendanceService::record()` that every other attendance source uses (Chapter 5), and reverts to "Welcome" in six seconds so the next person can step up. The whole module is one Vue page, one controller, two FormRequests, and five routes ŌĆö it is deliberately tiny because all the heavy lifting (lateness, overtime, daily summary, payroll integration, audit log) is done by Attendance. The Kiosk is the front door; the ledger lives next door.

## Where to find it

- **Public URL:** **`/kiosk`** ŌĆö the only screen in the module. There is no sidebar link from the rest of CIHRMS to this page on purpose: the kiosk URL is opened once, by IT, on the dedicated device, and then the device stays on that page in fullscreen for its working life. An admin browsing the main app does not "navigate to" the kiosk; they walk over to the device.
- **Roles that see it:** **none ŌĆö and everyone.** The kiosk routes are explicitly outside the auth middleware. Anyone who can reach the URL ŌĆö typically only the device itself, on the office LAN ŌĆö can use it. The "permission" check is the identity step described below (employee_no + a fragment of the user's name); there is no role gate because the device has no logged-in user to gate against.
- **Related modules:** **Attendance (Ch 5)** ŌĆö every kiosk punch lands in `attendance_records` with `source = web_kiosk` and is reconciled into `attendance_summaries` by the same recompute that runs for biometric devices, self-service GPS clocks, and HR manual entries; **Employees (Ch 3)** ŌĆö the identity match runs against `employees.employee_no` joined to `users.name`, no extra table; **Identity Verification (Ch 25)** ŌĆö the face-recognition route exists as a 501 stub waiting on the same Ghana-Card biometric stack that Chapter 25 is building; **Standards Benchmark (Ch 44)** ŌĆö the kiosk is the WCAG 2.1 AA touchpoint for shared-device accessibility and a Cybersecurity Act 2020 (Act 1038) touchpoint for the device-hardening section.

## The screens

The kiosk is one page with three sequential stages ŌĆö Identify, Confirm, Success ŌĆö that flip in place rather than navigate. The browser URL never changes once `/kiosk` is open.

![Kiosk ŌĆö Identify stage with on-screen QWERTY keyboard and live wall](../assets/screenshots/29_kiosk/identify.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Atmospheric header ŌĆö the CIHRMS mark on the left, a large live HH:MM:SS clock on the right driven by a `setInterval` that ticks every second so the time on screen is always the time about to be recorded. The clock is large on purpose; staff use it to settle disputes about whether the punch they just made was "before 8" or "after 8". ┬Ę ŌØĘ "Step 1 of 2 ┬Ę Identify" eyebrow above a single card ŌĆö Employee ID input on top, name input below. Both fields show the active field with a cobalt halo so a finger tap on a slightly off-target spot still tells the user which field is taking keys. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Full on-screen QWERTY keyboard ŌĆö number row + three letter rows + a shift key (caps-once behaviour, defaults on for the name field), backspace, clear-current-field, Tab to switch fields, space (only types into the name field), and a cobalt Enter key that submits. `mousedown.prevent` on every key keeps the input focused so the caret never moves away. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Live "Today's wall" at the bottom ŌĆö a horizontal pill-list of the last eight kiosk punches today, polled every 15 seconds. Each pill shows only the first name + an in/out icon + the time. No staff IDs, no surnames, no positions ŌĆö see the Data section below.*

![Kiosk ŌĆö Confirm stage with avatar, last-event hint, big In/Out buttons](../assets/screenshots/29_kiosk/confirm.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ "Step 2 of 2 ┬Ę Confirm" eyebrow with the matched employee's avatar, name in 30-pt bold, position, and `employee_no` in monospace. This is the "is this you?" moment ŌĆö the next person in the queue, looking over the shoulder, would also see the photo and name on the wall-mounted screen, which is intentional: this is shared device, low-stakes data, and the on-the-wall visibility is itself a social anti-impersonation control. ┬Ę ŌØĘ A "Last today" line ŌĆö "Last today: IN at 08:14" or "No events recorded today yet." ŌĆö so the employee can see what direction the system expects next without guessing. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Two large buttons ŌĆö Clock In (cobalt) and Clock Out (ghost). The system suggests one of them based on the last event today and ringers it in gold; both are always tappable so a corrected mistake (clock-in twice in a row because the person forgot they already did it) still goes through and lets the daily-summary recompute decide. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ A small "ŌåÉ Not me, start over" link at the bottom for false matches.*

![Kiosk ŌĆö Success stage with the recorded time in gold](../assets/screenshots/29_kiosk/success.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ A pop-in cobalt check-mark medallion, the direction label ("CLOCKED IN"), the employee's name, and the `employee_no` in monospace. ┬Ę ŌØĘ The wall-clock time of the recorded punch in 40-pt gold, monospaced and tabular ŌĆö the only piece of information the employee needs to remember (for their own records, or to complain about a delay). ┬Ę ŌØĖ "Have a productive day." closing line and a "Done ŌĆö next person" link. The page auto-resets to the Identify stage after six seconds, but the link is there for the person who clocks out at 17:01 and wants to walk away without staring at their own time on the screen.*

> The three screenshot files referenced above will be captured in Wave 1 (task W1.29). Until then the build will substitute a "missing image" placeholder ŌĆö that is expected and does not break the build.

## Every button, every action

### Identify stage (`/kiosk`, default view)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Employee ID input** | Free text, max 20 chars on the client and max 50 on the server. The on-screen keyboard types into whichever field is "active"; tapping the field with a finger sets it active, as does the Tab key. | Anyone walking up to the device | The matching primary key. Case-insensitive on the backend so `GH-HR-001` and `gh-hr-001` both match the same row. |
| **Name input** | Free text, max 60 chars on the client and max 120 on the server. Shift starts on (capitalising the first letter of the first word), then flips off after the first letter; tapping space turns it back on for the next word (so "Ama Asante" comes out right by default). | Same | The "proof you typed an ID that's yours" step. A loose substring match against the user's full name ŌĆö see the Data section. |
| **On-screen QWERTY keyboard** | Four rows of keys (number row + Q-row + A-row + Z-row) plus shift, backspace, clear, Tab, space, Enter. `mousedown.prevent` on every key keeps focus on the active input so the caret stays put and a tapped key always goes into the right field. Shift is "caps-once" ŌĆö typing a letter while shift is on flips it off automatically, matching the convention of every other touch keyboard the user has ever met. | Same | Wall-mounted tablets often have no hardware keyboard. An on-screen one is the difference between "usable at reception" and "needs a Bluetooth dongle". |
| **Tab Ōćä key** | Switches the active field between Employee ID and Name. | Same | Faster than reaching out and tapping the other field, especially on tablets that are mounted above arm-comfort height. |
| **Clear key** | Empties the active field (not both). | Same | Single-mistake undo without resetting the other field. |
| **Backspace key** | Removes the last character from the active field. | Same | Standard. |
| **Shift key** | Toggles caps. Highlights in gold when on. | Same | "ATM-style" name entry for compound surnames where the user wants to enter the second word capitalised differently. |
| **Enter key (cobalt)** | Submits both fields to `POST /kiosk/verify`. Identical to clicking the "Continue ŌåÆ" button. A hardware keyboard's Enter key fires the same handler via `@keydown.enter` on each input. | Same | Single muscle-memory submit, regardless of input method. |
| **Continue ŌåÆ** (cobalt CTA at the bottom) | Same as Enter ŌĆö submits to `/kiosk/verify`. The button text flips to "VerifyingŌĆ”" while the request is in flight and the button is disabled to prevent double-submit. | Same | Big finger target for the second-most-common path (tap-keyboard, then tap-CTA rather than tap-Enter). |
| **Inline error** (magenta) | Appears in place under the keyboard when the server returns `ok: false` from `/kiosk/verify`, or when the client-side guard catches an empty field. Disappears as soon as the user touches a key. | Same | The mistake is almost always a typo; the message ("Employee ID or name did not match. Please try again.") is deliberately vague to avoid revealing whether the ID exists or only the name was wrong (anti-enumeration). |

> *Notes:* Both inputs have `autocomplete="off"` so a shared device does not autofill with the previous user's name. The page never stores typed values in `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or a cookie ŌĆö when the auto-reset timer fires (or "Done ŌĆö next person" is tapped), `resetFlow()` zeros every reactive ref and the next person walks up to a blank form. The Employee ID input has `tracking-[0.18em]` monospace so a long ID is easy to read at arm's length without leaning in.

### Confirm stage

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Identity panel** (avatar + name + position + ID) | Read-only confirmation block. The avatar is the same `avatar_url` shown everywhere else in the app; if the employee has no avatar, the initial of their first name shows in a gradient circle. | Anyone (whoever just identified themselves) | The visual half of the identity check ŌĆö the face and name on screen versus the person standing in front of it. This is the kiosk's authentication: a wrong typed name might still pass the substring match, but a wrong face won't pass the eyes of the person standing next in line. |
| **"Last today" hint** | "Last today: IN at 08:14" or "No events recorded today yet." Computed by `presentEmployee()` from the most recent record on today's date for this employee. | Same | Direction hinting. The employee glances at it and knows whether they're meant to clock in or out ŌĆö important after lunch, especially for shift workers who forget which side of break they're on. |
| **Clock In** (large cobalt button) | Posts `{ employee_no, name, direction: 'in' }` to `/kiosk/clock`. On 200 OK with `ok: true`, the page flips to the Success stage. Has a gold ring (`[data-recommended="true"]`) when the suggested direction is "in". | Same | The headline action. Big finger target, high colour contrast, gold ring when it's the expected next move. |
| **Clock Out** (large ghost button) | Posts the same payload with `direction: 'out'`. Same flow, same ring when it's the suggested direction. | Same | The other half of the pair. Both are always tappable so a self-correcting employee ("oh, I already clocked in") can do the right thing without being blocked. |
| **ŌåÉ Not me, start over** (link) | Calls `resetFlow()` ŌĆö zeros the typed values, returns to the Identify stage. | Same | False-match escape hatch. The loose substring match described in the Data section can occasionally surface the wrong employee (two people both named "Ama" in the same office); this is the way out without waiting six seconds. |
| **Inline error** (magenta) | Same as on the Identify stage ŌĆö shows the server message from `/kiosk/clock` if the request fails. Does not eat the form state; the user can retry the same button. | Same | Same as above ŌĆö usually a transient network or rate-limit error; the user just taps the button again. |

> *Notes:* The Confirm stage does not auto-advance and does not auto-reset ŌĆö it waits for a tap on In, Out, or "Not me". The reason: someone whose name resolved correctly might still be deciding which button to press (the canonical case is "I just got in but need to step back out before counting as on-the-clock"). The Clock In button intentionally remains tappable even when "Clock Out" is the suggested direction; the daily-summary recompute is the canonical reconciliation, and double-in or double-out punches at the kiosk are recovered there rather than blocked here.

### Success stage

| Element | What it does | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|
| **Check medallion** | A 4.5 rem cobalt circle with a tick, pop-in via a 450 ms cubic-bezier transform. | Single reassuring visual ŌĆö the punch is in. |
| **"Clocked IN" / "Clocked OUT" eyebrow** | The recorded direction in lowercase except for the verb. | Names the action that just happened. |
| **Employee name** | Full name in 40-pt bold. | Confirmation. |
| **Employee number** | Monospace, muted. | The audit handle. |
| **Recorded time** | The wall-clock time of the punch in 40-pt gold, monospaced, tabular numerals. Sourced from the `record.event_at` field returned by the server, not the client clock, so it is the same time payroll will see. | The one piece of information the employee leaves with. The gold and the tabular-nums treatment make it readable from the back of a queue. |
| **"Have a productive day."** | A closing line in tiny uppercase. | Human courtesy. |
| **"Done ŌĆö next person"** (link) | Cancels the auto-reset and immediately flips the page back to Identify. | Lets the next person in the queue start without waiting six seconds. |

> *Notes:* The Success stage has a `setTimeout` (six seconds) that calls `resetFlow()` when it expires. Any tap on "Done ŌĆö next person" clears the same timer (`clearTimeout`) before resetting, so the page does not accidentally double-fire the reset. The page does not navigate, refresh, or change URL during any of this ŌĆö Inertia is not involved; the three stages are reactive state in a single Vue component. That means the page is offline-safe between renders: a momentary network drop after `/kiosk/clock` returns 200 does not prevent the Success stage from showing.

## The data behind it

Five HTTP routes, all prefixed `/kiosk`, all unauthenticated, all rate-limited at **60 requests per minute per IP** (`throttle:60,1`):

| Route | Method | Returns | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `kiosk.show` | `GET /kiosk` | Inertia page (`Kiosk/Index`) + `serverTime` | The entry point. `serverTime` seeds the live clock so the first second on screen is the server's time, not the device's. |
| `kiosk.recent` | `GET /kiosk/recent` | JSON `{ recent: [...], serverTime }` | Polled every 15 seconds by the open page. Returns up to 8 of today's kiosk punches in descending time order, projecting only **first name + direction + event_at**. Staff IDs, last names, and positions are deliberately omitted ŌĆö see "Public wall privacy" below. |
| `kiosk.verify` | `POST /kiosk/verify` | JSON `{ ok, employee?, message? }` | Identity check only ŌĆö no record is written. Returns the matched employee (name, position, avatar URL, last event today, suggested direction) on success; returns 422 with `ok: false` on miss. |
| `kiosk.clock` | `POST /kiosk/clock` | JSON `{ ok, employee?, record?, message? }` | The actual write. Calls `AttendanceService::record()` with `source = AttendanceSource::WebKiosk` and `eventAt = now()`. No device ID, no GPS coordinates, no reason. Returns 422 with `ok: false` on any `DomainException` from the service. |
| `kiosk.face` | `POST /kiosk/face` | JSON `{ ok: false, message }` with status **501 Not Implemented** | Future face-recognition path. The route exists so device firmware and the frontend can wire against it without a backend redeploy; the implementation lands when the Ghana-Card biometric stack from Chapter 25 is wired in. |

What every reader of the screen needs to keep in mind:

- **No authentication. None.** The kiosk routes sit outside every auth middleware in the `web` group. The "authentication" for a punch is the loose substring match between the typed name and the employee's `users.name`. This is appropriate for the threat model ŌĆö the device is on the office LAN, the screen is visible to everyone, false punches are visible on the live wall, and the daily-summary recompute is the canonical reconciliation. It would not be appropriate for any high-trust action (no payroll changes, no leave approvals, no document downloads); the kiosk is intentionally limited to write-one-row-into-attendance-records.
- **The identity match is "loose substring", not exact equals.** `KioskController::matchEmployee()` lowercases and trims both sides, then runs `str_contains($fullName, $typedName)`. "Ama" matches "Ama Asante"; "Asante" matches it too; "A" does not (the controller enforces a 2-character minimum to avoid trivial matches like "a" matching every employee). This is forgiveness-by-design ŌĆö staff with diacritics in their official names (Akua Adoma vs the legal "Akua AdŪØma") can still log in by typing the everyday spelling.
- **The same `AttendanceService::record()` runs as for every other source.** Kiosk gets no shortcuts: the service still writes one immutable `attendance_records` row, then synchronously calls `recomputeDailySummary()` to refresh the day's `attendance_summaries` row, then calls `OvertimeCalculator` for Labour Act ┬¦35 overtime, then statuses the day Present / Late / Half-Day / Absent / On-Leave / Holiday. The kiosk gets exactly the same lateness grace period, exactly the same overtime rates, and exactly the same payroll downstream as a biometric punch from a fingerprint device.
- **Geofence is not triggered on kiosk punches.** The Attendance service's geofence rejection (Chapter 5) only fires when the call passes a `deviceId` together with `geoLat`/`geoLng`. `KioskController::clock()` passes none of those, so the haversine check is skipped. The kiosk's location guarantee is physical ŌĆö the device is bolted to the wall in the room where staff are supposed to be ŌĆö not GPS. Phase 2 will tie the kiosk to a registered `BiometricDevice` row (with stored coordinates + a shared secret) so the same geofence rule can apply; that work is tracked in the punchlist alongside the face-recognition path.
- **Public wall privacy.** The "Today's wall" pill list at the bottom of the kiosk shows only `first_name + direction + event_at`. There is a dedicated test (`KioskRecentTest::it exposes only first name + direction + event_at ŌĆö no Staff ID or full name`) that asserts the response payload does not carry `employee_no`, `name`, or `position`. This is deliberate: anyone within sight of the wall-mounted device ŌĆö visitors, contractors, the person standing in the queue ŌĆö must not be able to harvest staff IDs or surnames off the screen.
- **The wall is also a liveness indicator.** Because the recent-punches endpoint is polled every 15 seconds and renders pills as they arrive, the absence of new pills on a busy morning is a visible signal that something has gone wrong with the device or the network ŌĆö IT does not need a separate uptime monitor for "is the kiosk working" beyond walking past the device.
- **Auto-reset and no persistence.** No typed value survives beyond the active flow. `resetFlow()` is called automatically six seconds after a successful punch, manually by "ŌåÉ Not me, start over", and manually by "Done ŌĆö next person"; every call zeros `employeeNo`, `fullName`, `errorMessage`, `matched`, `lastRecord`, sets `activeField` back to the ID field, and turns shift back on. No `localStorage`, no `sessionStorage`, no cookies. The kiosk's working memory is its current Vue state and nothing else.
- **Rate limit is per-IP, not per-employee.** `throttle:60,1` means each IP can hit the kiosk routes 60 times a minute. On a single shared device this is generous ŌĆö a punch takes at least a few seconds of typing ŌĆö but it does mean that an attacker who can reach the URL on the LAN cannot brute-force the substring match faster than once per second on average. Combined with the 2-character minimum on the name field and the anti-enumeration "Employee ID or name did not match" generic error, the kiosk is not a usable channel for harvesting employee names.

## How it talks to other modules

- **Attendance (Ch 5)** is the only downstream consumer. Every kiosk clock hits `AttendanceService::record()` with `source = AttendanceSource::WebKiosk` and is reconciled into the same `attendance_summaries` row that the org-wide register, the personal "My Attendance" card, and the payroll engine all read from. There is no separate "kiosk records" table; the kiosk is a thin shell over Attendance.
- **Employees (Ch 3)** is the lookup target. The match runs against `employees.employee_no` joined to `users.name`, the same two columns every other identity lookup in the system uses; there is no kiosk-specific identifier or alias table.
- **Identity Verification (Ch 25)** is the planned upgrade path. The `/kiosk/face` route already exists as a 501 stub so the device firmware and the frontend can wire against it without a backend redeploy; when the Ghana-Card biometric SDK from Chapter 25 is integrated, the route turns into a real handler that takes a face image, matches it against the NIA template, and calls the same `AttendanceService::record()` with `source = WebKiosk` and a confidence score on the audit trail. The contract is already designed; only the SDK is missing.
- **Payroll (Ch 19)** never reads from the kiosk directly. It reads `attendance_summaries.overtime_hours`, which is recomputed every time a kiosk punch lands. The "punch on kiosk ŌåÆ flows into payroll" link is end-to-end through Attendance.
- **Biometric webhook** (Chapter 5) is the sibling channel for hardware fingerprint / face devices. Both write to the same `attendance_records` table via the same `AttendanceService::record()`; they differ only in their `source` value and in whether they carry a `device_id` + geo coordinates (the webhook does; the kiosk does not).
- **Notifications (Ch 16)** is intentionally not wired. The kiosk does not email or message anyone on a punch ŌĆö the live wall + the success screen + the next render of the My Attendance page are the only notifications the punch triggers. A future "clocked-out without clocking-in" exception alert is roadmapped through the standard analytics-event listener path, not as a kiosk-specific feature.

## Standards touchpoints

- **Ghana Labour Act, 2003 (Act 651) ┬¦33-39 ŌĆö Hours of work and overtime.** The kiosk is a recording channel, not a calculation engine; the calculation it feeds into is governed by `OvertimeCalculator` in Attendance (Chapter 5), which implements the ┬¦35 three-tier overtime rule. The kiosk's contribution to the standard is that it provides a recording option for workplaces without biometric devices or per-employee browsers ŌĆö making the ┬¦33 weekly-hours obligation enforceable in field offices that would otherwise have to fall back on paper sign-in sheets. See Chapter 44.
- **Cybersecurity Act, 2020 (Act 1038) ┬¦59 ŌĆö Critical information infrastructure protection.** Shared-device terminals are an attractive lateral-movement target on the office LAN; the kiosk is therefore deliberately minimal in attack surface ŌĆö five routes, no authenticated state, no admin actions, rate-limited at the route layer, no persistence in the browser, and a payload contract that omits Staff IDs and surnames from the public wall. The Phase 2 hardening work (device fingerprinting, per-kiosk shared secrets matched to a `BiometricDevice` row, IP allow-listing per location) is tracked in the same punchlist as the face-recognition path.
- **WCAG 2.1 AA ŌĆö Web Content Accessibility Guidelines.** The kiosk's on-screen keyboard, large text, high-contrast palette, big tap targets (Clock In / Clock Out are each ~144 px tall on a tablet), and live `role="alert"` announcement of the error message are the WCAG touchpoints for a shared-device interface that must work for someone with low vision, motor-impaired hands, or arthritis. The auto-reset timer of six seconds is on the long side of the recommended 3-5 second range deliberately ŌĆö the standard's "Enough Time" guideline (┬¦2.2.1) is honoured by giving users time to read the success screen before it disappears.
- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦18 ŌĆö Data minimisation principle.** The public wall's contract ŌĆö `first_name + direction + event_at`, with `employee_no`, `name`, and `position` actively excluded by the controller and asserted-out by a dedicated test ŌĆö is the ┬¦18 touchpoint: only the minimum personal data necessary for the social-proof and liveness purposes leaves the device. See Chapter 26 for the full DPA mapping.

## What's planned next

Phase 2 of the government-grade roadmap (Chapter 46) lands four things on this module: (1) **Face recognition** ŌĆö the existing `/kiosk/face` 501 stub turns into a working endpoint once the Ghana-Card biometric SDK from Chapter 25 is integrated, replacing the typed-name step with a camera frame and confidence-score check; (2) **Device registration** ŌĆö every kiosk gets a registered `BiometricDevice` row (with stored coordinates, a HMAC `shared_secret`, and a `last_seen_at` heartbeat field), so the same geofence rule that protects the biometric webhook applies to kiosk punches and IT can see at-a-glance which terminals are online; (3) **Offline buffer** ŌĆö a service-worker-cached version of the page that queues punches in IndexedDB during a network drop and replays them on reconnection, so a temporary office outage does not lose punches; (4) **Per-employee PIN fallback** ŌĆö for workplaces that prefer not to display the typed-name match on screen, an opt-in 4-digit PIN issued via the staff-phone-PIN flow in Chapter 25, swapping the name input for a PIN pad. The screen-level gap ŌĆö a dedicated admin page for IT to provision and revoke registered kiosks ŌĆö is tracked in the same wave; the routes and service layer are already in place.

<!-- ===== 30_departments.md ===== -->

# Chapter 30 ŌĆö Departments

> *In one paragraph.* Departments is the organisational chart of the institute ŌĆö every operating unit the workforce hangs off, with a unique code, a name, and (when set) a head. Every Employee row in Chapter 3 belongs to exactly one. Establishment positions in Chapter 8 roll up to one. Leave approvals in Chapter 4 route up through one. Payroll cost in Chapter 19 pivots on one. Reports in Chapter 31 group by one. In the MVP the structure is **flat** ŌĆö there is no parent/child tree, no nested org chart ŌĆö because every dashboard, RBAC scope, and aggregate query in the system works against a single `department_id` foreign key and a hierarchy would change the shape of every one of them. The Phase 1 establishment work (Ch 8) already gives us the org-chart shape via `positions.reports_to_position_id`; departments stay flat by design.

## Where to find it

- **Sidebar location:** there are two distinct entry points and they mean different things. The **Workforce** group ŌåÆ **Employees** page exposes a "Departments" button in its header that links to `/departments` ŌĆö the **admin register** documented in this chapter, where HR creates, edits, and deletes departments. Separately, the sidebar has a **Departments** expandable group (Settings ŌåÆ Departments) that lists eight **department portals** (IT, HR, Marketing, Finance, Membership, PCP, CPD, Administration) ŌĆö one Vue page per slug, each gated by its own `portal.*` permission. The two are *not* the same screen: the admin register is for shape-of-the-org work; the portal page is the dashboard the department itself looks at every morning.
- **Roles that see it:**
    - **super_admin** and **ceo** ŌĆö every department, every action, including delete.
    - **hr_admin** ŌĆö `employees.manage` is the load-bearing permission; HR creates, edits, and deletes departments and sees the headcount tile for every one.
    - **dept_head** ŌĆö `employees.view` lets them see the register read-only via the policy's `viewAny`/`view`; for the department(s) they head, `User::managesDepartment($id)` flips `update` true so they can rename or re-describe their own unit. They do *not* get `create` or `destroy` ŌĆö only HR adds or removes departments.
    - **manager** ŌĆö no register access in the MVP. They see their own department on the Employees directory filter dropdown; they do not reach `/departments`.
    - **employee** ŌĆö no register access. They see their own department label on the Profile portal (Ch 16).
    - **finance_officer / auditor / it_support** ŌĆö no register access by default. Portal access is granted by the orthogonal `portal.<slug>` permissions ŌĆö a finance officer typically holds `portal.finance` and lands on the Finance portal directly; an IT staffer holds `portal.it`.
- **Related modules:** Employees (Ch 3) ŌĆö every employee carries a `department_id` and the Employees directory's "All Departments" dropdown is keyed off this register; Establishment (Ch 8) ŌĆö every Position rolls up to one department and the `(department, grade, fiscal_year)` ceiling triple is keyed on it; Leave (Ch 4) ŌĆö approval routing climbs to the department head when an employee's direct manager is unset; Payroll (Ch 19) ŌĆö pay registers and GIFMIS exports group cost by department; Reports (Ch 31) ŌĆö every workforce, leave, and payroll report has a "by department" pivot; RBAC (Ch 28) ŌĆö the `user_roles.department_id` column scopes a role to a single department, which is how a manager becomes a *dept_head over Finance* without holding institute-wide HR permissions.

## The screens

![Departments register ŌĆö masthead, three-up card grid, hover-revealed row actions](../assets/screenshots/30_departments/index.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Page masthead ŌĆö "Departmental Structure" eyebrow, "Departments" headline, and a one-line description ("Institutional organogram ŌĆö operating units of record with headcount distribution"). The Directory button (top-right) jumps to the Employees module; the cobalt "Add Department" CTA opens the right-hand slide panel. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Department card ŌĆö a 1.5 px coloured top stripe (six-gradient pool keyed off the department id), a square icon tile in the same gradient, the department code in monospace top-right, the department name in bold, the description (clamped to two lines), and a footer strip with the active-staff count and a "View ŌåÆ" hover affordance. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Hover actions ŌĆö two small icon buttons (Edit, Delete) appear in the top-right corner of each card when the user moves a mouse onto it or tabs into it; both stop event propagation so they don't trigger the card-level "View employees" click. The card body itself is a single button that navigates to the Employees directory with the department filter pre-applied.*

![Add / Edit Department ŌĆö slide panel with three fields](../assets/screenshots/30_departments/create.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Slide panel title flips between "Add Department" and "Edit {name}" based on whether the panel was opened from the Add Department CTA or from a card's edit icon ŌĆö one Vue form serves both flows, the only difference is whether `editing.value` is null. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Three fields only ŌĆö Department Name (required, free text), Department Code (required, 2ŌĆō10 chars, uppercased on input), Description (optional, three-row textarea). ┬Ę ŌØĖ Save / Cancel footer ŌĆö the cobalt CTA reads "Save Department" on create and "Update Department" on edit, both with a spinner state while the form is processing.*

![Department portal ŌĆö hero, KPI strip, three section cards, four cross-links](../assets/screenshots/30_departments/portal_finance.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Hero strip ŌĆö cobalt gradient background with a per-portal accent glow (cyan for IT, magenta for HR, soft-sky for Marketing, indigo for Finance, etc.), the portal icon in a tinted tile, the "Department Portal" eyebrow, the portal title and one-line tagline, and an "Active" pill with a pulsing dot in the accent colour. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Four KPI tiles ŌĆö pinned to the top of the portal; the values are **illustrative** in the MVP (the IT portal shows "Servers Online 23/24"; the HR portal shows "Headcount 1,284"; the Finance portal shows "Monthly Revenue GHS 8.7M"). The numbers are hard-coded constants in `Departments/Show.vue` and are read as placeholders until the matching backend lands. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Three section cards below the KPIs ŌĆö each portal carries its own three-section narrative (IT: Infrastructure / Security / Integrations; HR: Recruitment / Learning / Engagement; Finance: Payroll Cycle / Audit / Statutory Reports; etc.). ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Four cross-link tiles at the bottom ŌĆö Department Roster (jumps to Employees), Service Desk (jumps to Tickets), Reports (jumps to the Reports module), and a Configure button that opens a "coming soon" toast.*

> The three screenshot files referenced above will be captured in Wave 1 (task W1.30). Until then the build will substitute a "missing image" placeholder ŌĆö that is expected and does not break the build.

## Every button, every action

### Departments register (`/departments`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Directory** (top-right link) | Inertia-links to the Employees directory (`/employees`). | Anyone who can see the register (`employees.manage`) | The register is the *back of house* of the Employees module; the Directory button is the one-click way back to the front of house. |
| **Add Department** (cobalt CTA, top-right) | Opens the right-hand slide panel with the form reset and `editing.value = null`. Submitting posts to `POST /departments` via the shared `submit()` handler. | `employees.manage` | One-step creation ŌĆö HR shouldn't have to leave the page to add a department it noticed was missing while reviewing the org chart. |
| **Department card body** (whole card, clickable) | Navigates to `/employees?department_id={id}` so the directory opens already filtered to that department. | Anyone who can see the register | Whole-card hit target is the fastest way to "show me the people in this unit"; the per-card "View ŌåÆ" affordance fades in on hover to confirm the click target. |
| **Edit** (pencil icon, top-right of each card, hover-revealed) | Opens the same slide panel with the form pre-filled and `editing.value` set to the department. The CTA label flips to "Update Department" and submitting PATCHes to `/departments/{id}`. | `employees.manage` or dept_head over that department (`DepartmentPolicy::update`) | Edit-in-place ŌĆö no separate detail page, no double click. The hover-reveal keeps the card scannable when not interacting. |
| **Delete** (trash icon, top-right of each card, hover-revealed) | Opens a `window.confirm` dialog. If the department has any active employees, the dialog warns with the headcount ("Finance has 12 active employee(s). Re-assign them first; the deletion will be refused otherwise."). On confirm, DELETEs to `/departments/{id}`. The server-side service refuses with a flash error if the headcount is non-zero; if the count is zero, the row soft-deletes. | `employees.manage` (the controller checks; the policy currently has no `delete` method, so HR-only by route middleware) | A department with people on it cannot be deleted ŌĆö re-assigning twelve employees is a multi-step task the UI shouldn't try to do silently. The confirm-then-refuse pattern is honest: the user knows up front the call may be rejected, and the rejection comes back as a clear flash error rather than a 500. |
| **Department card stats footer** | Read-only ŌĆö active staff count (`active_employee_count`), the people icon, and a hover-revealed "View ŌåÆ" arrow. | Anyone who can see the register | The five-second answer to "how big is this department?" without leaving the page. The count comes from `EmployeeService::listDepartments()` which uses `withCount(['employees as active_employee_count' => fn ($q) => $q->active()])` so a single query feeds every card. |
| **Empty state** (when zero departments exist) | A friendly card with the building icon, the "No departments yet" headline, a one-line description, and an "Add Department" button that opens the same slide panel as the header CTA. | Anyone who can see the register | First-run UX ŌĆö a brand-new institute landing on this page sees a clear next step rather than an empty page. |

> *Notes:* `EmployeeController::departments` returns `DepartmentResource::collection($this->employees->listDepartments())`, which is a single eager-loaded query ŌĆö there is no N+1 between the cards. The page is **not paginated** today: the list returns every department in one render, on the assumption that an institute has dozens, not thousands, of departments. If an institute ever crosses (say) 100 departments, the controller will need to switch to `paginate(48)` and the Vue page will need a pagination strip ŌĆö neither is in the MVP. The page is also **not searchable** in the MVP ŌĆö the grid is short enough to scan; if it gets long enough to need search, the same filter strip pattern from the Employees directory will be lifted into this page. The card gradient pool (six cool-family gradients) is keyed by `dept.id % 6`, so a department's accent colour is stable across renders without the database carrying a `color` column.

### Create / Edit slide panel

| Field | Validation | Who can set it | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Department Name** | required, string, max 255 chars, unique on `departments.name` | `employees.manage` (create); `employees.manage` *or* dept_head over the department (edit) | Free text. The UI does not enforce a Title Case convention but most institutes adopt one ŌĆö "Finance", "Human Resources", "IT & Technology". The unique constraint exists at the DB level (`departments.name` carries a UNIQUE index from the create migration); the FormRequest does *not* re-validate uniqueness on edit today, which means a manual rename can theoretically hit the DB constraint and surface as an integrity-error flash rather than a clean per-field error message. Phase 1 (W1.30) tightens the UpdateDepartment FormRequest to add `unique:departments,name,{$id}` so the validation message lands on the field. |
| **Department Code** | required, string, max 20 chars (create) or max 20 (update), unique on `departments.code` on create | Same | The load-bearing identifier ŌĆö `EmployeeController` and the static portal page both look departments up by code, not by name. Codes are uppercased client-side via a `uppercase` Tailwind class and `maxlength="10"` on the input. Audits, GIFMIS exports, and the portal slug-to-department map all key on code, so once an institute is in production the code should not change. The same `unique` caveat as Name applies to edit: the update FormRequest accepts `code` without the per-row exclusion, so a code rename can hit the DB constraint. Phase 1 fix list. |
| **Description** | optional, string, max 1000 chars (create) or max 500 (update) | Same | Free-form. Surfaces on the card body, clamped to two lines via Tailwind's `line-clamp-2` utility. Long descriptions render in full on the detail screen ŌĆö but the MVP has no detail screen; the card *is* the detail screen. |
| **Save Department / Update Department** (cobalt CTA) | Submits the form. On create, POSTs to `/departments`; on edit, PATCHes to `/departments/{id}`. Both `preserveScroll: true` on success so the grid doesn't jump to the top. | The same RBAC trio | The CTA label flips based on `editing.value`. The `:disabled="form.processing"` plus the spinner icon make the in-flight state visible. |
| **Cancel** | Closes the panel and resets `editing.value` to null. | Anyone | Standard escape. |

> *Notes:* The `StoreDepartmentRequest` FormRequest is the only one of the three with proper validation rules (name required + max:255, code required + max:20 + unique, description nullable + max:1000); the update path uses inline `$request->validate()` in `EmployeeController::updateDepartment` rather than a dedicated `UpdateDepartmentRequest`. The two diverge on `description` max length (1000 vs 500) and on `code` requirement (required on create, nullable on update). Both are honest current behaviour, not design choices ŌĆö the Phase 1 hardening (W1.30) consolidates them into one `UpdateDepartmentRequest` with the same rules as create. The destroy path uses the service-layer `\DomainException` pattern documented in Ch 3: `EmployeeService::deleteDepartment` counts active employees and throws if any are still assigned. The controller catches the exception and re-renders the previous page with a flash error.

### Reassign department head (not in the UI today)

| Action | What it would do | Who would use it | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reassign head** | PATCH `departments.head_user_id` to a `users.id`. Existing head's `headedDepartments()` collection drops the row; new head's collection picks it up; `User::managedDepartmentIds()` cache invalidates on next read (60-second TTL); `DepartmentPolicy::update` and `DepartmentPolicy::view` start returning true for the new head. | `employees.manage` | **Not in the MVP UI.** The `departments.head_user_id` column was added by the RBAC migration on 2026-05-15, and `User::headedDepartments()` and `User::managesDepartment()` read it correctly. But the slide-panel form in `Departments/Index.vue` does not include a head picker yet, and `StoreDepartmentRequest` does not list `head_user_id` in its rules. The field is settable today only via Tinker or a direct PATCH from an API consumer. The UI work ŌĆö a head picker dropdown in the slide panel scoped to `users.role IN (manager, dept_head, hr_admin)` plus a "Currently head: {name}" row on the card ŌĆö is on the Phase 1 backlog as W1.30b. The RBAC engine already does the right thing the moment the column is set; only the UI is missing. |

### Department portal (`/departments/portal/{slug}`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active pill** | Read-only ŌĆö a pulsing dot in the portal's accent colour next to an "Active" label. | Anyone who can see the portal (`portal.<slug>`) | Visual confirmation the portal is live; in Phase 1 this will flip to "Maintenance" when the underlying service is in a degraded state. |
| **KPI tile** ├Ś4 | Read-only ŌĆö each tile shows a label, a value, a sub-label, and a coloured icon. Values are **hard-coded constants** in the MVP. | Anyone who can see the portal | The MVP ships the visual layout and the per-portal narrative; the values will be back-filled by the matching module's stats endpoint as each backend lands (IT/Tickets in Ch 11 already does; Finance pulls from Ch 19 payroll once the portal endpoint is added in W1.30c). |
| **Section card** ├Ś3 | Read-only narrative blocks ŌĆö three per portal, each with an icon, a short title, and a one-paragraph body. | Same | Same logic as the KPI tiles ŌĆö the narrative is the shipping contract for the visual; the live data will land later. |
| **Department Roster** (cross-link tile) | Inertia-links to the Employees directory (`/employees`). Does *not* pre-filter to the portal's department in the MVP ŌĆö that's an open polish item. | Same | The most-clicked tile from a portal page in usability testing was always "who works here?"; surfacing it as a top-level cross-link beats forcing the user to find Employees in the sidebar. |
| **Service Desk** (cross-link tile) | Inertia-links to `/tickets`. | Same | The second-most-clicked. |
| **Reports** (cross-link tile) | Inertia-links to `/reports`. | Same | Department heads pivot reports by their own department; a one-click jump to the Reports module is the fastest path. |
| **Configure** (cross-link tile) | Fires the `useToast().comingSoon()` helper, which surfaces a "Configure {portal title} settings ŌĆö coming soon" toast. | Same | Honest placeholder. The Phase 1 plan replaces this with a real per-portal preferences sheet (default filters, pinned widgets, alert thresholds) once two or more institutes have asked for one. |

> *Notes:* The portal page is a single shared Vue file (`Departments/Show.vue`) driven by a static `PORTALS` constant keyed on the URL slug. All eight portals ŌĆö IT, HR, Marketing, Finance, Membership, PCP, CPD, Administration ŌĆö render from the same file with different palette/icon/KPI/section data. The `StaticPageController::department` action does try to load the matching real `Department` row by code (`'it' ŌåÆ 'IT'`, `'hr' ŌåÆ 'HR'`, etc.) so the page can show the head's name, but the rendered view ignores that field today and reads only `portal.title` / `portal.tagline` / `portal.kpis`. The two halves ŌĆö real department record + static portal narrative ŌĆö converge in Phase 1 (W1.30c) when each portal's KPI tile is wired to a real stats endpoint scoped to the matching department.

## The data behind it

The departments register is the simplest table in the schema ŌĆö a single row per operating unit:

- **`departments`** ŌĆö `id`, `name` (string, unique), `code` (string, unique), `description` (text, nullable), `head_user_id` (foreign key to `users.id`, nullable, `nullOnDelete`), `deleted_at` (soft-delete timestamp), and the standard `created_at` / `updated_at` audit columns. The table was created on 2026-05-12 with the first three fields, gained `deleted_at` from the all-tables soft-deletes migration on 2026-05-13, and gained `head_user_id` from the RBAC migration on 2026-05-15. There is **no `parent_id`** ŌĆö the structure is intentionally flat.

The relationships that hang off it:

- **`employees.department_id`** ŌĆö one Employee belongs to one Department. Deleting a department is blocked while any active employee is still assigned to it (the `DomainException` pattern documented in Ch 3); the count of active employees is also rendered on every card so the UI can warn before the call.
- **`departments.head_user_id` ŌåÆ `users.id`** ŌĆö one department has at most one head. The relationship is exposed on the User side as `User::headedDepartments()` (one user can head zero or many departments ŌĆö concurrent heads are rare but legal) and on the Department side as `Department::head()`. `head_user_id` is nullable, so a department without a head is a legitimate state (typically during a transition).
- **`user_roles.department_id`** ŌĆö the RBAC pivot can scope a role to a department. A user with a `dept_head` role pivot carrying `department_id = 5` is a department head over department 5 *via the RBAC pivot* even if they are not the `head_user_id` on the row. Both pathways feed `User::managedDepartmentIds()` and either one is sufficient for `User::managesDepartment(5)` to return true.
- **`positions.department_id`** ŌĆö every approved post rolls up to one department (Ch 8). Deleting a department is also blocked by the establishment side: the position service refuses to leave orphaned posts behind.
- **`establishment_ceilings.department_id`** ŌĆö the `(department, grade, fiscal_year)` ceiling triple is keyed on the department row. Deleting a department orphans the ceiling rows; the soft-delete approach (rather than a hard delete) is what keeps the ceiling history intact for audit.

What every reader of the screen needs to keep in mind:

- **The structure is flat.** There is no parent department, no nested hierarchy, no recursive tree. The Phase 1 establishment module gives us *position-level* hierarchy via `positions.reports_to_position_id` (Ch 8); the department layer above is deliberately one level deep. An institute that needs a divisional layer above departments models it on the position tree, not by adding a `parent_id` here. The reason is uniform ŌĆö every dashboard, every RBAC scope, and every aggregate query in the system reads `department_id` as a single FK. Adding `parent_id` would change the shape of every one of those queries (recursive descendants, transitive RBAC, cumulative roll-ups), and the cost-vs-benefit has consistently come out against the change.
- **`head_user_id` is the operational owner; RBAC `user_roles.department_id` is the scoping mechanism.** The two overlap but they are not the same field. Setting `head_user_id` makes the user the headcount-display owner on the org chart and gives them `update` on the Department row through the policy; attaching a `dept_head` role pivot scoped to the department gives them the *operational permission set* (leave approvals, the dept-head dashboard, scoped employee visibility). Most institutes do both, and the RBAC seeder sets both when a head is appointed via the HR flow. They are kept separate so that a finance officer scoped to the Finance department for *payroll* reasons does not accidentally inherit "head of Finance" semantics on the org chart.
- **Code is the load-bearing identifier.** GIFMIS exports, audit findings, the static-portal slug map, and every cross-module foreign-key trace use the department code (not the id, not the name). Renaming a department in the UI is fine ŌĆö the code stays. Changing the code is a multi-system migration and the MVP UI does not warn about it; Phase 1 adds a "Code change ŌåÆ audit cascade" sheet that lists every downstream system that needs updating.
- **Delete is a soft delete.** The `SoftDeletes` trait on the model means `DELETE /departments/{id}` writes `deleted_at` and removes the row from every list, but does not erase it. A deleted department can be restored from the console if needed; the audit trail of "who worked there in 2026" remains queryable. The "Cannot delete department: N employee(s) still assigned" flash error is the *first* line of defence; the soft delete itself is the second.

## How it talks to other modules

- **Employees (Chapter 3)** ŌĆö every employee carries a `department_id` FK. The Employees directory's "All Departments" filter dropdown is populated by `EmployeeService::listDepartments()` ŌĆö the same call the register itself uses, so the two are always in sync. `Employee::scopeVisibleTo($user)` filters by `whereIn('department_id', $user->managedDepartmentIds())` for any non-HR viewer, which is how the same query returns "everyone" to HR and "my team" to a department head without a separate code path.
- **Establishment (Chapter 8)** ŌĆö every Position rolls up to one Department via `positions.department_id`; the `(department, grade, fiscal_year)` headcount ceiling is keyed on the department row. Deleting a department is blocked while any non-soft-deleted position is still assigned to it (the same `DomainException` pattern Employees uses). Phase 1's "approved vs filled" department-dashboard tile pulls its numbers by joining `positions` against `position_assignments` filtered to the department.
- **Leave (Chapter 4)** ŌĆö leave approval routing climbs from the employee ŌåÆ manager ŌåÆ department head. When `Employee::manager_id` is null, `LeaveRequestService::approverFor()` falls back to `Department::head()` so the request still has an approver. The notification template `LeaveRequestPending` carries the department name in the email subject for context.
- **Payroll (Chapter 19)** ŌĆö pay registers, payslip cohorts, and GIFMIS exports all group cost lines by `employees.department_id`. The payroll dashboard's "by department" stack is a direct `groupBy` on the same field. A department that has been soft-deleted but still has historical pay rows continues to surface in payroll exports for historical periods ŌĆö that is intentional; restating "who worked in Finance in March 2026" must remain answerable even after Finance is renamed or retired.
- **Reports (Chapter 31)** ŌĆö every workforce, leave, and payroll report has a "by department" pivot, driven by the same department register. The Auditor-General pack (Ch 24) groups establishment cost by department ├Ś funding source ├Ś grade, so an unbalanced (department, grade) ceiling is visible at a glance.
- **RBAC (Chapter 28)** ŌĆö `user_roles.department_id` scopes a role pivot to a single department. A user with the `dept_head` legacy role and a `user_roles` row with `department_id = 5` is a department head over department 5; they get `employees.view` scoped to their team, `leave.approve` scoped to their team's requests, and `tickets.manage` scoped to their team's tickets. The same user without the pivot (just the legacy role) has *no* department scope and *no* permissions ŌĆö the pivot is the load-bearing record.
- **Department portals (this chapter)** ŌĆö the eight slug-keyed portals (`/departments/portal/{it,hr,marketing,finance,membership,pcp,cpd,administration}`) render from `Departments/Show.vue` and each gate on a `portal.<slug>` permission. Real Department records are looked up by code when the slug matches the codeMap; the portal currently displays only the static narrative, but the wiring is there for Phase 1 to pull live KPIs from the matching backend module.
- **Profile Portal (Chapter 16)** ŌĆö the employee's department label is read straight from this register through the `Employee::department` belongs-to relation; no caching, so a department rename surfaces on the profile page on next render.

## Standards touchpoints

- **ISO 30414 ┬¦4.7 workforce planning** ŌĆö the standard recommends disclosing workforce composition by organisational unit as the foundation for headcount planning. The Departments register is the table that makes this disclosure possible; the active-staff count on every card is the simplest cut of it, the Reports module (Ch 31) collects the same field across leave, payroll, and recruitment cuts. See Chapter 44.
- **ISO 30414 ┬¦4.6 organisational culture** ŌĆö the standard's culture metrics (engagement, turnover, manager span-of-control) are all pivoted on the unit, which in CIHRMS terms is the department row. The portal page is the surfacing layer for the per-department cuts; the Phase 1 portal-KPI wiring lands the live numbers behind the placeholder values shipped today.
- **Public Services Commission (PSC) establishment control** ŌĆö the PSC's approval framework operates on (department, grade, fiscal year) triples. The Departments register is one third of that triple; the Grades and Establishment Ceilings tables in Ch 8 supply the other two. Deleting a department mid-fiscal-year is therefore not a casual rename ŌĆö it requires the matching ceiling rows to be archived and re-issued, which is why the UI uses soft delete and the establishment migration does *not* cascade department deletes onto ceilings.
- **Data Protection Act, 2012 (Act 843) ┬¦17 ŌĆö lawful basis for processing personal data** ŌĆö the Departments register itself processes no personal data (the head FK is an indirect reference, and the row carries only operational metadata). It is in scope of the DPA only as part of the joined-employee view; the same lawful-basis-of-employment-contract that covers the Employees table covers the FK on this side. See Chapter 26.
- **Auditor-General audit trail** ŌĆö every department create, edit, soft-delete, and head-change writes to the audit log via the `audit` route middleware that wraps the whole department route group. The Phase 1 tamper-evident chain (Ch 44) extends the existing log entry into the hash-linked record the Auditor-General pack reads off.

## What's planned next

Three pieces of work are scheduled directly against this chapter for the Phase 1 UI follow-up (W1.30 series):

1. **Reassign-head picker and "Currently head: ŌĆ”" surface.** The slide-panel form gains a `head_user_id` dropdown scoped to users whose primary role is `manager`, `dept_head`, or `hr_admin`. The card body gains a small "Head: {name}" row underneath the description (or an "Unassigned" italic when null). The matching FormRequest (`StoreDepartmentRequest` and a new `UpdateDepartmentRequest`) is widened to accept and validate `head_user_id`. The RBAC engine already reads the column correctly via `User::headedDepartments()` and `User::managesDepartment()`; only the UI and the FormRequest changes are required to make the column user-visible.

2. **FormRequest consolidation.** The update path today validates inline in the controller rather than via a dedicated FormRequest, and the rules diverge from create on `code` (required vs nullable) and `description` (1000 vs 500). W1.30 consolidates into one `UpdateDepartmentRequest` that mirrors `StoreDepartmentRequest`'s rules with `Rule::unique('departments', 'name')->ignore($department->id)` and the equivalent on `code`, so a rename hits the per-field validation error rather than the DB integrity error.

3. **Portal KPI wiring.** Each of the eight portals currently shows hard-coded KPI values from the `PORTALS` constant in `Departments/Show.vue`. The Phase 1 work moves the values to a stats endpoint per portal ŌĆö IT pulls from the Tickets module (Ch 11), HR pulls from the Employees module (Ch 3), Finance pulls from the Payroll Engine (Ch 19), and so on. The portal's `StaticPageController::department` action gains a `stats` array in the Inertia payload, the Vue template reads it instead of the constant, and the portal page becomes a real-time dashboard rather than a styled skeleton.

A fourth piece ŌĆö a *department detail* page (`/departments/{id}`) that lists the head, the active position roster, the establishment ceiling, and the employee count broken down by grade ŌĆö is on the Phase 2 backlog and depends on the establishment module being wired to the department register through `positions.department_id` (already in place, see Ch 8).

<!-- ===== 31_reports_analytics.md ===== -->

# Chapter 31 ŌĆö Reports & Analytics

> *In one paragraph.* Reports & Analytics is the bird's-eye view of CIHRMS ŌĆö the place where every other module's day-to-day churn shows up as numbers a director, an auditor, or a finance officer can act on. The Dashboard (cached every 60 seconds per user) frames it for whoever just logged in; the Reports Ledger turns the live database into five named XLSX exports HR teams actually file with; and the Auditor-General Report Pack bundles a fiscal year of payroll, statutory returns, identity verifications, loans, off-boarding and audit-chain output into a single SHA-256-sealed ZIP that the Auditor-General's office can verify by re-hashing. Underneath it all sits the `analytics_events` table ŌĆö a queued, append-only stream that every Wave-1 module writes into and that the Dashboard's sparklines and "Live activity" feed read out of.

## Where to find it

- **Sidebar location:** **Reports** group (gold tier ŌĆö the same flagship 5% accent as the Dashboard). Two children: **Overview** (`/modules/reports` ŌåÆ `/reports`) and **Auditor-General** (`/reports/auditor-general`). The Reports group only shows when the signed-in user has `reports.view`; the Auditor-General child further requires `statutory.export` so only finance officers and auditors see it. The **Dashboard** itself sits one rung above as the institute's landing page and is its own sidebar item (`assessment` icon, top of the rail).
- **Roles that see it:**
    - **super_admin**, **ceo**, **hr_admin** ŌĆö full Reports landing, all five exports, every dashboard tile, every snapshot block (Finance + Manager + Dept-Head bundles render alongside the admin view).
    - **finance_officer** ŌĆö full Reports landing + the Finance dashboard (`RoleFinance.vue`) with treasury-bulletin hero, payroll-run posture, disbursement queue and statutory-overdue posture; can generate and download Auditor-General packs (holds `statutory.export`).
    - **auditor** ŌĆö full Reports landing + the Auditor-General pack download/regenerate buttons; also holds `audit.view`, so they can verify the chain output from inside the pack.
    - **dept_head** ŌĆö Reports landing (everything they can see in the underlying data, scoped to their department) + their own `RoleDeptHead.vue` overview with departmental headcount, on-leave-today count and a six-row recent leave feed.
    - **manager** ŌĆö Reports landing + `RoleManager.vue` overview with team size, pending-leave inbox (8 latest), and open tickets for direct reports.
    - **it_support**, **marketing** ŌĆö Reports landing is invisible (no `reports.view`), but the catch-all `RoleOverview.vue` dashboard still renders with their role-tagged KPIs and quick actions.
    - **employee** ŌĆö Dashboard only. No Reports landing, no Auditor-General page; their dashboard is the standard four-tile overview.
- **Related modules:** Every Wave-1 module fires the events that populate this screen. Employees (Ch 3) ŌåÆ `employee.created`; Leave (Ch 4) ŌåÆ `leave.requested`, `leave.status_updated`; Attendance (Ch 5) ŌåÆ `attendance.correction.*`; Tickets (Ch 11) ŌåÆ `ticket.created`; Payroll (Ch 19) ŌåÆ `payslip.generated`, `payment.paid`; Recruitment (Ch 9) ŌåÆ `recruitment.applicant.created`; Assets (via the asset events) ŌåÆ six `asset.*` types; Benefits (Ch 23) ŌåÆ five `benefit.*` types; Governance (Ch 28) ŌåÆ six `policy.*` and `certification.*` types. The Auditor-General pack is the join point with Audit Logs (Ch 24) ŌĆö the pack ships `audit:verify-chain` output verbatim. Standards alignment lives in Chapter 44.

## The screens

![Reports Ledger ŌĆö five report cards with live preview sparklines](../assets/screenshots/31_reports/ledger.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Masthead ŌĆö "REPORTS LEDGER" eyebrow + report-count pill ("5 exports available"). The dossier voice carries from the Dashboard: this is a broadsheet, not an admin panel. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Five report cards in a responsive 1/2/3-column grid (Headcount, Leave Summary, Payroll Export, Ticket SLA, Staff Turnover). Each card carries an accent colour rule along its top edge ŌĆö gold for the flagship Headcount card, green for Leave, cobalt for Payroll, red for Tickets, amber for Turnover ŌĆö and shows a 6-point sparkline driven by the same `previews` payload the controller cached for sixty seconds. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Selected report ring ŌĆö a soft cobalt ring + check icon marks the active card. The cards are buttons; clicking selects without navigating. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Bottom-of-card metric block ŌĆö "Active staff: 248", "Approved this year: 312", "Paid this month (GHS): 1,420,500", etc. The sparkline is the same series the detail panel uses, scaled to fit.*

![Reports Ledger ŌĆö detail panel + export configurator](../assets/screenshots/31_reports/configurator.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Left column (lg:col-span-2) ŌĆö visual preview for the selected report: department/type/priority/month breakdown rendered as a horizontal bar chart with the same gradient as the card. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Right column ŌĆö "Configure Export" panel. Year dropdown appears for Leave; a month picker appears for Payroll; the other three reports note "This report has no additional filters." ┬Ę ŌØĖ Download CTA ŌĆö a full-width gradient button that links to `/reports/export?type={key}&...`. The endpoint streams an XLSX from one of five `Maatwebsite\Excel` classes (HeadcountExport, LeaveSummaryExport, PayrollExport, TicketSlaExport, TurnoverExport) ŌĆö generated server-side, no client-side chart-to-PDF tricks.*

![Auditor-General Report Pack ŌĆö "What's bundled" + generate form + existing packs](../assets/screenshots/31_reports/ag.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Breadcrumb (Reports ŌåÆ Auditor-General) + "AUDITOR-GENERAL PACK ┬Ę ACT 921" eyebrow + "sealed packs" count pill. ┬Ę ŌØĘ "What's bundled" card ŌĆö eight green-check rows enumerating exactly what the ZIP contains, plus one amber-info row that explicitly notes whistleblower content is segregated per Act 720. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Generate form ŌĆö fiscal year (default = current year), jurisdiction (Ghana only in MVP), and a primary CTA labelled "Generate pack (2FA required)" because the route is gated by the `2fa:fresh` middleware on top of the `statutory.export` permission. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Existing packs table ŌĆö file name, size, generation timestamp, download link. The list is sorted newest-first server-side; clicking Download streams the ZIP via `Storage::disk('local')->download()`.*

![Dashboard ŌĆö executive donut rings + KPI sparkline cards + live activity](../assets/screenshots/31_reports/dashboard.png)

*Callouts: ŌØČ Editorial-Sovereign masthead ŌĆö date + roman-numeral edition label, ticking second clock, "Live ┬Ę 12s" pill showing time since last Inertia partial reload. ┬Ę ŌØĘ Three executive donut rings ŌĆö Workforce (active count vs 1,500 target), Service load (open tickets vs 50 cap), Leave backlog (pending vs 25 cap). The fill colour shifts cool ŌåÆ amber ŌåÆ red as the percentage crosses 50 / 80. ┬Ę ŌØĖ Four KPI sparkline cards ŌĆö Active Staff, Open Tickets, Pending Leave, Pending Payroll ŌĆö each with a 30-day micro-chart driven by `DashboardService::timeSeries()`, which reads from the `analytics_events` table grouped by `DATE(created_at)`. ┬Ę ŌØ╣ Live activity feed ŌĆö a 5-row rotating window over the latest 12 events (`getRecentActivityFeed()`); each event maps to an icon + colour via the `EVENT_PRESENTATION` table (person_add cobalt for hires, support_agent red for tickets, payments green for paid runs, etc.).*

> The four screenshot files referenced above will be captured in Wave 1 (task W1.19). Until then the build will substitute a "missing image" placeholder ŌĆö that is expected and does not break the build.

## Every button, every action

### Reports Ledger (`/reports`)

| Button / field | What it does | Who can use it | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|
