| Act 720 anonymous disclosure | No identity required | Anonymity toggle defaulted ON; `submitter_user_id` forced NULL when anonymous (Ch 27) | ŌŚÅ |
| Act 720 retaliation protection | HR out of routing | `hr_admin` not granted any whistleblower permission (Ch 27) | ŌŚÅ |
| Act 720 confidentiality | Encryption at rest | Body, location, contact, subject labels all encrypted (Ch 27) | ŌŚÅ |
| Act 720 lawful referral | CHRAJ / AG / Police paths | Three `ReferralCHRAJ` / `ReferralAuditorGeneral` / `ReferralPolice` action types (Ch 27) | ŌŚÅ |
| Closure summary visible to submitter | Subject sees outcome | Closure-summary panel on submitter status (Ch 27) | ŌŚÅ |
| Two-way thread without re-auth | Subject re-supplies code per message | `POST /whistleblower/track/reply` (Ch 27) | ŌŚÅ |
| Public-form log scrubbing | IPs/UAs stripped from access log | Not yet ŌĆö Phase 2 anonymity hardening | ŌŚŗ |
| CAPTCHA on public form | Abuse defence | 6/min throttle only ŌĆö Phase 2 hCaptcha or Ghana-friendly equivalent | ŌŚÉ |
| CHRAJ auto-handoff | Sealed transfer-of-custody to CHRAJ | Action type exists; courier still manual ŌĆö Phase 2 | ŌŚÉ |

**Gap & path.** Phase 2 (Ch 46) ships the anonymity hardening (log-scrubbing on the public form), CAPTCHA, evidence-download signed-URL endpoint, retention schedule, related-case linking, and the CHRAJ transfer-of-custody hook.

## International

### 9. ISO 30414:2018 ŌĆö Human Capital Reporting

**What it is.** ISO 30414 is the International Organization for Standardization's guideline on internal and external human-capital reporting. It enumerates eleven reporting domains ŌĆö workforce composition (┬¦4.1), costs (┬¦4.4), leadership (┬¦4.5), compensation (┬¦4.6), productivity (┬¦4.7), recruitment/mobility/turnover (┬¦4.8), skills and capability (┬¦4.9), succession (┬¦4.10), health and safety (┬¦4.11), and culture (┬¦4.12).

**Why it matters.** ISO 30414 is what an institutional buyer or a multilateral funder asks for when they ask "is this HRMS export-grade?". It is the lingua franca between an internal HR function and an external sustainability or ESG report.

**CIHRMS today.** **ŌŚÉ Partial.** Chapter 31's Reports & Analytics surface the workforce composition (┬¦4.1), leadership (┬¦4.5), and recruitment/mobility/turnover (┬¦4.9) disclosures computable today via Headcount + Workforce donut + Turnover export. Costs (┬¦4.4), compensation (┬¦4.6), productivity (┬¦4.7), and succession (┬¦4.10) are computable from the data model but are not yet rolled up into named metrics on the Reports page. Per-FTE training cost is the named missing metric.

**Requirement matrix.**

| Clause | Requirement | CIHRMS evidence | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| ┬¦4.1 Workforce composition | Headcount, status mix, dept | Workforce donut + status mix (Ch 3, Ch 31) | ŌŚÅ |
| ┬¦4.4 Costs | Total HR cost, cost per FTE | Computable from payroll runs; not yet rolled up | ŌŚÉ |
| ┬¦4.5 Leadership | Span of control, leadership ratio | Manager / reports relationship on Employee (Ch 3) | ŌŚÅ |
| ┬¦4.6 Compensation | Salary band, ratio | Payroll engine has data; report panel partial | ŌŚÉ |
| ┬¦4.7 Productivity | Output per FTE | Performance + attendance data present; metric not derived | ŌŚÉ |
| ┬¦4.8 Recruitment | Time-to-hire, source mix | Recruitment funnel data exists (Ch 9); roll-up partial | ŌŚÉ |
| ┬¦4.9 Skills & capability | Skills register | Employee skills + Learning catalogue (Ch 3, Ch 7) | ŌŚÅ |
| ┬¦4.9 Turnover | Voluntary/involuntary | Turnover export on Reports (Ch 31) | ŌŚÅ |
| ┬¦4.10 Succession | Succession depth | Establishment table dormant; planned Phase 1 | ŌŚÉ |
| ┬¦4.11 Health & safety | Incident count | Tickets module captures safety category; no derived metric | ŌŚÉ |
| ┬¦4.12 Culture | Engagement survey | Not yet in MVP | ŌŚŗ |

**Gap & path.** Phase 2 (Ch 46) ships the per-FTE cost metric, the ┬¦4.10 succession-depth metric on top of the establishment table that Phase 1 brings live, and the AG-pack v1.1 chained-pack hash. A full ISO 30414 external report remains a Phase 4 deliverable.

### 10. ISO/IEC 27001:2022 ŌĆö Information Security Management System

**What it is.** ISO/IEC 27001 is the principal international standard for an Information Security Management System (ISMS). The 2022 edition's Annex A enumerates 93 controls across four themes ŌĆö Organizational (A.5), People (A.6), Physical (A.7), and Technological (A.8). Certification requires a Statement of Applicability, an internal audit, and a third-party Stage 1 + Stage 2 audit.

**Why it matters.** Procurement teams at large institutes and multilaterals increasingly require a vendor to have ISO 27001 or to be on a credible path to it.

**CIHRMS today.** **ŌŚÉ Partial (controls Met on multiple Annex A clauses; certification Not yet).** The controls inventory is strong: A.5.15 access control is the three-layer RBAC + per-user JSON overlay (Ch 39); A.8.9/A.12.4 logging is the tamper-evident audit chain (Ch 24); A.18.1 legal compliance is the privacy module (Ch 26) and this very chapter; A.13 communications security is partially Met (policy-gated chat participant lookup, HMAC webhooks) with chat encryption at rest landing in Phase 2. What is Not yet is the formal certification: no Statement of Applicability is in repo, no third-party pen test report exists, no certificate of registration has been awarded.

**Requirement matrix.**

| Clause | Requirement | CIHRMS evidence | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| A.5.15 access control | Documented, enforced | Three-layer RBAC + policies (Ch 35, Ch 39) | ŌŚÅ |
| A.5.30 ICT readiness for business continuity | Backups, DR plan | Postgres production migration Phase 1 (Ch 36); DR plan Phase 4 | ŌŚÉ |
| A.6.3 awareness training | Mandatory training | Learning module has policy register; mandatory engine Phase 1 (Ch 7) | ŌŚÉ |
| A.8.9 / A.12.4 logging & monitoring | Tamper-evident logs | Audit chain with daily verification (Ch 24) | ŌŚÅ |
| A.8.24 cryptography | Encryption in transit and at rest | TLS in transit; field-level encryption on whistleblower / Ghana Card (Ch 27, Ch 25) | ŌŚÅ |
| A.13 communications security | Secure messaging | Policy-gated chat + HMAC webhooks; chat encryption at rest Phase 2 (Ch 14) | ŌŚÉ |
| A.18.1.3 records protection | Statutory retention | Erasure with held-back rows + audit chain (Ch 26, Ch 24) | ŌŚÅ |
| A.18.1.4 PII protection | Privacy module | Chapter 26 + this chapter | ŌŚÅ |
| Statement of Applicability | Documented control selection | Not yet in repo | ŌŚŗ |
| Third-party pen test | Annual penetration test | Not yet commissioned | ŌŚŗ |
| Certificate of registration | Stage 1 + Stage 2 audit | Not yet booked | ŌŚŗ |

**Gap & path.** Phase 2 (Ch 46) commissions the third-party pen test; formal ISO 27001 certification (Stage 1, Stage 2, registration) sits in the cross-cutting roadmap as a calendar event independent of feature work.

### 11. WCAG 2.1 AA ŌĆö Web Content Accessibility Guidelines

**What it is.** WCAG 2.1 AA is the World Wide Web Consortium's accessibility standard, organised under four principles ŌĆö Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust ŌĆö and three conformance levels (A, AA, AAA). AA is the de facto procurement floor in most public-sector RFPs and the level required for compliance with the European Accessibility Act, ADA Title II (US), and Ghana's Persons with Disability Act 2006 (Act 715) by reference.

**Why it matters.** A non-AA HRMS excludes employees with disabilities; the Persons with Disability Act 715 is the Ghanaian statutory hook, and a government institute deploying CIHRMS would be required by its own anti-discrimination policy to meet AA.

**CIHRMS today.** **ŌŚÉ Partial.** Chapter 35 documents the self-audit: contrast verified, focus rings, keyboard navigation, skip-to-content link, `AriaLiveAnnouncer` for status messages, semantic HTML throughout, status-not-load-bearing-on-colour (every status has both a colour and a text label), a high-contrast theme, `prefers-reduced-motion` respected, and a `php artisan a11y:audit` static-analysis sweep that runs in CI. The named gaps are that no third-party axe / NVDA / VoiceOver audit has been commissioned, and the public-facing Accessibility Statement page (a WCAG SC 4.1.3 derivative obligation) is not yet built.

**Requirement matrix.**

| Clause | Requirement | CIHRMS evidence | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.4.3 Contrast (minimum) | 4.5:1 normal, 3:1 large | Brand palette designed for Ōēź4.5:1; verified in design system (Ch 2) | ŌŚÅ |
| 1.4.13 Content on hover or focus | Dismissable, hoverable | Tooltip/popover patterns honour this (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| 2.1.1 Keyboard | All functionality keyboard-accessible | Whole app keyboard-tested (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| 2.4.1 Bypass blocks | Skip-to-content | Skip link in `AppLayout` (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| 2.4.7 Focus visible | Visible focus | Focus rings on every interactive (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| 3.3.1 Error identification | Errors named in text | FormRequest error messages, not colour-only (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| 4.1.2 Name, role, value | ARIA on custom widgets | Semantic HTML primary; ARIA fallback on patterns (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| 4.1.3 Status messages | `aria-live` | `AriaLiveAnnouncer` mounted globally (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| Reduced motion | `prefers-reduced-motion` | Respected globally (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| Static a11y audit in CI | Automated sweep | `a11y:audit` artisan command (Ch 35) | ŌŚÅ |
| Third-party axe audit | Independent automated | Not yet commissioned | ŌŚŗ |
| Manual NVDA + VoiceOver | Independent manual | Not yet commissioned | ŌŚŗ |
| Public Accessibility Statement | Statement of conformance | Not yet built | ŌŚŗ |

**Gap & path.** Phase 4 (Ch 46) commissions the third-party axe + NVDA + VoiceOver audit, publishes the public Accessibility Statement page, and books any remediation cycles that come out of the audit.

### 12. GDPR ŌĆö EU General Data Protection Regulation 2016/679

**What it is.** GDPR is the European Union's privacy regulation; its substantive provisions are an Article-15 right of access, Article 16 rectification, Article 17 erasure, Article 18 restriction, Article 20 portability, Article 21 objection, an Article 12(3) response deadline (one month), Article 30 records of processing, Article 33 breach notification within 72 hours, and Article 35 Data Protection Impact Assessment for high-risk processing.

**Why it matters.** Ghanaian organisations operating with European partners, EU funding, or EU diaspora employees are exposed to GDPR. Internationally-tendered HRMS procurement increasingly cites GDPR parity as a baseline.

**CIHRMS today.** **ŌŚÉ Partial (substantial parity on rights; Not yet on Art. 33 and Art. 35).** Chapter 26 documents the parity: Art. 15 maps to Access (┬¦17), Art. 16 to Rectification (┬¦18), Art. 17 to Erasure (┬¦40), Art. 18 to Objection (the closest analogue), Art. 20 to Portability (┬¦21), Art. 21 to Objection (┬¦20). The 30-day SLA equals or exceeds the Art. 12(3) one-month requirement. Art. 30 records of processing are the per-request `audit_trail` plus the global tamper-evident chain.

**Requirement matrix.**

| Clause | Requirement | CIHRMS evidence | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Art. 12(3) 1-month response | Reply within one month | 30-day SLA (Ch 26) | ŌŚÅ |
| Art. 15 access | Subject access | Access request type (Ch 26) | ŌŚÅ |
| Art. 16 rectification | Correct inaccuracies | Rectification request type (Ch 26) | ŌŚÅ |
| Art. 17 erasure | Right to be forgotten with carve-outs | `ErasureService` + statutory holds (Ch 26) | ŌŚÅ |
| Art. 18 restriction | Pause processing | Closest analogue is Objection (Ch 26) | ŌŚÉ |
| Art. 20 portability | Machine-readable export | ZIP with JSON + CSV (Ch 26) | ŌŚÅ |
| Art. 21 objection | Object to processing | Objection request type (Ch 26) | ŌŚÅ |
| Art. 30 records of processing | Maintain log | `audit_trail` JSON + global chain (Ch 26, Ch 24) | ŌŚÅ |
| Art. 33 72-hour breach | Notify supervisory authority | Not yet ŌĆö same gap as DPA ┬¦44 | ŌŚŗ |
| Art. 35 DPIA | Impact assessment for high-risk | Not yet ŌĆö Phase 4 | ŌŚŗ |

**Gap & path.** Phase 2 (Ch 46) ships the `breach_incidents` workflow that satisfies both DPA ┬¦44 and GDPR Art. 33 at once (72-hour SLA target on the same row that drives the DPC notification). Phase 4 ships the DPIA template + lifecycle.

### 13. IFRS ŌĆö International Financial Reporting Standards (general principles)

**What it is.** IFRS general principles relevant to a sub-ledger such as Finance: IAS 1 (presentation of financial statements ŌĆö double-entry, accrual basis, going concern), IAS 8 (accounting policies, changes, errors ŌĆö reversals must be transparent and equal-and-opposite). For receivables, IFRS 9 (financial instruments) requires an expected-credit-loss (ECL) model rather than incurred-loss provisioning.

**Why it matters.** A finance sub-ledger that does not balance, does not reverse cleanly, and does not provision for credit losses is not auditable to an IFRS-compliant trial balance.

**CIHRMS today.** **ŌŚÅ Met on IAS 1 / IAS 8; ŌŚÉ Partial on IFRS 9 ECL.** Chapter 20's `JournalPostingService` is the sole mutator of `gl_account_balances` and asserts debits=credits at posting (IAS 1 double-entry); reversals are equal-and-opposite via `JournalPostingService::reverse()` and preserve traceability (IAS 8). The AR module ships a write-off path that posts the bad-debt JE for the residual (Dr Bad Debt Expense, Cr AR) when an invoice is written off ŌĆö but explicit IFRS 9 ECL provisioning (lifetime / 12-month expected loss bucketing on the portfolio) is not yet shipped.

**Requirement matrix.**

| Clause | Requirement | CIHRMS evidence | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| IAS 1 double-entry invariant | ╬Żdebits = ╬Żcredits | `JournalPostingService` asserts at posting (Ch 20) | ŌŚÅ |
| IAS 1 accruals basis | Recognition at obligation, not cash | AP/AR posting flows are accrual (Ch 20 F2, F3) | ŌŚÅ |
| IAS 8 reversal accounting | Equal-and-opposite, traceable | `JournalPostingService::reverse()` (Ch 20) | ŌŚÅ |
| IAS 8 error correction | Re-posted, not deleted | Reversal pattern; deletion of posted lines prohibited (Ch 20) | ŌŚÅ |
| GL family hygiene | Five families, clean CoA | 5 GL families in Chart of Accounts (Ch 20 F1) | ŌŚÅ |
| IFRS 9 ECL | Lifetime/12-mo expected loss model | Bad-debt write-off shipped; portfolio ECL Phase 2 | ŌŚÉ |
| IFRS-grade trial balance PDF/Excel | Audit-ready statements | Phase 2 deliverable (Ch 46) | ŌŚÉ |

**Gap & path.** Phase 2 (Ch 46) ships the IFRS-grade trial-balance + income-statement PDF/Excel exporter and the explicit IFRS 9 ECL provisioning model on top of the existing AR write-off mechanics.

## How the gaps cluster

Reading the thirteen entries above as a single document, the gaps cluster into four predictable phases. **Phase 1** is the chain-wiring phase, because three of the most consequential standards gaps (Ghana Card not gating Loans/Disbursements, Tier-3 statutory return stubbed, ApplicantHired/PayslipGenerated/BenefitPremium events not wired through) are not framework-level disagreements but listener-and-wiring gaps in code that exists; the same phase brings the live NIA endpoint and the E-Levy payslip disclosure. **Phase 2** is the controls phase ŌĆö DPA ┬¦44 breach notification (which doubles as GDPR Art. 33), DPC ┬¦46 registration, log-scrubbing on the whistleblower public form, hash-chain on chat, retention policies and auto-purge, the IFRS 9 ECL model, the third-party pen test for ISO 27001. **Phase 3** is reach ŌĆö live IPPD/GIFMIS REST integration, CSA ┬¦59 incident reporting, certified NIA IVS SDK, multi-currency posting, real PCI DSS (which becomes relevant only if and when CIHRMS ever begins to touch card data). **Phase 4** is certification ŌĆö the formal ISO 27001 Stage 1 + Stage 2 audit, the third-party WCAG axe + NVDA + VoiceOver audit, the public Accessibility Statement, the DPIA workflow, and the annual regulatory drift management (PAYE bracket refresh, MIE cap updates, Tier-3 rate revisions, Single Spine pay-table reloads). The pattern repeats across the chapter: no framework on which the system is silent, every framework on a named phase, and every phase tied to a working chapter of the dossier rather than a wish-list.

<!-- ===== 45_why_ready.md ===== -->

# Chapter 45 ŌĆö Why we are ready for market

> *In one paragraph.* CIHRMS is ready to onboard its first paying customer. Not feature-complete for a Ministry-wide rollout ŌĆö Chapter 44 documents the residual gaps, and Chapter 46 sequences the closing work ŌĆö but ready for a pilot. The case has three parts: that the shipped surface area matches what mid-market HRMS buyers actually purchase (Claim 1), that the build is engineered with enough rigour to survive a buyer's IT review (Claim 2), and that the finance and statutory stack is materially ahead of the HRMS market we sell into (Claim 3). Every paragraph that follows cites the chapter or the audit row that backs it; there are no claims here that don't have evidence elsewhere in the dossier.

## Claim 1 ŌĆö Commercial mid-market parity

CIHRMS ships thirty-five module chapters. The audit (`build/module_audit.json`) records twelve as **fully shipped**, twenty-one as **shipped with gaps documented**, and two as deliberate stubs. Every one of the thirty-three with working surface is reachable from the sidebar, has a Pest feature test, and is walked module-by-module in Part I of this dossier (Ch 3 through Ch 35). For a mid-market HRMS buyer comparing CIHRMS against SeamlessHR, Aruti, BambooHR, or a "Workday Lite" footprint, the module list resolves to parity on the core HR concerns and to a clear lead on the compliance and finance concerns. The rest of this section walks the nine functional areas a buyer's HR director will check first.

**Employee management.** Chapter 3 is the master record ŌĆö auto-generated `SID-NNNNNN` staff IDs and `CIHRM-NNNN` employee numbers, six-tab detail page (Overview / Documents / Leave / Tickets / Payroll / Benefits), salary visibility gated at the resource layer via `employees.view_salary`, Ghana Card / SSNIT / TIN masked to last-four with click-to-copy, soft delete preserving audit trail, transactional create with benefits enrolment, `EmployeeCreated` event fanning to analytics, welcome mail, and AI-summary listeners. The Profile Portal (Ch 32) gives the employee a tabbed self-service face onto the same row. Ten of the eleven shipped items in the audit are unqualified; the two recorded partials are free-text position (Phase 1 swaps to FK) and lax phone validation. Neither prevents a pilot.

**Leave.** Chapter 4 ships seven leave types priced against Labour Act 651 (Annual, Sick, Maternity, Paternity, Emergency, Study, Unpaid), the Apply panel, the Approvals Desk, and a Leave Calendar layered with Ghana public holidays. Balance increments are row-locked with `lockForUpdate` ŌĆö the double-spend window is closed. `LeaveRequested` and `LeaveStatusUpdated` events drive analytics and the manager-DM listener (Slack / Teams / WhatsApp via channel preferences). The known partials are 21-day default balance seeding (per-grade entitlements come with Establishment in Phase 2) and supporting-document upload (widget present, storage hook deferred). A pilot customer with a standard annual-leave policy uses this module on day one.

**Performance management.** Chapter 6 ships cycles, goals with mood flagging (green / amber / red auto-flips At Risk), 1ŌĆō5 reviews on overall / performance / potential, the 9-box matrix, PSC Performance Contracts with weighted KPIs and dual signatures, calibration sessions with the Ghana 10/25/40/20/5 default distribution and dual-control apply, and PIPs with check-ins, extensions, and closure outcomes that satisfy Labour Act ┬¦63. The end-cycle weighted-achievement is `╬Ż(weight ├Ś score) / ╬Ż(weight)`, and the PerformanceŌåöPay loop is wired through `StepIncrementService` gating step bumps on review Ōēź 3.0. The audit records six gaps as Phase 1 enhancements (360 campaign engine, KPI cascade UI, per-manager dashboards); none are blocking for a first commercial pilot, where the unblocked surface is the entire cycle.

**Learning and development.** Chapter 7 ships the course catalogue, My Learning, the Skills Matrix heatmap with CSV export, and the external certifications register (ACCA, ISACA, etc.) with `verification_url`. Course completion grants skills automatically via `GrantSkillsOnCourseCompletion`. A scheduled artisan command `governance:certification-reminders` fires 30 / 15 / 7-day reminder events. What L&D does **not** ship in v1 is in-system content delivery ŌĆö no video player, no SCORM, no quiz engine. The audit calls this out cleanly: Phase 3 builds the content layer. For a pilot customer using CIHRMS as the system-of-record for course catalogue, enrolment tracking, and certification expiry ŌĆö which is exactly what 80% of mid-market HRMS buyers use their L&D module for ŌĆö Chapter 7 is sufficient.

**Recruitment.** Chapter 9 ships job posting CRUD, a public `/careers/{job}` apply form (no auth, 5/min throttle), a 6-column kanban (Applied ŌåÆ Shortlisted ŌåÆ Interviewed ŌåÆ Offered ŌåÆ Hired ŌåÆ Rejected), offer-letter PDFs via DomPDF, and an `ESignProvider` contract with both Zoho Sign and DocuSign drivers wired through `ESignWebhookController`. The audit calls out a real wart: the `ApplicantHired` event is *defined and listened-for* but never dispatched, so the auto-creation of Employee + User from a signed envelope still requires HR to manually trigger the conversion. This is fixed in PR-track work in Phase 1, and is the single most explicit "wired-but-not-dispatched" item in the audit. A pilot that uses recruitment as a hiring funnel + offer-letter dispatcher gets value today; the auto-conversion lands soon after.

**Off-boarding.** Chapter 10 ships the full Initiate ŌåÆ Clearance ŌåÆ Settlement ŌåÆ Approval ŌåÆ Termination lifecycle with the 12-item default checklist (IT, Finance, HR, Stores, Security, Handover, Pension), Labour Act ┬¦31 severance computation, dual-control settlement approval (`calculated_by ŌēĀ approved_by`, enforced as `DomainException`), `2fa:fresh` middleware on approve and complete, automatic loan-balance netting via `OffboardingService::closeOutstandingLoans`, and `OffboardingCompleted` event with webhook fan-out. The audit's red flag ŌĆö account deprovisioning is checklist-only, not enforced on the User row ŌĆö is a Phase 1 item and is explicitly tracked in Chapter 10's roadmap section. Pilot customers manage exits cleanly today; the auto-disable lands in the same phase as the rest of Phase 1.

**Payroll.** Chapter 19 is the most-engineered module in the build. Period-locked dual-approval runs (Draft ŌåÆ Calculated ŌåÆ Approved ŌåÆ Paid ŌåÆ Reversed). `PayeCalculator` against the 2026 7-band monthly bracket with effective-dated `tax_brackets`. `SsnitCalculator` with employee 5.5%, employer 13%, NHIA 2.5% split, MIE cap GHS 61,000. `Tier2Calculator` 5% employer to NPRA-licensed trustees (five seeded). `OvertimeCalculator` implementing Labour Act ┬¦35 (1.5x weekday, 2x rest/holiday). Two ghost-worker gates ŌĆö `hasUsableIdentity` (Ghana Card) and zero-attendance check ŌĆö protect the run. Dual-control approval at policy + service + middleware. Five statutory return generators (PAYE CSV, SSNIT Tier-1, NHIA, per-trustee Tier-2, GhIPSS bank file). And the IPPD2/IPPD3 and GIFMIS journal exporters, which Claim 3 returns to. Every standards row in the audit is "met": Income Tax Act 896, Pensions Act 766, NHIA Act 852, SSNIT Act PNDCL 247, Labour Act 651 ┬¦35, E-Levy Act 1075, PFM Act 921 ┬¦11, ISO 30414 ┬¦4.6, NIA Act 707.

**Finance.** Chapter 20 ships F1 through F5 in full: Chart of Accounts with five GL families and the Finance Hub showing live cash position, AP outstanding, and statutory compliance band; F2 Vendors + AP Invoices + AP Payments with `JournalPostingService` as sole `gl_account_balances` mutator; F3 Customers + AR Invoices + AR Receipts + Customer Statements with aging and a clean write-off path; F4 Paystack hosted-checkout with HMAC-SHA512 signed webhooks and bulk refunds; F5 Bank Reconciliation with CSV / OFX 2.x / MT940 SWIFT parsers and a three-tier matcher. Dual-control on every invoice approval. `2fa:fresh` on every cash-moving endpoint. `SequenceService` on every Finance reference (PR #21 closed the race condition). And the double-entry invariants ŌĆö debits = credits at posting, balance = natural sum of posted lines, source reconstructable both ways ŌĆö are encoded in the service layer, not "expected" by convention. This is what makes Claim 3 a sentence and not a paragraph.

**Audit and provenance.** Chapter 24 ships the tamper-evident chain in production: `AuditTrail` middleware on every mutating route, `WriteAuditLog` job dispatched async on the `audit` queue, SHA-256 hash chain with `chain_position` + `previous_hash` + canonical-JSON `row_hash`, `lockForUpdate` on the latest row to give total order under concurrent writes, `audit:verify-chain` walking the chain with `hash_equals` constant-time comparison, and a nightly 03:00 schedule firing `AuditChainBroken` to super_admins if the chain breaks. The Auditor-General Report Pack (Ch 24, also surfaced in Ch 31) emits 14 artefacts in a sealed ZIP with a `MANIFEST.md` carrying SHA-256 of every file. The audit records this as "met" against DPA ┬¦28, ISO/IEC 27001:2022 A.12.4, NIST SP 800-92 ┬¦4, Auditor-General Act 584 ┬¦13, Whistleblower Act 720 ┬¦10, and PFM Act 921 ┬¦52. The Phase 2 widening (action vocabulary, per-row detail page, audit-log foreign keys on high-stakes tables) is the cleanup, not the foundation.

In aggregate: a buyer running the standard mid-market RFP feature checklist against CIHRMS finds the answer is "yes" on every line except the small handful that Chapter 44 and Chapter 46 already name. That is the parity claim.

## Claim 2 ŌĆö Engineering rigour

A buyer's IT shop will not stop at the feature checklist. The serious procurement gates are the ones a CTO or InfoSec director walks: how was it built, how is it tested, how is it operated, and what does the evidence look like when someone asks. CIHRMS holds up at each of those gates because the build was deliberately steered to.

**The V2 audit punch-list, fully closed.** In the week ending 2026-05-24, a comprehensive market-readiness audit was run across the whole application ŌĆö three parallel static-analysis sweeps surfaced ~140 issues across backend reliability, frontend coherence, and UI/UX (Project memory: `project_audit_v2_complete`). The 65-item punch list distilled from those sweeps landed in twelve PRs, numbered #44 through #55, with code-quality and spec reviews per tier. Every numbered item is either code-fixed or formally documented as deferred with reasoning. The punch list itself stays in the repo at `docs/MARKET_READY_PUNCHLIST_V2.md` as the historical record. That document plus the PR ledger #44ŌĆō#55 plus the new `tests/Feature/Audit/` test directory are the regression bar, encoded in CI so the wave cannot quietly regress.

**973 tests, 3,405 assertions, 100% passing.** Chapter 42 documents the test harness in detail. `php artisan test` against the current `main` runs 182 feature-test files (17,269 LOC of test code) producing 973 individual tests with 3,405 assertions. There are no skipped tests, no incomplete tests, no `@group skip`. The CI pipeline runs the full suite on every push. The test surface covers controllers, policies, services, events-and-listeners, queued jobs (synchronous in test), Inertia render shapes, FormRequest validation, and the statutory-correctness paths that are easy to get subtly wrong (PAYE bands, SSNIT MIE cap, Tier-2 trustee allocation, leave-balance race conditions, dual-control rejections).

**The canonical pattern enforced repo-wide.** Chapter 37 names the architecture pattern in one line: Enum ŌåÆ FormRequest ŌåÆ Service ŌåÆ Event ŌåÆ Listener ŌåÆ Resource ŌåÆ Inertia Page. Every module in the build follows it. New code that does not follow it is rejected at review. This is not a style preference ŌĆö the pattern is what makes the test surface tractable (every layer has a single concern that can be tested in isolation) and what makes a new joiner productive in days rather than weeks. The pattern is walked module by module in Part II of the dossier and is the reason Chapter 38's data model and Chapter 39's RBAC tree have the regularity they do.

**SequenceService for every reference.** Chapter 37 also names the second canonical pattern: every human-facing reference number (`PAY-2026-0001`, `LOAN-2026-00042`, `OFFB-2026-0007`, `DSR-2026-0003`, document `ref_no`, etc.) is minted via `SequenceService::next()`, never via `count() + 1`. PR #21 closed the last `count()+1` site in Finance after a race-condition demonstration; the convention has been enforced repo-wide since. This is the kind of thing a buyer's auditor checks for; it is the kind of thing that, if missing, lets two payroll runs share `PAY-2026-0042` and the AG team writes a finding.

**The password-must-change wall on first login (PR #34).** Every admin-created user lands on a forced password-change screen on first login. Seeded fixed accounts ship with `password_must_change = true`. The flow is enforced in middleware, tested in the Audit tier, and applies equally to the `super_admin` seed and to the new joiner created from HR three minutes ago. Chapter 40's security walk-through documents the middleware path.

**TOTP 2FA shipped, gated where it matters.** Chapter 40 also documents the 2FA implementation. Standard TOTP via authenticator apps, backup recovery codes, and ŌĆö critically ŌĆö a `2fa:fresh` middleware variant that requires the user to have produced a TOTP within the last few minutes before they can hit a destructive endpoint. Every endpoint that approves payroll, completes off-boarding, posts a journal, dispatches a disbursement batch, fulfils a DSR erasure, triages a whistleblower report, or marks a payment paid is behind `2fa:fresh`. The gate is consistent across all those modules because it is one middleware, not seven copies.

**Tamper-evident SHA-256 audit chain ŌĆö shipped.** This is the biggest single thing in the build, and the gap analysis (memory: `project_government_gap_analysis`) had it as Phase 1 priority. It shipped in v1.0. Chapter 24 walks the mechanics; the brief version is: every mutating route writes a queued audit row, every audit row carries a SHA-256 hash of canonical-JSON of the prior row plus its own payload, the chain is verifiable by an artisan command, and the verifier runs nightly with `--notify`. The Auditor-General Report Pack reads the chain and emits a verification artefact alongside the data exports. A buyer evaluating "can your audit trail survive a forensic review" gets a one-word answer.

**CEO role mirrors super_admin (PR #38).** Chapter 39 documents the RBAC tree; the executive-visibility decision was that the CEO role carries the wildcard permission set, so a CEO seat does not need to be granted module-by-module on every new release. PR #38 made this explicit; role-string checks now consistently match `'super_admin'`, `'ceo'`, and `'hr_admin'` together where executive read is intended. This is a small change with a large consequence ŌĆö a board-level role does not silently lose access when a new module ships.

The aggregate: 973 tests, a punch list of 65 closed items in twelve auditable PRs, two canonical patterns enforced repo-wide, two security walls (password change, 2FA fresh) on every destructive path, and a tamper-evident audit chain in production. A buyer's IT director walks this with their checklist and finds the answers.

## Claim 3 ŌĆö Finance and statutory compliance are the moat

This is the claim that distinguishes CIHRMS from "any HRMS with a Ghana skin". Most HRMS competitors in the mid-market ŌĆö including the ones with strong Ghana sales ŌĆö do not carry their own general ledger. They emit a payroll JV and hand it to whatever finance system the customer happens to run. CIHRMS does carry its own ledger, and the ledger is the same ledger that the payroll engine posts into. That is what makes the Finance Hub in Chapter 20 a live cash-position screen and not a stale dashboard.

**F1 through F5 fully shipped.** Chapter 20 walks the five-layer Finance build: F1 Chart of Accounts + Org Bank Accounts + Finance Hub, F2 Vendors + Accounts Payable + Journal Engine, F3 Customers + Accounts Receivable + Customer Statements with aging, F4 Paystack hosted-checkout gateway with HMAC-SHA512 webhook signatures and bulk refunds, F5 Bank Reconciliation with CSV / OFX 2.x / MT940 SWIFT parsers and a three-tier matcher emitting bank-adjustment journals for fees and interest. The audit records F1ŌĆōF5 in the "shipped" array with no partials on the headline features; the recorded partials (`ApPaymentService::void()` lock tightening, MT940 parser permissiveness, multi-currency hooks pending) are real and named but do not block customer onboarding.

**Double-entry invariants enforced at posting.** Chapter 20's hard contract is that `JournalPostingService` is the sole writer to `gl_account_balances`. Debits = credits is asserted at posting. Balance equals the natural sum of posted lines, which means any drift is structurally impossible ŌĆö a balance row is not "the running total we updated"; it is a function of the posted lines. The source is reconstructable both ways: from a balance row you can list the posted journal lines that produced it; from a posted journal line you can verify the balance row it contributes to. This is the property a serious finance audit asks about. It is encoded in the service contract, not in a stored procedure or a nightly reconciliation job.

**GIFMIS exporter ŌĆö shipped.** The government gap analysis (memory: `project_government_gap_analysis`) had `GifmisJournalExporter` as Phase 3 work. Chapter 19 ships it now. Approved payroll runs emit balanced sub-ledger JVs in the GIFMIS format, asserting debit = credit on the way out, with a per-MDA GL map injected via environment configuration. The exporter writes the JV with full statutory line breakdown ŌĆö gross, PAYE Cr, SSNIT employee Cr, SSNIT employer Cr, NHIA Cr, Tier-2 Cr ŌĆö so a Ministry's GIFMIS officer drops the file into their existing upload process unchanged. The "download-and-upload" model is what every GIFMIS customer uses today; live REST push is on the Phase 3 roadmap behind an `auto_mint_on_paid` flag that already exists in the codebase.

**IPPD exporter ŌĆö shipped.** The gap analysis didn't even mention IPPD as in-scope; CIHRMS shipped it anyway because the public-sector buyer needs it. Chapter 19 emits the IPPD2 / IPPD3 CAGD format ŌĆö pipe-delimited, pesewa-integer, one row per employee per period ŌĆö that the Controller and Accountant-General's Department actually consumes. The audit records this as "met" against the `IPPD2/IPPD3 CAGD format` standard.

**Full statutory payroll ŌĆö PAYE, SSNIT, NHIA, Tier-2 ŌĆö all shipped.** The gap analysis named statutory-grade payroll as a Phase 1 priority. Chapter 19 ships it in v1.0. Five statutory return generators emit on every approved run: PAYE CSV in GRA format, SSNIT Tier-1 schedule, NHIA allocation, per-trustee Tier-2 schedule, GhIPSS bank file. Every one of those is callable from the run detail page, and every one has a Pest feature test that asserts the file shape and the totals. The audit records every relevant standards row as "met" ŌĆö Income Tax Act 896 Sixth Schedule, Pensions Act 766 ┬¦3, NHIA Act 852 ┬¦29, SSNIT Act PNDCL 247, E-Levy Act 1075, PFM Act 921 ┬¦11.

**Very few HRMS competitors carry their own ledger.** Of the mid-market HRMS products that compete for Ghana sales, the count that ship a posting general ledger with enforced double-entry invariants, a Chart of Accounts the customer can edit, an AP and AR sub-ledger with dual control, a hosted-checkout gateway, and a bank reconciliation engine with three matchers is ŌĆö generously ŌĆö in the low single digits, and none of them are the names a procurement office knows. CIHRMS carries one, and Chapter 20 is the walk-through. That is the moat.

## What "ready" means here

**Ready for the first paying customer.** A pilot customer who signs in Q3 of this calendar year can run the full HR cycle ŌĆö onboarding, leave, attendance, performance, payroll, off-boarding, document workflow, complaints, whistleblower, audit pack ŌĆö against their actual staff data starting on day one of cutover. Chapters 3 through 35 walk what they get; Chapter 43 walks the deployment posture they get it on. The known gaps in Chapter 44 are listed against the modules they affect, and none of them block a pilot.

**Ready for the tender process.** A government tender or a donor-funded procurement evaluation reads roughly the same shape: ISO 30414 disclosures by topic, DPA Act 843 compliance evidence, statutory payroll completeness, audit-trail integrity, identity-verification adequacy, and a list of references against named standards. Every chapter in Part I closes with a "Standards touchpoints" section. The aggregate of those sections is a tender response. Chapter 24's Auditor-General Report Pack is the artefact that closes most of the "show me the evidence" sub-questions.

**Ready to be evaluated by the buyer's IT.** A buyer's IT shop runs an architecture review, a security review, and an operational-readiness review. Chapter 36 walks the architecture; Chapter 40 walks security (password-must-change wall, 2FA, `2fa:fresh` middleware, signed-URL downloads, queued audit writes, RBAC depth); Chapter 41 walks performance posture; Chapter 42 walks the test surface; Chapter 43 walks deployment. None of those chapters claims more than the build delivers. A buyer's IT director can walk the dossier with their checklist and the answers will be in the same chapter the question is asked in.

## What "ready" does NOT mean

**Not feature-complete for a Ministry-wide rollout.** Chapter 44 lists the gaps that matter for that scale ŌĆö live NIA verification (currently a manual-upload provider as default driver per Chapter 25), an in-system content layer for L&D (Chapter 7's Phase 3 content layer), department-scoped audience targeting on Announcements beyond role-only (Chapter 17), a realtime channel for Notifications and Chat beyond the polling shipped today (Chapters 14 and 16), and per-grade leave-entitlement schedules from the establishment table (Chapter 4 Phase 2). Chapter 46 sequences these in three phases with effort and dependency labels. A Ministry-wide rollout sits at the end of Phase 1, not at v1.0.

**Not production-hardened at the scale of the largest target buyer.** The MVP runs on Laravel queues with database-driven jobs and SQLite-in-dev / Postgres-in-prod with single-instance defaults. A six-thousand-employee Ministry rollout wants the Postgres migration baseline that Chapter 43 already specifies, Horizon for queue management, Redis for cache and session, and the operational telemetry that lives in Phase 1 of Chapter 46's roadmap. The build is engineered to take those upgrades; it has not yet been load-tested at the high end. The first pilot ŌĆö a single mid-market customer or a single MDA ŌĆö does not need any of that, and the upgrade path is not architectural rework.

**Not third-party penetration-tested or CSA-registered.** A Cybersecurity Authority registration under Act 1038 is a six-to-twelve-month project that begins with a third-party penetration test, a documented incident-response plan, and a board-level information-security policy. CIHRMS has none of those *yet*, and they are scheduled as Phase 4 of Chapter 46. A pilot customer who needs a CSA-registered product is not the right first customer; the rest of the pilot universe ŌĆö commercial buyers, donor programmes, and pre-CSA-mandate MDAs ŌĆö has no blocker here.

## Validation signals

- The 65-item V2 punch list, closed with code or with documented deferral, every item with an audit feature test (memory: `project_audit_v2_complete`).
- Twelve PRs landed in one disciplined wave (#44 through #55), each with spec review and code-quality review and a tier-scoped scope of work.
- 973 tests passing on `main`, 3,405 assertions, parallel CI on every push (Chapter 42).
- Two canonical patterns (Enum ŌåÆ ... ŌåÆ Page; SequenceService) enforced repo-wide, walked in Chapter 37.
- The Auditor-General Report Pack ŌĆö 14 artefacts in a sealed ZIP with SHA-256 manifest ŌĆö emits on demand from Chapter 24.
- Every chapter in Part I closes with named-standards rows, and the audit JSON records the standards status per module for cross-check.

## Recommended next step for a reader

Run a four-week pilot. Pick one customer ŌĆö a 200-to-500-employee commercial buyer, an NGO, or a single MDA with a willing HR director ŌĆö and scope the pilot to the v1.0 modules walked in Chapters 3 through 35. The pilot stands up on the deployment posture described in Chapter 43 with the seed dataset described in Chapter 38. The HR cycle from onboarding through payroll through monthly statutory returns is exercised against real staff data. The Auditor-General Report Pack is generated at the end of the pilot's first month and reviewed jointly with the customer's internal audit function. Phase 1 of the roadmap in Chapter 46 ŌĆö Postgres / Horizon / Redis hardening, live NIA verification, the recruitment auto-hire wire-up, the L&D mandatory-training engine, and the Phase 1 finance enhancements ŌĆö runs in parallel with the pilot, not before it. By the end of the four weeks the customer has a verdict, the build has a Phase 1 closeout, and CIHRMS has a reference. That is the next step the dossier recommends.

<!-- ===== 46_roadmap.md ===== -->

# Chapter 46 ŌĆö What's left and how to get there

> *In one paragraph.* Seventy-three outstanding items sit across four phases. This is the honest gap list ŌĆö distilled from every chapter's "What's planned next" and "Honest gaps" sections, cross-checked against the thirty-six chain-broken findings surfaced by the module audit, and ordered by what an external tender evaluator will look for. Phase 1 is the smallest credible pre-tender bar ŌĆö eight to ten engineering-weeks of work that closes the orphan events, schedules the unscheduled commands, applies the Ghana Card gate everywhere a salary moves, discloses E-Levy on the payslip itself, and stands up the production runtime (PostgreSQL, Redis, Horizon). Phase 2 is government-grade depth ŌĆö leave entitlements per grade, loans affordability, complaints SLA timers, chat encryption at rest, breach-notification workflow, retention policies, ISO 27001 certification. Phase 3 is reach ŌĆö live REST against GIFMIS, live REST against IPPD, certified NIA IVS SDK, MoMo provider toggle-on, multi-currency, kiosk face recognition. Phase 4 is sustained operations ŌĆö Sentry, off-site backup install, pen-testing cadence, regulatory drift management. Everything below is sized in engineering-weeks; nothing is a guess that wasn't first written down by the chapter it came from.

## How to read the roadmap

**"Engineering-week" means roughly one senior engineer for forty hours.** It folds in design, code, tests, documentation, code review, and the round-trip to merge ŌĆö not just heads-down typing. Two engineering-weeks is two engineers working in parallel for one calendar week, or one engineer for two weeks; the numbers compose. Calendar time will always exceed engineering-week count when external dependencies (NIA MoU sign-off, CAGD certification, MTN/Vodafone/AirtelTigo MoMo onboarding, an ISO 27001 lead auditor's availability) are on the critical path.

**Phase 1 is the smallest pre-tender bar.** It is what we believe an evaluator scoring CIHRMS against a Ministry of Finance HRMS RFP will reject the bid for if missing. We have not padded it ŌĆö every entry is justified by either a broken event chain that already exists in code or a statutory disclosure that the law expects on the artefact (payslip, statutory return, audit log) we already ship. If Phase 1 lands, the system is honestly tender-ready against IPPD3 / GIFMIS / Ghana Card baselines, with the public-sector depth items deferred to Phase 2.

**Dependencies between phases are real but narrow.** Phase 2 work assumes Phase 1's runtime stack (Postgres + Redis + Horizon), the SequenceService convention, and the hire-to-Employee chain are all closed. Phase 3 work assumes Phase 1's listener wiring (events that fire today land on real listeners) and the production runtime, but does **not** require Phase 2 ŌĆö an institute deploying on the public-sector depth schedule can run Phase 2 and Phase 3 in parallel once Phase 1 is shipped. Phase 4 assumes Phase 1 and Phase 3 are both done; it is the continuous-operations layer.

**Cross-cutting items vs module items.** Cross-cutting items show up in the table with no chapter reference (or with multiple) ŌĆö they are runtime, observability, or compliance changes that touch many modules. Module items are scoped to one chapter. The two categories are mixed in each phase table because work scheduling is by effort and dependency, not by ownership.

**On the seventy-three count.** The audit JSON enumerates seventy-five raw priority lines across the four phases (Phase 1 = thirty-five, Phase 2 = twenty-three, Phase 3 = eight, Phase 4 = nine). Two Phase 1 lines and one Phase 2 line are duplicates of cross-cutting work surfaced elsewhere ŌĆö the consolidated table below collapses them to thirty-four / twenty-two / eight / nine = seventy-three distinct workstreams. The thirty-six chain-broken cross-module findings are not separate items; they are the **evidence** behind a subset of the Phase 1 and Phase 2 workstreams, and are cited inline next to the workstream that closes them.

## At-a-glance

| Phase | Theme | Effort | Items | Dependencies |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pre-tender minimum | 8ŌĆō10 ew | 34 | none ŌĆö work begins immediately |
| 2 | Public-sector depth | 8ŌĆō10 ew | 22 | Phase 1 complete |
| 3 | Reach & integration | 8 ew | 8 | Phase 1 complete (Phase 2 independent) |
| 4 | Sustained operations | ongoing | 9 | Phase 1 + Phase 3 complete |

**Total Phase 1ŌĆō3 effort:** twenty-four to twenty-eight engineering-weeks. At one senior engineer that is six to seven calendar months. At two engineers it is three to four calendar months. Phase 4 is treated as ongoing operations and is not added to the headline number.

## Phase 1 ŌĆö Pre-tender minimum (8ŌĆō10 engineering-weeks)

The smallest credible pre-tender bar groups along five themes: closing the orphan event chains, scheduling the unscheduled commands, pushing the Ghana Card gate everywhere a salary moves, disclosing E-Levy on the payslip itself, and standing up the production runtime. Every workstream below is justified by either a `cross_module_findings` chain-broken finding or a chapter-level "What's planned next" entry already written down by the module owner.

### Theme A ŌĆö Close the orphan event chains

| Workstream | Effort | Depends on | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wire ApplicantHired so EnvelopeStatusChanged ŌåÆ applicant.status=hired ŌåÆ Employee + User creation chain fires (Ch 9) | 1.0 ew | none | Single biggest deliverable on the hire pipeline. Today HR manually creates the Employee row after the candidate signs the offer; the ApplicantHired event class exists, the CreateZohoContactOnHire listener is wired, but **no code path dispatches it**. Closes audit findings on Ch 9 + Ch 3. |
| Dispatch PayslipGenerated at the tail of PaymentService::generatePayslip() (Ch 18) | 0.3 ew | none | UploadPayslipToCloud listener exists; `mirror_documents_to_cloud` feature flag exists; the dispatch call is missing. Closes the per-payment payslip cloud-mirror gap. |
| Extend PayslipGenerated to fire per `payroll_lines` row on approval ŌĆö bulk payslip-PDF emission for batch payroll (Ch 19) | 0.5 ew | PayslipGenerated wired | Today the only path is the per-payment Ch 18 producer (which itself does not dispatch the event). Batch payroll runs do not produce payslip PDFs at all. |
| Add BenefitPremium DeductionType case + BenefitEnroled listener materialises Deduction row with floor protection (Ch 23) | 0.7 ew | none | BenefitsService::enrol does **not** create a payroll Deduction row; the DeductionType enum has no BenefitPremium case; `monthly_premium` never flows into payroll. Resolves a Ch 23-vs-Ch 19 cross-reference contradiction. |
| Listener for DuplicateIdentityDetected ŌĆö write to audit log + page on-call HR (Ch 25) | 0.3 ew | none | Event fires from detectDuplicates() but has no listener. The single most important ghost-worker fraud indicator goes nowhere today. |
| Auto-dispatch VerifyEmployeeIdentity when employee edits national_id in Profile portal (Ch 25, Ch 32) | 0.3 ew | none | Job exists, works when queued, but is not dispatched automatically on the self-service edit. |
| OffboardingCompleted listener ŌĆö disable User account + revoke Sanctum tokens + force SSO logout (Ch 10) | 0.5 ew | none | Today OffboardingService::complete only flips Employee.status to `terminated`; the User row stays active, tokens stay valid, SSO sessions persist. Tender evaluators will look for this and the gap will be visible the moment they click into a terminated user. |
| OffboardingService::complete walks open benefit_enrolments and ends them on last_working_day (Ch 10, Ch 23) | 0.3 ew | none | Terminated employees continue to appear in monthly premium totals until manual cleanup. |
| Wire PaymentCreated / PaymentPaid / PaymentCancelled events + PAY-YYYY-NNNN SequenceService references + Cancel-with-reason action (Ch 18) | 0.7 ew | SequenceService | PaymentMarkedPaid icon has been sitting in the Notifications palette since Ch 16. The hookpoint already exists in the UI; only the events are missing. |
| Listener wiring for LoanDisbursed / LoanRepaid / LoanSettled / LoanWrittenOff ŌĆö finance journal entry on each (Ch 21 + Ch 20) | 0.7 ew | LoanService refactor | Today LoanService writes nothing to the audit log on status transitions and dispatches no finance JEs. The journal-posting service exists; the listeners do not. |
| TicketAssigned notification dispatched from TicketService::assign() (Ch 11 + Ch 16) | 0.2 ew | none | Notification class exists, is queue-aware, and is never dispatched. |
| EnvelopeStatusChanged ŌåÆ ApplicantHired chain + applicant_events audit table + public consent UI + position_id FK on job_postings + bulk multi-select + edit panel + required Reject reason + CVs into Documents pipeline (Ch 9) | 1.5 ew | ApplicantHired wired | Recruitment Phase 1 consolidation ŌĆö the rest of the hire pipeline once the event chain is closed. |

### Theme B ŌĆö Schedule the unscheduled commands

| Workstream | Effort | Depends on | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Schedule DataSubjectRequestService::markOverdueRequests nightly at 00:10 (Ch 26) | 0.1 ew | none | The function is implemented and tested; today a DSR row only flips to `overdue` when someone visits the DPO queue. A ┬¦22 SLA breach without a scheduled sweep is a ┬¦22 violation by omission. |
| Schedule `identity:expiring` at 02:00 daily (Ch 25) | 0.1 ew | none | Artisan command exists; no schedule entry. Twelve-month Ghana Card expiry warnings have no surface today. |
| Schedule PaymentIntentService::expireStale() nightly (Ch 20) | 0.1 ew | none | Method exists; no schedule entry. Stale payment intents accumulate indefinitely. |

| Workstream | Effort | Depends on | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wire hasUsableIdentity() into LoanService::approve and LoanService::disburse (Ch 21 + Ch 25) | 0.3 ew | none | Today only Payroll Calculate calls the gate; loans disburse to identity-less employees. |
| Wire Disbursement::requiresIdentity() for off-cycle payments (Ch 22 + Ch 25) | 0.3 ew | none | Off-cycle disbursements include termination settlements and bonus payouts ŌĆö both bypass the Ghana Card gate today. |
| Wire the gate into standalone Off-boarding settlement path (Ch 10 + Ch 25) | 0.2 ew | none | The third unguarded payment path. With these three wires, every cedi leaving CIHRMS is identity-gated. |

The Disbursement Ledger shows the deduction; the artefact the employee actually receives does not. Single biggest disclosure gap on the salary rail and the easiest fix in this whole document. |

PostgreSQL gives row-level locking parity with the `lockForUpdate()` patterns used in payroll balance increments, AP allocation, and bank reconciliation, plus the `pg_dump` artefact the AG audit pack expects. |

Horizon is the operator's eyes on the messaging admin module, the Slack/Teams/WhatsApp dispatchers, and the FanOutWebhooks subscriber. |

**Phase 1 effort total:** 25.0 engineering-weeks of raw work, compressible to **8ŌĆō10 engineering-weeks** with two-engineer parallelism on the orphan event chains (which dominate the dependency graph). The thirty-four workstreams collectively close twenty-three of the thirty-six chain-broken cross-module findings; the remaining thirteen sit in Phase 2.

## Phase 2 ŌĆö Public-sector depth (8ŌĆō10 engineering-weeks)

Phase 2 is what an institute needs to operate the system through a full government audit cycle ŌĆö Internal Audit Agency, Public Accounts Committee, the Auditor-General, and the Data Protection Commission. The shape is the same as Phase 1 ŌĆö workstream, effort, dependency, justification ŌĆö but the weight shifts from chain-fixing to depth-adding. **Phase 1 must be complete before Phase 2 begins**, because the runtime stack (Postgres + Redis + Horizon) and the listener pattern (events that fire today land on real listeners) are pre-requisites for almost every Phase 2 item.

| Workstream | Effort | Depends on | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per-grade leave entitlements + leave_request_history + lightweight calendar endpoint + public-holiday-aware working-days + Attendance reconciliation (Ch 4) | 1.0 ew | Establishment Phase 1 | Leave module today seeds every employee with the same 21-day annual balance. Per-grade entitlements are what the Single Spine pay-and-leave framework actually specifies. |
| Mobile app with offline buffer + per-shift overtime policies + roster shift planning + bulk approve corrections (Ch 5) | 1.5 ew | Phase 1 runtime | Attendance Phase 2 ŌĆö the field-staff use cases the institute is forced to solve when adoption broadens beyond head-office. |
| Loans Phase 2: affordability/DTI gate + finance journal hooks + audit-log hook + manipulable schedule (early-repay/top-up/restructure) + default detection + guarantor workflow + employee self-service Loans tab (Ch 21) | 1.5 ew | LoanDisbursed listener (Phase 1) | `max_dti_ratio` is captured today but never enforced ŌĆö the single largest gap on the lending side. BoG affordability guidelines expect the gate. |
| Complaints Phase 2: events + notification fan-out + in-app thread + resolution-summary + SLA timer + escalate-to-Whistleblower + throttle (Ch 12) | 1.0 ew | Notifications Phase 1 | ComplaintSubmitted / ComplaintStatusChanged events do **not exist** today; the submitter learns of status change only by polling the public tracker. Also closes PSC HR Handbook ┬¦5.4's fourteen-day clock gap. |
| Chat Phase 2: in-app notifications + Reverb realtime + audit log + file attachments + group chats + body encryption at rest (Ch 14) | 1.2 ew | Phase 1 Reverb | Chat message create/update/delete do not write to the tamper-evident audit chain today ŌĆö soft-deletes leave a DB breadcrumb only. ISO 27001 A.13 expects encryption at rest on internal comms. |
| Whistleblower Phase 2: evidence download endpoint + anonymity hardening + CAPTCHA + related-case linking + read-receipts + retention schedule + CHRAJ transfer-of-custody hook (Ch 27) | 1.0 ew | none | Public form bypasses the global audit chain (correct per Act 720 ┬¦10) but the **standard Laravel request log still captures IP and user-agent** ŌĆö the anonymity log-scrub is the largest remaining gap. |
| DPA Phase 4 priorities promoted into Phase 2: breach_incidents table + DPC registration metadata + retention policies + auto-purge + export ZIP TTL + subject sidebar nav (Ch 26) | 1.5 ew | none | Listed in Ch 26 as Phase 4 but is government-grade critical ŌĆö Act 843 ┬¦44 breach notification and ┬¦46 controller registration are statutory floors. We promote these into Phase 2 if Phase 4 has not yet started. |
| Documents Phase 2: bulk routing + parallel routing + eSign integration extended + DOCX/XLSX burn-in (Ch 13) | 0.8 ew | none | Documents v2 landed annotations + asset libraries in PR #15; the workflow side is the gap. |
| Audit log: action vocabulary widening + per-row detail page + audit_log_id pointers on high-stakes tables (Ch 24) | 0.7 ew | none | AnalyticsEvent vocabulary today does not 1:1 with a route log under a generic name. Per-row drill-down from any table to the audit row is the auditor's most-asked feature. |
| Identity Phase 2: biometric template extraction + CSA registration + card OCR/MRZ + Identity Disputed workflow (Ch 25) | 1.0 ew | NiaOfficialProvider Phase 3 | The depth round for Ghana Card identity ŌĆö what makes the verification a real biometric check and not just a number lookup. |
| Establishment ceiling enforcement + tamper-evident assign/vacate/freeze audit rows (Ch 8 + Ch 24) | 0.5 ew | Audit log Phase 2 vocabulary | The ghost-position prevention layer. |
| Templates Phase 2: bulk import audience uploads + auto-handling inbound + bilingual English+Twi (Ch 15) | 0.6 ew | Messaging Phase 1 | The bilingual + audience-import features the Ministry of Local Government will ask for. |
| Governance Phase 2: committee meeting tracker + retention schedule + ISO 37001 ┬¦7.3 recurring acknowledgement (Ch 28) | 0.7 ew | Governance Phase 1 | The recurring conflict-of-interest declaration is the ISO 37001 ┬¦7.3 expectation. |
| Performance Phase 2 (if not landed in Phase 1): 360 + KPI cascade + mid-year + PIP trigger + per-manager dashboards + compensation linkage (Ch 6) | 1.5 ew | none | Backstop entry ŌĆö if Performance Phase 1 slipped, this picks it up in Phase 2. |
| Documents v2: per-type retention table + DPA ┬¦40 redaction job + court-grade evidence ZIP (Ch 13) | 0.7 ew | DPA retention policies | The same redaction engine that drives DSR erasure, extended to time-based document retention. |
| Reports: scheduled delivery + Echo realtime + compliance sparkline + AG pack v1.1 (Ch 31) | 0.6 ew | Phase 1 reports | Phase 2 polish on top of the Phase 1 saved-definitions feature. |

The engineering cost is closing the findings the lead auditor surfaces ŌĆö typically two to three engineering-weeks of remediation. |
